name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads hero service entry content, featured case studies and articles, and the user’s saved-content summary, and routes users to deeper pages such as services, project estimator, case studies, and blog articles.

core_features: Load homepage hero content, including heading, subheading, and primary/secondary calls-to-action, using getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks and route users to the appropriate pages based on target_page_key values.

Expose core service entry points (cards or links) from getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks.core_services, each linking to the appropriate service, work, blog, estimator, or contact page using the provided target_page_key.
Display a featured case studies section using getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies(limit), allowing users to navigate to individual case study detail pages via slug URL parameters.
Display a featured articles section using getHomeFeaturedArticles(limit), allowing users to navigate to individual article detail pages via slug URL parameters and to the blog overview page.

Show a header-level summary of the user’s shortlist and reading list using getUserSavedContentSummary, including counts and a small set of recent items, and provide navigation into the full shortlist and reading list views.

Coordinate all data loading on page initialization (and when URL parameters change), passing URL-based configuration (such as limits and default section focus) into getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies and getHomeFeaturedArticles.
user_workflows: Discover services from homepage hero: 1) User loads index.html (Home). 2) Page controller calls getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks with no parameters. 3) Hero component renders heading, subheading, and two CTAs using the returned data. 4) User clicks the primary hero CTA. 5) The click handler inspects the primary_cta_label’s associated target_page_key from getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks and navigates to the appropriate page (e.g., services.html) without hardcoding the label. 6) URL changes to the resolved target path (e.g., services.html) with any required query parameters.

Access project estimator from hero: 1) User loads Home and sees secondary hero CTA based on getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks. 2) User clicks the secondary CTA. 3) The click handler reads the associated target_page_key and navigates to the estimator page (e.g., project_estimator.html) using URL navigation, such as project_estimator.html. 4) The browser loads the estimator with no additional parameters or with parameters defined by the routing rules associated with the target_page_key.

Explore services via core service entry points: 1) After getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks resolves, the hero/services component lists core_services. 2) User clicks a core service item. 3) The service click handler uses the core_services[n].target_page_key to resolve the destination path (e.g., a specific services overview page, case studies, blog, project_estimator, or contact). 4) The browser navigates to the resolved URL with any required query parameters (for example, services.html?serviceKey=<core_services[n].key> if the router uses that scheme).

Browse featured case studies: 1) On page load or when URL parameters change, the page controller determines the case studies limit (from a URL parameter such as homeCaseStudiesLimit, or a system default if no parameter is present). 2) The controller calls getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies(limit) with that limit. 3) The featured case studies component renders cards based on the returned CaseStudy objects. 4) User clicks a case study card title. 5) The click handler reads the caseStudy.slug from the selected item and navigates to case_study.html?slug=<caseStudy.slug>. 6) Case study detail page uses the slug to load the appropriate content.

Browse featured articles from Home: 1) On page load or URL change, the controller determines the articles limit (for example from a URL parameter such as homeArticlesLimit or a default). 2) The controller calls getHomeFeaturedArticles(limit). 3) The featured articles component lists articles with titles, summaries (if provided), tags, and estimated read time taken from the Article records. 4) User clicks a featured article title. 5) The click handler reads article.slug and navigates to article.html?slug=<article.slug>. 6) Alternatively, user clicks a “view all insights” style link whose target is resolved via routing (e.g., blog.html); the click handler navigates to that blog listing URL using standard navigation.

Use shortlist and reading list from the header: 1) On page load, the page controller calls getUserSavedContentSummary. 2) The header saved-content component displays counts for shortlist.count and reading_list.count and a small preview (latest_items) if available. 3) User clicks the shortlist icon or label. 4) The click handler navigates to the shortlist page URL (such as shortlist.html) without modifying any saved data. 5) User clicks the reading list icon or label. 6) The click handler navigates to the reading list page URL (such as reading_list.html). 7) If the user clicks a specific item within the header preview, the handler navigates directly to either a case_study.html?slug=… or article.html?slug=… URL, using the item’s associated identifiers resolved via existing routing logic.

Deep-link and section-focus behavior via URL parameters: 1) A user or external link opens index.html with query parameters, such as index.html?homeSection=articles&homeCaseStudiesLimit=<value>. 2) On page load, the controller reads homeSection and limit parameters from location.search. 3) The controller passes the parsed limit values into getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies and getHomeFeaturedArticles instead of using a fixed limit. 4) The value of homeSection controls which section is emphasized (for example, automatically scrolling to or highlighting the articles section) without changing the underlying data from the interfaces. 5) If the query string changes (e.g., user updates filters or navigation triggers a new state within Home without leaving the page), the controller re-reads the parameters and refreshes the relevant sections.
interactions: onPrimaryHeroCtaClick: Triggered when the user activates the primary CTA in the hero area. Uses the associated target_page_key from getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks to navigate to the resolved page (e.g., services.html) using URL navigation.

onSecondaryHeroCtaClick: Triggered when the user activates the secondary CTA in the hero area. Uses the associated target_page_key from getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks to navigate (for example, to project_estimator.html) using URL navigation.

onServiceCardClick: Triggered when the user clicks on a core service entry item in the hero/services area. Reads the clicked service object’s key and target_page_key from core_services, resolves the destination path, and navigates to that URL (optionally appending query parameters such as ?serviceKey=<key> if routing uses such parameters).

onCaseStudyCardClick: Triggered when a user clicks on a featured case study title or card. Reads the corresponding CaseStudy.slug from the getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies(limit) response and navigates to case_study.html?slug=<CaseStudy.slug>.

onArticleTitleClick: Triggered when a user clicks on a featured article title. Reads the Article.slug from the getHomeFeaturedArticles(limit) response and navigates to article.html?slug=<Article.slug>.

onViewAllInsightsClick: Triggered when a user clicks on the link to view all articles/insights from the featured articles section. Uses routing logic to navigate to the blog listing page (e.g., blog.html) without additional parameters, or with parameters if defined by the router.

onShortlistIconClick: Triggered when the user clicks the shortlist icon or label in the header saved-content summary. Navigates to the full shortlist page (e.g., shortlist.html) using URL navigation; no data mutation occurs on Home.

onReadingListIconClick: Triggered when the user clicks the reading list icon or label in the header saved-content summary. Navigates to the full reading list page (e.g., reading_list.html) using URL navigation.

onSavedShortlistItemClick: Triggered when a user selects a specific case study from the shortlist preview dropdown in the header. The handler resolves the CaseStudy.slug associated with that saved item (via existing routing rules for the stored case_study_id) and navigates to case_study.html?slug=<slug>.

onSavedReadingListItemClick: Triggered when a user selects a specific article from the reading list preview dropdown in the header. The handler resolves the Article.slug associated with that saved item (via existing routing rules for the stored article_id) and navigates to article.html?slug=<slug>.

onPageLoad: Triggered when index.html initializes. Reads relevant URL parameters (such as homeSection, homeCaseStudiesLimit, homeArticlesLimit) from location.search, then issues calls to getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks, getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies(limit derived from URL or default), getHomeFeaturedArticles(limit derived from URL or default), and getUserSavedContentSummary. Passes the resolved data to child components.

onUrlParamChange: Triggered when the URL query string changes while staying on index.html (e.g., through client-side navigation or history API). Re-reads the query parameters and adjusts section focus and limits, then re-calls getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies and/or getHomeFeaturedArticles with the updated limit values.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not require any server-side URL parameters for basic rendering, but it uses client-side URL query parameters to manage view configuration and navigation. Example parameters: (1) homeSection: Optional. Controls which major section (e.g., services, case-studies, articles) is programmatically focused or scrolled into view after load. When homeSection is present, the page controller highlights or scrolls to the corresponding component without modifying what the interfaces return. (2) homeCaseStudiesLimit: Optional. Parsed as a number from the query string. When present and valid, this value is passed as the limit argument to getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies(limit) instead of any default. When absent or invalid, the controller uses a system-defined default limit without hardcoding it within this page specification. (3) homeArticlesLimit: Optional. Parsed as a number from the query string and used to call getHomeFeaturedArticles(limit). When absent or invalid, a system default is used. (4) Additional configuration parameters can be supported by the controller (such as homeScrollTo for scrolling to a specific section anchor) as long as they do not change which interfaces are used or the data structures they return. All persistent application state that affects navigation to case_study.html and article.html is represented via URL parameters (slug for detail pages). The Home page itself does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence; it reads data exclusively through the assigned interfaces and URL parameters.

components.functionality: Orchestrates data loading and state management for the Home page. On initialization and whenever URL parameters change, it reads query parameters related to section focus and list limits (e.g., homeSection, homeCaseStudiesLimit, homeArticlesLimit). It then calls getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks, getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies(limit), getHomeFeaturedArticles(limit), and getUserSavedContentSummary. The controller passes the returned data, along with interpreted URL state, as props or inputs to the hero/services, featured case studies, featured articles, and header saved-content components. It also exposes handlers for navigation events (e.g., CTA clicks) that translate interface data into URL navigations.

Displays the homepage’s primary introductory content and core service entry points using data from getHomeHeroAndServiceLinks. Renders the hero heading and subheading, plus primary and secondary CTAs that navigate to key pages. Below or alongside the hero area, lists core_services (each with name, short_description, and primary_cta_label) and any primary_nav_ctas as quick links to important pages (such as project estimator or contact). All labels and destinations come from the interface response and routing logic, not hardcoded strings.

Renders a grid or list of featured case studies returned by getHomeFeaturedCaseStudies(limit), using the limit derived from URL parameters or defaults. Each case study card displays key fields such as title, summary, industry, and selected metrics, all taken from the CaseStudy objects. Clicking on a case study title or card triggers navigation to case_study.html with the appropriate slug query parameter.

Displays a list of featured blog/insights articles based on getHomeFeaturedArticles(limit), using the limit derived from URL parameters or defaults. Each article item shows data such as title, summary (if present), tags, category, and estimated_read_time_minutes from the Article records. Clicking an article title navigates to the article detail page using the slug query parameter. A separate control (e.g., a link) triggers navigation to the full blog listing page.

Integrates into the persistent site header to show dynamic counts and recent items for the user’s shortlist and reading list. Uses getUserSavedContentSummary to display shortlist.count and reading_list.count, and exposes a compact list of latest_items for each. Clicking the icons or labels navigates to the full shortlist or reading list pages. Clicking a specific preview item navigates directly to its associated case study or article detail page via the appropriate slug in the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly linear, top-to-bottom scroll with a dominant full-height hero at the top, followed by clearly separated content bands: hero & service entry, featured case studies, and featured articles. A persistent top navigation bar (logo left, primary actions right) sits at the top of the hero and remains consistent as the user scrolls. Each major section sits inside a centered max-width content band (12-column grid), preserving the strong left/right split from the mockup wherever possible.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly linear, top-to-bottom scroll with a dominant full-height hero at the top, followed by clearly separated content bands: hero & service entry, featured case studies, and featured articles. A persistent top navigation bar (logo left, primary actions right) sits at the top of the hero and remains consistent as the user scrolls. Each major section sits inside a centered max-width content band (12-column grid), preserving the strong left/right split from the mockup wherever possible.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 90–100% of the initial viewport height, with a slim (10–15% height) header band at the top and the remaining height dedicated to the hero body. Horizontally, sections use a 12-column grid with a 7/12 left and 5/12 right split for hero content. Subsequent sections use similar splits (e.g., 4/12 info column + 8/12 content grid for case studies, 5/12 intro + 7/12 list for articles). Section bands are full-width on the black background but actual content is constrained to ~1120–1280px and centered. Vertical gaps between major sections are generous (~80–120px) to maintain the premium, spacious feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is intentionally low-density with oversized typography, minimal copy, and large areas of empty black space. Supporting sections below become progressively denser but still airy: case studies use a grid of 3–6 cards with clear separation and ample padding; articles are presented in a tidy list with comfortable line spacing and whitespace. All components adhere to the 8px spacing system, using 24–64px increments between stacked elements and 32–64px padding inside cards and interactive areas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top-left logo, then sweeps to the top-right where the primary CTA, saved-content summary, and circular menu icon sit. From there, the eye drops diagonally down to the massive left-aligned hero headline, then moves right to the supporting hero description and trust badge. As the user scrolls, each new section repeats a left-right reading pattern: left side provides section labels, context, or filters; right side presents the main consumable content (case study cards, article list). This reinforces a modified Z-pattern across the page: header → hero headline → hero side content → next-section left label → next-section main content, and so on.

layout.overall_description: The homepage sits on a pure black canvas with a centered, max-width content band that runs down the middle of the screen. At the top, a slim, sticky navigation bar spans the full width: a neon-accented logo anchors the left, while a compact cluster of primary actions (saved-content summary, CTA button, circular menu icon) sits on the right, aligned to the same max-width container. Below this, a cinematic hero fills almost the entire viewport. On the left, an oversized two-line headline dominates about two-thirds of the width, with generous breathing room above and below. On the right, a narrower column holds a multi-line description and a trust/award badge, stacked vertically with deliberate gaps. Along the lower edge of the hero, minimal service entry links sit aligned with the left column, acting as a subtle gateway into the agency’s offerings without cluttering the composition. As the user scrolls, the page transitions into a featured case study section where a concise left-hand column introduces the section while a denser right-hand grid of case study cards showcases work. Further down, a featured articles band mirrors this split: contextual text and taxonomy on the left, a vertical list of articles on the right. Throughout, the layout leverages large fields of negative space, bold type, and neon accents to guide the eye along a consistent left-to-right, top-to-bottom rhythm that feels modern, controlled, and premium.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-layout-controller acts as the full-page frame. It sets a pure black background that spans the entire viewport width and height and establishes a centered max-width content band (approximately 1120–1280px wide) aligned in the horizontal center of the page. All page sections (hero & services, featured case studies, featured articles) are stacked vertically within this band, separated by generous vertical margins (~80–120px). A 12-column grid runs through the band, with consistent left and right padding (48–64px from the viewport edges on large screens) to match the spacious hero from the mockup. The global header (containing the saved-content summary) is anchored at the top of this controller and remains aligned to the same max-width container as the user scrolls, ensuring all content feels locked into a single, coherent skeleton.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-page vertical scroll container with a fixed black background. The internal 12-column grid and max-width band remain centered at all viewport sizes. The top navigation region is sticky within this controller, remaining pinned to the top of the viewport while other sections scroll underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-hero-and-services component occupies the top section of the page, directly under the sticky header, and fills approximately one full viewport height on desktop. Within the max-width content band, it uses two primary horizontal zones: a top navigation row and a hero body. The top navigation row matches the mockup: a 10–15% height band where the logo is anchored to the far left of the grid, and the right side hosts the CTA, saved-content summary, and circular menu icon, all right-aligned with 16–24px spacing between them. Beneath this, the hero body uses a 7/12 left and 5/12 right split. The left 7 columns are dedicated to a massive, two-line H1 headline, vertically centered within the available space between header and fold. The headline text runs nearly the full width of this left block, leaving comfortable margins on all sides so it can breathe against the black background. The right 5 columns, aligned to the same top baseline as the headline, contain a multi-line descriptive paragraph at the top and a trust/award badge below it, separated by 24–32px of vertical space. The paragraph spans roughly 70–80% of the right column width for optimal readability, with generous line-height. The badge is horizontally centered within the right block or slightly aligned toward the left edge of that block to tie it visually to the paragraph above. At the bottom of the hero, aligned with the left column and sitting 40–64px above the bottom of the viewport, a low-profile service entry strip appears: a row of 3–5 service links or pill-style buttons that spread horizontally across the lower portion of the hero. These are smaller, understated elements using neon accent strokes or text, ensuring they support rather than compete with the main hero message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The hero section occupies at least 90–100% of the initial viewport height on desktop, effectively functioning as a full-screen hero. It is responsive: on smaller screens, the two columns stack so that the headline appears first and the descriptive column (text + badge) moves below with consistent padding. The top navigation row remains part of the sticky header; the hero content scrolls under it once the user moves past the initial viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-featured-case-studies component sits immediately below the hero, separated by a large vertical gap (~96px) that allows the hero to feel self-contained. Within the max-width content band, this section uses a left-right composition that echoes the hero’s split while adapting to a grid. A left informational column occupies roughly 4/12 of the width: at the top of this column, a section label (e.g., “Featured Case Studies”) appears in bold, slightly oversized type, followed by a short supporting sentence or two explaining the selection; below that, optional filter tags or a small link (e.g., “View all case studies”) are aligned left, all stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing. The right content area occupies the remaining 8/12 columns and presents the case studies in a 2-column grid of cards. Each card is roughly equal in width, with 24–32px gaps between columns and rows, and vertical card count (e.g., 2–3 rows visible) depends on the content but should not overwhelm the screen—approximately 4–6 cards on initial load. Cards have generous padding (24–32px inside), a subtle neon border or accent line, and titles that are clearly legible against the black background. The top edge of the right grid aligns horizontally with the section title on the left, maintaining a clean, shared baseline. Ample black space remains around and beneath this section so it feels like a distinct band of content rather than a crowded gallery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-width horizontal band within the page flow, expanding vertically as needed to accommodate the grid. On medium breakpoints, the left intro and right grid still maintain a side-by-side layout but with narrower columns; on small screens, the left intro column stacks above the grid, both centered with consistent padding and preserved vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-featured-articles component appears below the case studies, separated by another generous vertical gap (~80–96px) to maintain the rhythm of distinct sections. It again uses a two-column layout within the centered max-width band, but with a slightly different proportion to suit list content. The left column occupies about 5/12 of the width and acts as a contextual spine: at the top, a section heading such as “Insights & Articles” is set in bold type, followed by a brief description of the content and, optionally, a small set of filters or category chips. This column may also include a subtle secondary CTA (e.g., “Visit the blog”) aligned left under the description. The right column takes roughly 7/12 of the width and presents a vertical list of article items. Each article row spans nearly the full width of this right column, with 24–32px vertical gaps between items. Article rows are fairly minimal: bold article title, a short one-line summary, and a small meta line (date, read time, or category), all left-aligned. The right column’s content starts at the same vertical baseline as the title in the left column, and the list is allowed to extend downward, with black space preserved at the bottom to avoid the layout feeling crowded. The density here is higher than the hero but still airy, with ample line-height and breathing room between items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Functions as a scrollable content band in the normal document flow. Height grows with the number of featured articles. On smaller screens, the left contextual column stacks above the right article list, maintaining central alignment and consistent side padding, so the user reads the section label and context before scrolling into the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-saved-content-summary component lives inside the global top navigation row, aligned to the right side and visually grouped with the main CTA and menu icon. Within the header’s right cluster, it appears as a compact pill or icon+label chip positioned between the main CTA button and the circular menu icon, or slightly to the left of both if more hierarchy is needed. It occupies a modest horizontal footprint—roughly the width of a small button—with 8–16px internal horizontal padding and 4–8px vertical padding, matching the spacing system for small interactive elements. The chip may display an icon (e.g., bookmark or heart) followed by a concise label and, optionally, a small count of saved items. It is vertically centered within the header bar and aligned to the right edge of the max-width content band, keeping its baseline consistent with the logo on the left and the CTA/menu elements around it. Negative space is preserved above and below it within the header band, reinforcing the header’s light, uncluttered feel despite multiple actions being present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains fixed in position within the sticky top navigation bar as the user scrolls, always visible at the top-right cluster. It does not independently float or move; its spatial behavior is tied to the header, which stays pinned to the top of the viewport. On smaller screens, it can collapse to an icon-only pill or move into a secondary row beneath the main header actions while still aligning to the right side.

name: Services Overview
title: Services
description: Page that fetches and displays the list of service categories and guides users to the appropriate service detail pages.

core_features: Fetch services overview content via the getServicesOverviewContent interface when the page loads.
Display an introductory heading and copy describing the overall services offering, using data from getServicesOverviewContent.
Render a list of service categories returned by getServicesOverviewContent, showing each category’s name, short description, and when-to-choose guidance.
Provide clickable service cards and primary call-to-action (CTA) buttons for each service category that navigate to the appropriate service detail page using the category’s target_page_key.
Support URL-based state to highlight or preselect a specific service category (e.g., from another page) and to preserve the user’s source context when navigating to a service detail page.
user_workflows:
Browse all services and navigate to a detail page:
1. User visits services.html.
2. On initial load, the page calls getServicesOverviewContent with no parameters.
3. The page displays the intro heading and intro copy from the response.
4. The page renders one card for each entry in service_categories, showing the name, short_description, and when_to_choose fields.
5. The user scans the when_to_choose guidance to decide which service is relevant.
6. The user clicks either the full service card area or the primary CTA on that card.
7. The click handler reads the selected item’s target_page_key and key and constructs a navigation URL to the corresponding service detail page, optionally appending URL parameters such as service_key={selected.key} and from=services_overview.
8. The browser navigates to the service detail page URL.

Arrive with a specific service highlighted (deep link):
1. User follows a link from another page (e.g., homepage) to services.html with a query parameter highlight_service={some_service_key}.
2. On load, the page parses the URL, extracting highlight_service.
3. The page calls getServicesOverviewContent.
4. After the data is loaded, the page searches service_categories for an item with key matching the highlight_service value.
5. If a match is found, the corresponding service card is marked as highlighted in state and the page scrolls it into view.
6. The user can then click the highlighted card or its CTA to navigate to the detail page.

Return from a detail page with context:
1. User is on a service detail page and uses a local navigation link back to services.html, including a URL parameter such as last_viewed_service={service_key}.
2. The user lands on services.html; the page parses last_viewed_service.
3. The page calls getServicesOverviewContent.
4. After loading, the page finds the matching category by key and sets a state flag such as isLastViewed=true for that card.
5. The card can be visually indicated as last viewed (functional state) and remains in the same scroll position if combined with scroll restoration.
6. The user may choose a different service or revisit the same one by clicking the appropriate card or CTA.
interactions:
Initial data load:
- Trigger: Page load (services.html) or URL change.
- Action: Call getServicesOverviewContent.
- Result: Store the returned intro_heading, intro_copy, and service_categories in page state; render components bound to this state.

Click on a service card:
- Trigger: User clicks on the body of a service card.
- Action: Determine which service was clicked based on its key; read its target_page_key and current URL parameters (e.g., existing source info) and construct a navigation URL to the corresponding service detail page.
- Result: Browser navigates to the appropriate detail page with URL parameters such as service_key and from=services_overview.

Click on a service primary CTA button:
- Trigger: User clicks the primary CTA button inside a service card.
- Action: Use the associated service item’s target_page_key to construct the destination URL, optionally adding a cta_label parameter using primary_cta_label and a source parameter indicating that the user came from the services overview.
- Result: Browser navigates to the target service detail page; the detail page can read the URL parameters to pre-focus on quoting, maintenance plans, research inquiries, or branding packages relevant to that service.

URL parameter-based highlighting:
- Trigger: Page load or URL change with query parameters such as highlight_service or last_viewed_service.
- Action: After getServicesOverviewContent returns, compare these parameter values against each service_categories[i].key.
- Result: Update state for matching service cards (e.g., isHighlighted or isLastViewed) so that the card can be functionally distinguished (e.g., scrolled into view, focused for accessibility).

Keyboard navigation and focus handling:
- Trigger: User navigates between service cards using keyboard (e.g., tab/shift+tab).
- Action: Move focus between actionable elements (card wrapper, CTA button). Optionally, when a highlighted_service is present in the URL and found in the data, programmatically focus that card’s main action after content load.
- Result: User can activate any card or CTA via keyboard; activation triggers the same navigation logic as mouse clicks.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Services Overview page uses URL query parameters to manage UI state and navigation context without localStorage. Example parameters and behaviors:
- highlight_service: When present, its value is treated as a candidate service key. After getServicesOverviewContent resolves, the page searches service_categories for an item with key equal to highlight_service. If found, state.highlightedServiceKey is set to that key, and the corresponding card is scrolled into view or given initial focus.
- last_viewed_service: When present, its value is matched against service_categories[i].key after data load. If a match is found, state.lastViewedServiceKey is set, and the card can be functionally marked as last viewed (e.g., by adding an attribute or text indicator via the component logic).
- from: A generic context parameter indicating the source page (e.g., from=home or from=services_overview). This parameter is read on load and stored in state.navigationSource. When the user navigates from Services Overview to a service detail page, this parameter can be forwarded or overridden (from=services_overview), enabling downstream pages to adjust their behavior.
- view_mode: Optional parameter controlling list behavior, such as view_mode=list or view_mode=compact. On load, the page reads view_mode and stores it in state.viewMode; the service list component uses this to adjust how many details are initially shown per card (e.g., whether when_to_choose text is expanded by default).
State is initialized from URL parameters first, then enriched with the response of getServicesOverviewContent. If URL parameters refer to a service key that is not found in the returned service_categories, the page simply does not set the related highlight or last-viewed flags and proceeds with a standard list rendering.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same cinematic hero structure from the mockup: a full-width, full-height hero at the top with a persistent navigation bar. Within a centered max-width container, the hero body uses a two-column layout: the left column (about 60–65% width) carries a bold, oversized page title like “Services” (or similar) and a short sub-line, while the right column (about 35–40% width) carries a concise explanation of the services offering and a primary CTA leading into service details. Below the hero, content continues in a linear, top-to-bottom flow using wide, full-width sections that remain aligned to the same central content band, preserving the strong left-right rhythm established in the hero.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same cinematic hero structure from the mockup: a full-width, full-height hero at the top with a persistent navigation bar. Within a centered max-width container, the hero body uses a two-column layout: the left column (about 60–65% width) carries a bold, oversized page title like “Services” (or similar) and a short sub-line, while the right column (about 35–40% width) carries a concise explanation of the services offering and a primary CTA leading into service details. Below the hero, content continues in a linear, top-to-bottom flow using wide, full-width sections that remain aligned to the same central content band, preserving the strong left-right rhythm established in the hero.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the full viewport height on desktop, with the top navigation using a shallow band (10–15% of height) and the remainder devoted to the hero content. Horizontal space uses a centered max-width container (e.g., 1120–1280px) sitting inside a full-bleed black background. Within this container, the left hero column spans about 7/12 of the grid, the right column spans about 5/12. Vertical spacing between major sections (hero → services listing → any subsequent content) is generous, in the 80–120px range, to keep the premium, airy feel. Internal spacing between elements in each section follows the 8px scale, with 16–32px gaps between stacked text and 48–64px padding from the viewport edges to content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains intentionally low-density at the top of the page. The hero prioritizes a minimal set of elements: page title, one or two short lines of supporting text, and a primary CTA. The services overview list below can show more information but should still respect generous spacing: clear separation between categories, ample breathing room between headings and descriptions, and no visually cluttered cards. The overall feel is editorial and high-end rather than dense or data-heavy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the modified Z-pattern of the mockup. The user’s eye starts at the top-left logo, moves along the top bar to the right where a bright magenta CTA and circular menu icon live. From there, the eye drops diagonally down to the oversized left-column hero heading that declares the Services context. After locking onto this message, attention naturally shifts right to the explanatory paragraph and the main CTA that leads into service exploration. As the user scrolls, the flow becomes more vertical and sequential, moving down through each service category section in a clean, linear path with clear visual anchors (section titles aligned left, descriptions and links aligned consistently).

layout.overall_description: The Services Overview page opens on a full-bleed black background, with a slim, fixed-height navigation bar pinned to the very top. The logo sits at the far left of this bar, glowing in the brand’s magenta–cyan accent, while on the far right a solid magenta primary CTA button and a minimal circular menu icon balance the header visually. Directly beneath, the hero fills nearly the rest of the initial viewport. A centered content band—spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width within a max-width container—houses a two-column layout. On the left, occupying about two-thirds of this band, an oversized, two-line “Services” headline (or equivalent) dominates the space, set in stark white typography with generous line height and ample negative space above and below. Just beneath it, a short sub-line reinforces that these services encompass web design, product design, and software builds. On the right, taking up about one-third of the content width, a neatly stacked block of text explains how the agency structures its service categories and how users can explore them, followed by a prominent CTA button leading into the primary services section. The margins between the headline and the right column remain wide, letting the black background show through as active negative space. Scrolling down, the hero gives way to the services listing area, which continues the same centered max-width band. Each service category section is aligned left, with large, confident headings, a single short paragraph of explanation, and clear actions leading to details. Vertical spacing between sections is generous, so each category feels like a distinct, breathable band of content against the dark backdrop. This preserves the minimalist, cinematic quality of the mockup while guiding users smoothly from the high-impact hero into the more practical exploration of services.

name: Web Design & Development
title: Web Design & Development
description: Functional service detail page that loads web design & development content and provides a configurable web project quote form (including website redesign requests) using the assigned interfaces.

core_features: Load Web Design & Development service content (including general offerings and website redesign section) via getWebDesignServiceContent when the page is opened.
Render service information sections (intro, offerings overview, website redesign section) entirely from getWebDesignServiceContent without hardcoded copy.
Display a dedicated website redesign section that uses redesign_section data and provides controls to preselect a website_redesign project_type in the quote form.
Render a web project quote form whose project type and timeline options are dynamically populated from getWebDesignServiceContent.project_type_options and getWebDesignServiceContent.timeline_options.

Collect web project quote details (project_type, approximate_number_of_pages, exact_budget, timeline, additional_details, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone) and submit them via submitWebProjectQuoteRequest to create WebProjectQuoteRequest records.
Provide validation and feedback states (success/error) for quote submissions based on the response from submitWebProjectQuoteRequest, without resubmitting on page reload.
Expose a secondary call-to-action that navigates to contact.html with topic=project_inquiry via URL parameters for users who prefer to initiate the project via email.
user_workflows: Workflow: View web design & development service information. Step 1: The user navigates to web_design_development.html (optionally with URL parameters). Step 2: On page load, a data-loading component invokes getWebDesignServiceContent. Step 3: When the response resolves, the page title and sections (intro_copy, offerings_overview, redesign_section) are bound to content components and rendered. Step 4: The user scrolls through the general offerings and the website redesign section; no data is submitted during this read-only interaction.

Workflow: Request a website redesign quote from the redesign section. Step 1: The user either arrives with a URL parameter setting project_type=website_redesign or clicks a control in the redesign section that updates the URL to include project_type=website_redesign and scrolls to the quote form. Step 2: The quote form reads the project_type URL parameter on initialization or when the URL changes and preselects the matching option from project_type_options returned by getWebDesignServiceContent. Step 3: The user enters approximate_number_of_pages and exact_budget, selects a timeline from timeline_options, optionally fills additional_details, and enters contact_name, contact_email, and optional contact_phone. Step 4: On form submission, the form validates required fields and invokes submitWebProjectQuoteRequest with the current field values. Step 5: On a successful response, the component displays a confirmation state, can optionally clear or lock the form, and updates the URL (for example, by adding a quote_status indicator and the returned quote_request.id) so that reloading the page preserves the confirmation without duplicating the submission.

Workflow: Request a non-redesign web project quote (new website, web app, landing page, or other). Step 1: The user either lands with a project_type URL parameter corresponding to one of the configured project_type_options or selects a project type directly in the form. Step 2: The quote form keeps the selected project_type synchronized with the URL parameter so that the link can be shared or revisited with the same selection. Step 3: The user completes the remaining fields and submits the form, which calls submitWebProjectQuoteRequest. Step 4: On success, the user sees confirmation and can choose to remain on the page; on failure, error feedback is shown, but the previously entered values remain in component state so they can be corrected and resubmitted.

Workflow: Switch from the quote form to an email-based inquiry. Step 1: The user notices a secondary control near the quote form offering an email/contact alternative. Step 2: On click, the browser navigates to contact.html with the URL parameter topic set to project_inquiry (e.g., contact.html?topic=project_inquiry) without invoking additional interfaces. Step 3: Optional contextual parameters (such as the current project_type from the quote form) can also be appended to the URL if the receiving page supports them, enabling the contact page to pre-contextualize the inquiry.
interactions: On page load (onLoad), call getWebDesignServiceContent once; store its response in page-level state and render the title, intro_copy, offerings_overview, redesign_section, project_type_options, and timeline_options into their respective components.

When URL parameters change (onURLParamChange) while the user is on the page, synchronize relevant local state (for example, project_type, timeline, and any section anchor such as a focus on the redesign section) to keep the form and scroll position consistent with the URL.

When the user clicks a control in the website redesign section (onClick) intended to start a redesign quote, update the URL to include or replace the project_type parameter with the enum value representing website_redesign and programmatically scroll to the quote form component.

When the user selects a project type within the quote form (onChange), update local form state and set or update the project_type URL parameter to the selected enum value, ensuring that it corresponds to one of the values present in project_type_options.

When the user selects a timeline option (onChange), update local form state and set or update the timeline URL parameter to the selected enum value, ensuring that it matches one of the values returned in timeline_options.

When the user edits numerical or text fields such as approximate_number_of_pages, exact_budget, additional_details, contact_name, contact_email, or contact_phone (onChange), update local form state and optionally reflect stable fields (like exact_budget or approximate_number_of_pages) in URL parameters to allow prefilling on reload.

When the user submits the quote form (onSubmit), prevent the default form submission behavior, perform client-side validation for required fields (project_type, exact_budget, timeline, contact_name, contact_email), and then call submitWebProjectQuoteRequest with the current form state values.

Upon receiving a successful response from submitWebProjectQuoteRequest (onSuccess), store the returned quote_request object (including its id and created_at fields) in component state for display, set a success flag, display a non-marketing confirmation message, and update the URL with parameters (such as quote_status and quote_id) so that the success state can be restored on refresh without resubmission.

Upon receiving an error from submitWebProjectQuoteRequest or encountering a network failure (onError), set an error flag and bind any error message to an error display component, while preserving current form values so the user can make corrections and resubmit.

When the user clicks the secondary contact CTA (onClick), navigate to contact.html with a URL parameter topic=project_inquiry (and optionally additional context parameters) using standard navigation; no additional interface calls are made from this interaction.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters to manage and restore state, without using localStorage. Typical parameters include a project_type parameter, which must match one of the enum values defined in WebProjectQuoteRequest.project_type and should correspond to an option value in getWebDesignServiceContent.project_type_options; when present, the quote form initializes or updates its project_type selection accordingly. A timeline parameter, when present, must match one of the enum values defined in WebProjectQuoteRequest.timeline and an option in getWebDesignServiceContent.timeline_options; the quote form uses this to preselect the appropriate timeline. Optional parameters for numeric or text fields (such as approximate_number_of_pages and exact_budget) can be read on load to prefill the form and written back to the URL whenever the user updates those fields, allowing users to share a partially completed configuration. A section or anchor-style parameter can indicate which content block should be focused upon load (for example, a value that instructs the page to scroll to the redesign section or directly to the quote form). After a successful submission via submitWebProjectQuoteRequest, the response handler should update the URL with a stable quote identifier (such as quote_id equal to the returned WebProjectQuoteRequest.id) and a status indicator (such as quote_status set to a success enum value); on a subsequent page load, the presence and values of these parameters instruct the page to render the confirmation state while avoiding a duplicate submission (no automatic re-call to submitWebProjectQuoteRequest). Error states are not persisted in the URL and are instead handled in transient component state. Navigation to contact.html includes a required topic URL parameter with the value project_inquiry, as specified in the outgoing navigation configuration; additional context parameters can be appended but must not trigger any new interfaces from this page.

components.functionality: Initializes and maintains the content state for the Web Design & Development page by invoking getWebDesignServiceContent on page load and exposing its response (page_title, intro_copy, offerings_overview, redesign_section, project_type_options, timeline_options) to child components.

Displays the main service information for web design and development using the intro_copy and offerings_overview properties from getWebDesignServiceContent, and binds the page title from page_title to the page-level heading.

Renders the dedicated website redesign content using redesign_section.heading, redesign_section.description, and redesign_section.bullet_points from getWebDesignServiceContent, and provides an interaction that focuses the quote form with project_type preset for a redesign request.

Provides a configurable web project quote form that allows users to select a project_type and timeline from options supplied by getWebDesignServiceContent, enter approximate_number_of_pages, exact_budget, additional_details, contact_name, contact_email, and optional contact_phone, validates required fields, and submits the data via submitWebProjectQuoteRequest to create a WebProjectQuoteRequest record.

Displays non-marketing success or error messages and relevant details (such as a reference to the created WebProjectQuoteRequest) after a submission attempt, using internal state keyed by the response of submitWebProjectQuoteRequest and any URL parameters that indicate a persisted success state.

Provides an alternative path for users who prefer email-based communication by linking to contact.html with the URL parameter topic set to project_inquiry, optionally including additional non-sensitive context from the current page state.

Centralizes reading from and writing to URL parameters for this page, including project_type, timeline, numeric and text form fields suitable for prefilling, section focus indicators, and post-submission status flags; ensures that the web-project-quote-form and other components remain consistent with the URL without using localStorage.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, scrollable page with a dominant full-width hero-style service overview at the top, followed by a focused redesign highlight band, then a large, high-importance quote form section. Within key sections, apply the two-column pattern from the mockup: a wider left column for primary messaging and a narrower right column for supporting text or interactive elements, all inside a centered max-width container based on a 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, scrollable page with a dominant full-width hero-style service overview at the top, followed by a focused redesign highlight band, then a large, high-importance quote form section. Within key sections, apply the two-column pattern from the mockup: a wider left column for primary messaging and a narrower right column for supporting text or interactive elements, all inside a centered max-width container based on a 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Give the top service-overview-section a near full-viewport-height hero treatment with 60–65% width dedicated to the left headline block and 35–40% to right-side descriptive content. Subsequent sections (redesign highlight and quote form) remain full-width bands but slightly shorter than the hero, each with generous top and bottom padding (64–96px). The quote form occupies a substantial vertical slice with a strong presence, but is visually balanced by ample negative space around it.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, premium feel: few elements per section, generous line-height, and clear separation between sections via large vertical margins (72–120px). Keep typography dominant and uncluttered, using internal spacing increments of 16–32px between stacked elements and minimal decorative elements beyond subtle dividers or badges.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the modified Z-pattern from the mockup: navigation/logo at the very top (outside this component set), then the eye moves from the bold, left-aligned service headline in the hero to the right-hand service description, then down to the redesign highlight section, and finally to the quote form. Within the form section, the eye again starts left (context and reassurance) and then settles on the right where the input fields and primary submission CTA live, with the status banner and secondary contact CTA reinforcing clarity and alternatives.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure black background, with a centered max-width content band following a 12-column grid. Immediately below the global nav, a cinematic hero-style service-overview-section fills most of the initial viewport height. In this hero, a massive, left-aligned headline announcing “Web Design & Development” spans roughly two-thirds of the width, broken into two bold lines, while the right third stacks a concise descriptive paragraph and key service details with ample breathing room. Scrolling down reveals a separate, full-width redesign-highlight-section, visually connected but clearly distinct via vertical spacing and subtle dividers, again using a left-heavy headline and right-side explanatory copy. Further down, the web-project-quote-form section becomes the focal interaction zone: a broad, horizontally centered panel where a left column offers brief guidance and reassurance and a right column houses the structured quote form fields, all framed by generous negative space. Just above the form’s fields, a slim quote-form-status-banner communicates progress or submission state, remaining visible as the user interacts, while a secondary-contact-cta is positioned nearby as an alternative path. Invisible technical components like the service-content-loader and url-state-sync-controller operate behind the scenes, ensuring content and form state feel seamless. The overall effect is a dramatic, tech-forward layout where bold typography and clear spacing guide the user smoothly from understanding the service to requesting a custom quote.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, behind-the-scenes component with no direct footprint in the visual layout. It sits logically at the top of the page’s content stack, initializing before the hero-style service-overview-section. In terms of layout, it occupies no visible height or width and does not alter the spacing between sections. While it may trigger loading states within visible components (e.g., skeleton text in the service-overview-section or disabled controls in the web-project-quote-form), the loader itself does not render any standalone UI element on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; effectively zero-size in the layout, influencing only the data and loading states of other visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned immediately below the global navigation bar, the service-overview-section forms a full-width, near full-viewport-height hero. Within this band, content is constrained to a centered max-width container (e.g., 1200–1440px) aligned to a 12-column grid. Horizontally, the section is split into two columns: a dominant left column occupying approximately 7 of 12 columns (about 60–65% of the container width) and a narrower right column occupying the remaining 5 columns (about 35–40%). The left column is aligned to the left edge of the container with a generous left padding of 48–64px from the viewport edge, and it contains a massive, two-line H1 such as “Web Design / & Development” plus a minimal subheading or service label stacked beneath it. Vertical spacing between these text elements uses 16–32px increments, preserving a clean, editorial feel. The right column is aligned to the top of the left headline block, starting with a multi-line descriptive paragraph that explains the service, followed by optional supporting bullets or a concise value statement, each separated by 16–24px. There is ample negative space on the right and below the text so the hero never feels crowded. Top and bottom padding for this section are large (64–96px), giving the impression that the hero breathes within the dark canvas. On narrower screens, the two columns collapse into a single column, with the headline stack appearing first and the descriptive text flowing underneath, maintaining generous vertical gaps between elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section; scrolls naturally with the page. Its height adapts to content but is designed to approximately fill the initial viewport on common desktop resolutions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The redesign-highlight-section sits directly beneath the hero, separated from the service-overview-section by a distinct vertical gap of roughly 72–96px, preserving the spacious, premium rhythm. It spans the full width of the viewport, but like the hero, its content is constrained to the centered max-width container. Inside, it continues the two-column layout pattern, but in a more compact, copy-focused band. The left column, again about 60–65% of the container width (7 of 12 columns), holds a bold but smaller headline such as “Website Redesigns without the Guesswork,” plus possibly a short lead line beneath it. The right column (about 35–40% width, 5 of 12 columns) contains a paragraph or concise bullet list explaining how redesign projects are handled—positioned so that the first line of text roughly aligns vertically with the midline or lower third of the left headline to keep things visually balanced and less rigid than a perfect top alignment. The overall vertical padding for this section is slightly smaller than the hero (48–72px top and bottom), but still clearly generous. Large stretches of black background remain visible to the left and right of the container, and internal spacing between text blocks follows 16–24px increments, ensuring the section feels like a distinct, focused highlight rather than a dense feature list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section; scrolls naturally with the page. Its height is content-driven but visually tuned to feel like a self-contained, editorial band between the hero and the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The web-project-quote-form forms the primary interaction zone and is placed below the redesign-highlight-section, separated by a generous vertical margin (approximately 96–120px) to mark the transition from informational content to action. It spans the full width of the viewport, with the form content contained within the same centered max-width container used above. Inside this container, the section uses a refined two-column layout: a narrower left column for contextual guidance and a wider right column for the actual form. The left column occupies about 4 of 12 grid columns (roughly 30–35% width) and starts slightly lower than the top of the right column, so that its first line of text aligns with the form title or first input, creating a subtle stagger and visual interest. This left column includes a concise headline such as “Start your web project quote,” a brief explanatory paragraph, and possibly 1–2 key reassurance points (e.g., typical response time, no-obligation note) stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing. The right column, occupying the remaining 8 of 12 columns (about 65–70% width), is the main form area: a vertically stacked set of input fields (project type, budget, timeline, redesign vs. new build, etc.), grouped logically using 24–32px vertical gaps between sections. The primary submit button sits near the bottom-right within this column, with comfortable padding (12–16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal) and strong contrast in the magenta–cyan brand palette against the black background. The entire form column is visually grounded by generous top and bottom padding (64–96px) within the section, leaving noticeable black space above the left context column and below the last form field to avoid a cramped feel. On mobile, the form collapses into a single column, with the contextual text appearing above the form fields and maintaining clear 32–48px gaps between major pieces.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section with internal scroll as needed; the form itself scrolls with the page, while its associated status banner may use sticky behavior relative to the form column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote-form-status-banner is visually and spatially attached to the web-project-quote-form, appearing directly above the right-hand form column within the same container. It takes the shape of a slim horizontal bar or pill that spans nearly the full width of the form column (approximately 80–100% of that column’s width), aligned to the right column’s left edge. Vertically, it sits just below the top padding of the form section, with a 16–24px gap separating it from any form headline or the upper boundary of the right column. Its height is modest (around 40–56px), relying on strong color contrast (e.g., a subtle dark gray or slightly luminous accent with bright text) rather than size to draw attention. The banner’s text is concise and left-aligned within the bar, with 12–16px vertical and 24px horizontal internal padding. When the user scrolls within the form section, the banner remains visible: it can stick to the top edge of the form column once that edge reaches the viewport top, maintaining a small offset (e.g., 16px) so it never feels glued directly to the browser chrome. This sticky behavior ensures that submission errors, success messages, or in-progress feedback stay in the user’s line of sight while they interact with the form fields below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as a child of the form section, anchored to the top of the right form column; becomes sticky relative to the viewport once scrolled into position so it remains visible while the user scrolls through the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The secondary-contact-cta is placed in close proximity to the web-project-quote-form but clearly separated from the primary submit action to signal it as an alternative path rather than a competing main action. Within the form section’s container, it is positioned at the bottom of the layout, aligned with the right form column. Horizontally, it appears left-aligned with the form fields’ left edge or right-aligned under the submit button, depending on the overall button layout; in both cases, the visual hierarchy ensures it reads as a secondary option. Vertically, it is separated from the last form field or primary button by a comfortable gap of 24–32px, preserving the airy feel. The CTA can be rendered as a text link with a subtle underline or as a ghost/outline-style button that uses the brand accent color for the border and text while letting the black background show through, making it clearly visible but less visually dominant. If the content is phrased as “Prefer email? Contact us directly,” it may also sit inline with a short sentence to further downplay its hierarchy. On narrower screens, it remains directly below the main form actions, centered or left-aligned, with enough spacing around it to prevent crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the form section; scrolls with the page and does not use sticky or floating behavior. Its position is always anchored to the bottom of the form content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is entirely non-visual and does not occupy any perceivable space in the layout. Logically, it is associated with the web-project-quote-form and service-content-loader, synchronizing things like selected project type or step in the form with the URL. In layout terms, it behaves like an invisible controller that neither introduces gaps nor overlaps visible content. Any state changes it drives (for example, loading a pre-configured quote state from a URL parameter) manifest as changes in the content or active step within the form, but the controller itself does not render any UI element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; functions as an invisible controller with no spatial footprint, only affecting behavior and state of visible components.

name: Care & Maintenance
title: Care & Maintenance
description: Service detail page where users can review website care and maintenance options, see a simplified set of plans, and proceed to full comparison or plan signup.

core_features: Load and display Care & Maintenance overview content using getMaintenanceOverviewContent (intro heading, intro copy, benefits, featured plans).
Show a simplified list of featured maintenance plans based on the featured_plans returned from getMaintenanceOverviewContent.
Allow users to adjust how plan pricing is displayed (e.g., monthly vs. yearly) using URL parameters to control state.
Provide a dedicated button to navigate to the full maintenance plan comparison page (maintenance_plans.html).
Provide a clear action on each featured plan to navigate to the maintenance plan signup page (maintenance_signup.html) with the selected plan id as a URL parameter.
Optionally highlight or focus a specific featured plan when a matching plan id is provided in the Care & Maintenance page URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Read overview and benefits
1. User navigates to care_maintenance.html from the footer navigation or a direct services link.
2. On initial load, the page runs getMaintenanceOverviewContent with no parameters.
3. The intro section displays the intro_heading and intro_copy values from the interface.
4. The benefits section lists each item from highlight_benefits so the user can quickly understand what is covered by care and maintenance.
5. The user may scroll down to see the simplified maintenance plans.

Workflow 2: Choose a plan and proceed to signup
1. User arrives on care_maintenance.html (optionally with a highlight_plan_id URL parameter).
2. The page runs getMaintenanceOverviewContent and renders featured_plans.
3. If highlight_plan_id is present in the URL and matches a plan.id in featured_plans, that plan is visually emphasized and, if implemented, brought into view.
4. The user reviews plan details (name, description, pricing information, support hours, and other fields available on MaintenancePlan via featured_plans).
5. The user clicks the "Select plan" action on one of the featured plan cards.
6. The page navigates to maintenance_signup.html with a maintenance_plan_id query parameter set to the selected plan.id (e.g., maintenance_signup.html?maintenance_plan_id={plan.id}).

Workflow 3: View full plan comparison
1. User scrolls to the bottom or a designated section containing the full comparison call-to-action.
2. The user clicks the "View full plan comparison" button.
3. The page navigates to maintenance_plans.html. Optionally, if a plan is currently highlighted on care_maintenance.html, the navigation can include a URL parameter (e.g., maintenance_plans.html?highlight_plan_id={plan.id}) to keep the selected context.

Workflow 4: Control displayed billing frequency via URL
1. User opens or shares a link to care_maintenance.html including an optional billing_frequency parameter (e.g., care_maintenance.html?billing_frequency=monthly or care_maintenance.html?billing_frequency=yearly).
2. On load, the page reads billing_frequency from the URL.
3. The featured plans component uses this parameter to decide whether to surface monthly_price or yearly_price from each MaintenancePlan in featured_plans as the primary price indicator.
4. If the user changes the billing frequency via an in-page control (e.g., a toggle), the page updates the URL (without reloading) to reflect the new billing_frequency, keeping the state shareable via URL and consistent on refresh.

Workflow 5: Deep-link to a specific featured plan from other pages
1. User clicks a link from another page (e.g., Services Overview) that points to care_maintenance.html with a highlight_plan_id query parameter referencing a known MaintenancePlan id.
2. On load, care_maintenance.html runs getMaintenanceOverviewContent, then matches highlight_plan_id to plan.id within featured_plans.
3. The matching plan is emphasized (and optionally scrolled into view), so the user immediately sees the relevant plan.
4. The user can then click "Select plan" (navigating to maintenance_signup.html with maintenance_plan_id set) or click the full comparison button.
interactions: On page load: Trigger getMaintenanceOverviewContent to populate intro, benefits, and featured plans.
On intro section render: Bind intro_heading and intro_copy from the getMaintenanceOverviewContent response; no user input required.
On benefits list render: Iterate over highlight_benefits and render each entry as a list item; this is read-only for the user.

On featured plan cards render: For each item in featured_plans, bind MaintenancePlan fields (such as name, description, pricing, support_hours_per_month, and any boolean inclusions) to the card content.
On "Select plan" click within a featured plan card: Read the corresponding plan.id from featured_plans and navigate the browser to maintenance_signup.html?maintenance_plan_id={plan.id}.
On "View full plan comparison" button click: Navigate to maintenance_plans.html, optionally including a highlight_plan_id query parameter if a plan is currently selected or highlighted.

On URL parameter detection for highlight_plan_id: After getMaintenanceOverviewContent completes, compare the highlight_plan_id value from the current URL to each plan.id in featured_plans and update component state to mark the matching plan as highlighted.

On URL parameter detection for billing_frequency: Read billing_frequency from the URL, validate that it matches one of the supported billing frequencies, and set the initial display mode for pricing in the featured plans component.

On billing frequency toggle change: When the user toggles between billing frequencies (if such a control is implemented), update the internal display state and update the URL query string billing_frequency value accordingly, without requesting new data from the server.

On navigation from other pages: When care_maintenance.html is opened via the footer link or direct link, the page behaves the same way, always loading data via getMaintenanceOverviewContent and reconstructing state from URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Care & Maintenance page maintains all sharable and restorable state via URL query parameters, without using localStorage. Supported parameters include: 1) highlight_plan_id: Optional. If present, after getMaintenanceOverviewContent returns, the page checks all featured_plans for a plan whose id matches highlight_plan_id. If a match is found, the plan is marked as highlighted in component state and can be scrolled into view. If no match is found, no highlight is applied. This parameter is also used when navigating to maintenance_plans.html to pass context for which plan should be emphasized on the comparison page. 2) billing_frequency: Optional. Determines which pricing value to emphasize when displaying each MaintenancePlan from featured_plans. If the value matches a known billing frequency in the MaintenancePlan.billing_frequency enum, the featured plans component uses the relevant price fields (e.g., monthly_price or yearly_price) accordingly. If the parameter is missing or invalid, the component falls back to a default display mode (defined at implementation time) without requiring additional API calls. When the user interacts with any billing frequency control on the page, the URL's billing_frequency is updated via pushState/replaceState so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the current view. No other persistent client-side storage is used. Navigation out of this page uses URL parameters as well: clicking "Select plan" constructs a maintenance_signup.html URL that includes maintenance_plan_id set to the selected plan.id; clicking the full comparison button navigates to maintenance_plans.html, optionally including highlight_plan_id corresponding to the currently highlighted or selected plan.

components.functionality: On initial page load, call getMaintenanceOverviewContent with no parameters, store the returned intro_heading, intro_copy, highlight_benefits, and featured_plans in page-level state, and expose this state to child components.
Display the Care & Maintenance introductory heading and copy fetched via getMaintenanceOverviewContent, giving users a concise explanation of the service and what the page contains.
Render a list of key benefits or inclusions of the maintenance service using the highlight_benefits array from getMaintenanceOverviewContent.

Render a simplified set of featured maintenance plans returned as featured_plans, applying URL-driven state such as highlight_plan_id and billing_frequency to control which plan is emphasized and which pricing values are displayed. Delegates per-plan rendering and actions to maintenance-plan-card components.

For a single MaintenancePlan from featured_plans, show plan attributes (name, description, relevant pricing fields, support_hours_per_month, and other flags) and provide a "Select plan" action. If the card's plan.id matches the current highlight_plan_id state, the card is rendered in a highlighted state.

Handle the navigation when a user clicks "Select plan" on a maintenance-plan-card. Reads the plan.id from the clicked card and navigates to maintenance_signup.html with the maintenance_plan_id query parameter set to that id.

Provide the "View full plan comparison" button. When clicked, navigate the user to maintenance_plans.html, optionally including a highlight_plan_id parameter if there is a plan currently highlighted or selected on this page.

Parse and manage URL query parameters relevant to this page (highlight_plan_id and billing_frequency). On page load, it reads current parameters and sets shared state for other components. When the user changes billing frequency or selects a plan to highlight, it updates the URL (via pushState/replaceState) to reflect the new state without reloading the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width, hero-style intro section for the Care & Maintenance service. This hero mirrors the mockup: a top band (global nav, implied) and a two-column hero body with the service headline and key message on the left and supporting copy on the right. Below the hero, sections stack vertically: (1) a benefits section that explains why maintenance matters, (2) a featured plans section with selectable plan cards and an integrated plan-selection navigator, and (3) a closing full-width CTA that invites users to view the full plan comparison. Invisible functional components (data loader and URL state controller) sit above or alongside this structure without disrupting the visual flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width, hero-style intro section for the Care & Maintenance service. This hero mirrors the mockup: a top band (global nav, implied) and a two-column hero body with the service headline and key message on the left and supporting copy on the right. Below the hero, sections stack vertically: (1) a benefits section that explains why maintenance matters, (2) a featured plans section with selectable plan cards and an integrated plan-selection navigator, and (3) a closing full-width CTA that invites users to view the full plan comparison. Invisible functional components (data loader and URL state controller) sit above or alongside this structure without disrupting the visual flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero intro roughly fills 80–90% of the initial viewport height on desktop, maintaining the cinematic feel from the mockup. A centered max-width content band (e.g., 1120–1280px) sits within full-width black background. Inside this band, a 12-column grid is used with a 7/12 left column and 5/12 right column in the hero. Subsequent sections also use the 12-column grid but with flexible splits (e.g., 4/12 + 8/12 for the benefits section, and 12/12 spanning rows containing 3–4 column plan cards). Vertical spacing between major sections uses large 96px–120px margins to preserve spaciousness, and internal spacing between elements uses increments of 16px–32px based on the 8px system. Horizontal padding from the viewport edges to the content band is generous, around 48px–64px on desktop.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low-density and premium. Each section focuses on a small number of core elements: a large section title, a concise explanatory paragraph or list, and a clearly defined set of plan cards. Lines are not packed tightly; headings and paragraphs have ample breathing room, with 24px–32px spacing between stacked elements. Plan cards show only essential features (headline, short description, 3–5 bullets, and a primary CTA), avoiding clutter. Negative space around and between cards is pronounced, especially horizontally.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow keeps the modified Z-pattern from the mockup. Users begin at the top-left with the page/service label inside the hero, move to the top-right of that hero where any supporting key message or status appears, then are drawn down to the dominant left-aligned service headline. From there, the eye moves right to the supporting intro description. Scrolling down, the next focal point is the benefits section heading (left), then the benefits list (right). Below, the flow moves to the featured plans section title on the left, across to the plan-selection navigator in the top-right of that section, then down and horizontally across the plan cards.
