The journey culminates in a strong, centered bottom CTA that anchors the page and invites deeper comparison.

layout.overall_description: The Care & Maintenance page appears as a dark, cinematic canvas with neon accents and oversized typography. At the top, a full-width hero section fills most of the screen, with a centered content band divided into two columns: on the left, a very large, bold service title and short supporting line dominate; on the right, a compact stack of explanatory text and key reassurance messaging sit with ample padding. The background is pure black, letting magenta and cyan accents on headings, buttons, and subtle dividers glow. Scrolling reveals a second, slightly shorter full-width section where a left-aligned heading explains the value of ongoing care, while to the right, a vertically spaced benefits list stretches down, still surrounded by generous negative space. Further down, the featured plans section uses the same central grid but feels like a horizontal gallery: a left-aligned section title and brief intro align with a right-aligned plan-selection navigator, and beneath them, a row of dark plan cards sits across the width, with wide gaps between cards and strong hover/focus states. The entire composition flows down to a final, high-contrast CTA band spanning the full width, with centered copy and a bold button encouraging users to view the full comparison. Invisible controllers and loaders overlay or integrate without disrupting this coherent, spacious layout, ensuring the overall experience remains clean, modern, and focused.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-overview-loader appears only when data for the featured plans is being fetched or refreshed. Visually, it is a full-viewport overlay that sits above all other page content. It covers 100% of the width and height of the viewport with a semi-transparent black layer (e.g., 70–80% opacity over the existing pure black), subtly dimming the hero and all underlying sections without completely obscuring them. At the very center of the viewport, a compact loading cluster is vertically and horizontally centered: a small neon-accented spinner or progress indicator with a short, single-line status message beneath it. This central loading cluster occupies roughly 20–25% of the viewport width and is surrounded by ample empty space (at least 64px padding on all sides within the overlay) to maintain the minimalist, premium aesthetic. The overlay respects the same max-width center alignment for its internal text but is not constrained horizontally; it feels like a light veil over the entire page, keeping the user’s focus in the center while preserving the cinematic background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay: when active, it is positioned fixed to the viewport, anchored to all edges (top, right, bottom, left = 0), covering all underlying content and sitting at a high z-index. It scrolls with the viewport (effectively preventing interaction with underlying elements) until loading completes, at which point it fades out and removes itself from the visual flow, leaving no residual spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The care-maintenance-intro forms the primary hero section at the top of the main content, directly below the global navigation bar. It spans the full viewport width with a pure black background and vertically occupies approximately 80–90% of the initial viewport height on desktop. Within this hero, a centered max-width content container (around 1120–1280px) is horizontally centered with 48–64px padding on the left and right to the viewport edges. Inside the container, the content is organized into a two-column layout based on a 12-column grid: the left column spans roughly 7/12 of the width, and the right column 5/12. The vertical alignment of both columns is centered within the hero’s height, leaving generous top and bottom margins of 96–120px above and below the main text block. In the left column, a compact service label or breadcrumb sits at the top, followed by a massive two-line H1 like “Care & Maintenance” in bold, with letterforms large enough to occupy much of the column’s height. A short supporting subtitle sits below the H1, separated by 24–32px of vertical space. The right column begins near the same vertical baseline as the H1’s midpoint to avoid feeling top-heavy. It contains a multi-line descriptive paragraph explaining the service and possibly a short highlight or reassurance line underneath (e.g., uptime, response time), each separated by 16–24px. The right column’s text is set in a comfortable width, with line lengths around 60–75 characters and 24px line-height spacing. The entire hero uses significant empty black space around the text blocks, especially toward the bottom right and top left, echoing the dramatic negative space of the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal document flow, anchored directly below the global navigation. It does not scroll independently or stick; it moves with the page as the user scrolls. On very large screens, the content remains bounded by the fixed max-width container, keeping the two-column layout centered while the black background continues full-width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The care-maintenance-benefits-list sits immediately below the hero intro, separated from it by a large vertical gap (around 96–120px) to maintain a clear sectional break. Like the hero, it is full-width with the same pure black background but distinguished mainly by its content and spacing. Inside, a max-width container aligned and sized identically to the hero’s content band is centered. Within this container, the section uses a 12-column grid with an asymmetric split that reverses emphasis compared to the hero: the left portion spans about 4/12 of the width and the right portion about 8/12. The left column holds the section label and heading, e.g., a small uppercase label like “Why Ongoing Care” followed by a medium-large H2. These elements are stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing and occupy the upper-left quadrant of the section with generous empty space below. The right column starts near the same top baseline and contains the benefits list, which may be a vertical stack of bullets or short, compact rows. Each benefit item has a short title and possibly a one-line description, separated by 16–24px of vertical space to avoid visual clutter. The list extends downward, using roughly 60–70% of the section’s height, while the bottom portion of the container and the left side under the heading remain mostly empty, preserving a light, editorial feel. Overall, the section’s height is modest compared to the hero—approximately 50–70% of the viewport height—while still feeling spacious.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in normal flow, aligned to the same max-width as other content. It scrolls normally and does not stick or float. On narrower viewports, the two columns collapse into a single vertical stack, with the heading block appearing above the list, maintaining generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-maintenance-plans component forms the main decision-making section and sits below the benefits list, separated by a large vertical margin (around 96px) to clearly signal a new content area. It spans the full page width with the same dark background. Inside, the familiar centered max-width container is used. This section’s top portion is a horizontal header row aligned to the 12-column grid: the left side, occupying about 7/12 of the width, contains the section label and a concise H2 such as “Featured Care Plans,” possibly followed by one short descriptive line. These are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing. The right side of this header row, about 5/12 of the width, is reserved for the plan-selection-navigator, which aligns to the top-right of the content band and sits on the same horizontal baseline as the section title. Below this header row, a generous vertical gap (32–40px) leads into the plan cards area. In this area, multiple maintenance-plan-card components are laid out as a row within the grid: each card spans approximately 3–4 columns depending on the number of featured plans (e.g., three cards at 3.5–4 columns each with 24–32px gaps between them). The cards are horizontally aligned and top-aligned, leaving a substantial strip of black space above and below them within the section. The central or recommended plan card may subtly protrude visually via accent color or scale, but all cards share equal horizontal spacing, maintaining a balanced, gallery-like presentation. The section height is driven by the height of the cards plus roomy top and bottom padding (48–64px above the header row and below the cards), creating a substantial block that feels important yet not crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in normal flow. The plan cards within it respond to viewport width; on wide screens, they align in a single row; on smaller screens, the layout gracefully breaks into two per row or a horizontal scroll strip while preserving generous spacing. The section itself does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each maintenance-plan-card is a dark, elevated tile within the featured-maintenance-plans section’s card row. Visually, a card occupies roughly 25–30% of the max-width container on desktop, aligning with 3–4 columns of the 12-column grid, and has a fixed, balanced height sized to fit its text content plus padding (typically 280–380px tall, depending on content). The card background is a slightly lighter charcoal than the page’s pure black to distinguish it, with subtle neon magenta or cyan accents used for the border, top stripe, or key price text. Internal padding is generous: 24–32px on all sides, keeping text blocks away from edges. The content stack inside each card follows a clear vertical hierarchy: at the top, a small plan label (e.g., “Basic,” “Pro,” “Elite”) sits with some breathing room, followed by a mid-sized plan name or tagline, then the price line set in bold, larger type. These elements are spaced using 16–24px increments. Below the price, a short list of 3–5 key features is vertically stacked, each on its own line or compact bullet, spaced by about 8–12px to remain readable without overwhelming the card. At the bottom, a primary CTA button spans about 70–80% of the card width, centered or left-aligned depending on the site’s design language, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, matching the elevated, neon accent style from the hero’s main CTA. The button is separated from the last feature bullet by 24–32px. Surrounding each card, a horizontal gap of 24–32px and a smaller vertical gap of about 24px ensure that cards never visually merge. The recommended plan card may have a slightly thicker border or a subtle glow, but overall, each card remains minimal and spacious.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each card is a regular, non-sticky tile within a responsive grid or flex row. It maintains its internal padding and hierarchy regardless of wrapping; on smaller screens it stacks vertically with 24–32px vertical gaps between cards but remains centered in the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-selection-navigator is positioned within the featured-maintenance-plans section’s header row, anchored to the top-right of the main content container. Horizontally, it sits in the rightmost 5/12 of the grid, aligning its right edge with the container’s right padding, mirroring how the mockup’s top-right CTA and menu cluster. Vertically, it aligns to the same baseline as the section’s H2 on the left, creating a tight, balanced horizontal band. The navigator itself is a compact but visually distinct control—such as a segmented toggle or pill-based selector—spanning roughly 40–60% of its column width (about 20–30% of the overall container width), leaving negative space to the left and above/below. It might consist of 2–4 horizontally arranged options (e.g., “Core,” “Growth,” “Enterprise”), each pill having 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, separated by small 4–8px gaps. The active state is indicated with a neon magenta or cyan fill and contrasting text, while inactive states show minimal outlines or muted text on the dark background. The navigator’s total height, including internal elements, is modest (around 40–56px), and it does not draw more attention than the section title, but it stands out as a high-control, interactive element in the top-right corner of the plans area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the featured-maintenance-plans header, flowing with the section’s layout. It does not stick to the viewport when scrolling. On smaller screens, it may drop below the section title and center horizontally, but it retains its compact, pill-like shape and does not expand to full-width unless necessary for usability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The full-comparison-cta appears as the final major section near the bottom of the page, following the featured plans section with a generous vertical gap of about 96–120px. It spans the full width of the page, again on a pure black background, but is visually separated through strong typography and a prominent button. Within the familiar centered max-width container, the CTA content is horizontally centered and vertically arranged as a simple, focused stack in the middle of the section. The section height is shorter than the hero but still substantial—roughly 35–50% of the viewport height on desktop—with ample top and bottom padding (64–96px). At the top of this stack, a concise, bold heading invites users to dive deeper (e.g., “Compare All Care Plans in Detail”), followed by a short, one- or two-line supporting sentence centered beneath it, separated by 16–24px. Below this text block, a strong primary CTA button sits centered, using the same bright magenta or cyan accent and size hierarchy as the hero’s main button (12–16px vertical and 32–40px horizontal padding). The button is separated from the supporting text by about 24–32px. Above and below the content stack, there is abundant empty black space so the section reads as a dramatic closing statement rather than a dense information block. The CTA button and heading combination becomes the page’s final focal point before the global footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard centered block at the bottom of the main page content. It scrolls normally and does not stick to the viewport. On smaller viewports, it maintains centered alignment with consistent vertical spacing, possibly reducing the section height but preserving generous above/below margins around the CTA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-controller is a non-visual, structural component that manages the relationship between the user’s plan selections and the URL. It does not render any visible UI but conceptually sits alongside the plan-selection-navigator and featured-maintenance-plans components, ensuring that whenever a user selects a plan or toggles between plan categories, the URL state is updated and can restore the same configuration. From a layout perspective, it occupies no width or height in any section and does not alter spacing or alignment. All visible adjustments it triggers—such as highlighting a different maintenance-plan-card or changing which cards are displayed—are handled by the respective visual components that already adhere to the grid and spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It has no box, no padding, and does not participate in the document’s visual layout. It behaves like an invisible controller or hook that attaches behaviors to other components without affecting their physical placement.

name: Maintenance Plan Comparison
title: Maintenance Plan Comparison
description: Compare website care and maintenance plans side by side, filter by billing frequency, and proceed to sign up for a selected plan.

core_features: Load available billing frequency options using getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions and render them as a selectable filter control.
Read the optional billing_frequency URL parameter and use it to preselect the billing frequency filter when valid.
Fetch maintenance plans for the currently selected billing frequency using getMaintenancePlansForComparison and display them in a comparison table.
Maintain a comparison layout that allows horizontal and vertical scrolling across multiple maintenance plans while keeping key headers visible (e.g., plan name and main attributes).
Allow users to change the billing frequency filter, which updates the URL billing_frequency parameter and reloads the comparison data via getMaintenancePlansForComparison.
Provide a 'Select plan' action for each maintenance plan that navigates to maintenance_signup.html with the appropriate maintenance_plan_id URL parameter.
Handle loading, empty, and error states for both filter options and maintenance plans without using any static or hardcoded data.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Accessing the page and viewing plans
1) User navigates to maintenance_plans.html, optionally with a billing_frequency query parameter in the URL.
2) On page load, the page-init logic calls getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions to retrieve available billing frequency options.
3) The page checks the billing_frequency URL parameter against the returned options. If it matches one of the available values, that value becomes the selected billing frequency; otherwise, the first available option (from the API response) is used as the default.
4) The page then calls getMaintenancePlansForComparison with the selected billing frequency.
5) While data is loading, a loading state is shown instead of the comparison table.
6) Once data is returned, the comparison table is rendered with one column per maintenance plan, showing fields such as name, pricing for the selected frequency, support hours, and other attributes included in the MaintenancePlan model.
7) The user can scroll horizontally and vertically through the table while a header row and key identifying information for each plan remain visible.

Workflow: Changing billing frequency
1) The user clicks on a different billing frequency option in the filter control (options are sourced from getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions).
2) The filter component emits an event with the newly selected billing frequency value.
3) The page controller updates the internal state for selected billing frequency and updates the billing_frequency query parameter in the URL (using history APIs) to reflect the current selection.
4) The page controller calls getMaintenancePlansForComparison with the new billing frequency value.
5) The comparison table shows a loading state while the new data is being fetched.
6) When the new plans are returned, the table updates to show only the plans for that billing frequency.
7) If the API returns an empty array, an empty-state message is shown prompting the user to try another billing frequency (using the same filter control).

Workflow: Selecting a plan and proceeding to signup
1) After reviewing the comparison table, the user identifies a suitable maintenance plan in one of the table columns.
2) The user clicks the 'Select plan' button associated with that specific MaintenancePlan record.
3) The comparison table component emits an event containing the id of the selected plan.
4) A navigation handler constructs a URL in the format maintenance_signup.html?maintenance_plan_id={selected_plan_id}.
5) The browser navigates to that URL, where maintenance_signup.html can use the maintenance_plan_id parameter to prefill or fetch the relevant plan details for signup.

Workflow: Error handling and retry
1) If getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions fails (e.g., network error), the page shows an error state for the filter section with an option to retry.
2) When the user clicks the retry action, getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions is called again.
3) If getMaintenancePlansForComparison fails for the selected billing frequency, the page shows an error state in place of the comparison table, with a retry action.
4) When the user clicks the retry action, getMaintenancePlansForComparison is retried with the current billing frequency value from state (which still matches the billing_frequency URL parameter).
interactions: On page load: Automatically call getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions and then getMaintenancePlansForComparison using the selected billing frequency derived from URL parameters and/or available options.

Click on billing frequency option: Updates the selected billing frequency, updates the billing_frequency query parameter in the URL, triggers a new call to getMaintenancePlansForComparison, and refreshes the comparison table.

Scroll within the comparison table: Horizontal and vertical scrolling are enabled while header rows and key identifying columns remain fixed; scroll events are used only for layout behavior and do not trigger new data loads.
Click 'Select plan' button for a specific plan: Triggers navigation to maintenance_signup.html with maintenance_plan_id set to the id of the selected MaintenancePlan.
Click retry on filter load error: Retries getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions and, upon success, re-derives the selected billing frequency and reloads the plans.
Click retry on plans load error: Retries getMaintenancePlansForComparison with the current selected billing frequency.

Interaction with browser navigation: Using the browser back/forward buttons will update the URL billing_frequency parameter; the page listens for these URL changes and reloads plans accordingly to keep the UI consistent with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is centered around the selected billing frequency and the sets of data returned by the two assigned interfaces. On initial load, the page parses the billing_frequency query parameter from maintenance_plans.html?billing_frequency=... If a billing_frequency parameter is present, the page does not trust it blindly; instead, it waits for the result of getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions, then checks whether the URL value matches one of the billing_frequencies.value entries. If it matches, that value becomes the selected billing frequency in state. If there is no match or no parameter, the default selected billing frequency is set to the first available value returned by getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions. Once the selected billing frequency is determined, the page calls getMaintenancePlansForComparison with that value and stores the returned array of MaintenancePlan objects in state. Whenever the user changes the billing frequency via the filter component, the page updates both the in-memory selected billing frequency and the billing_frequency query parameter in the current URL (e.g., using history.pushState or history.replaceState) so that deep links are shareable and navigation is reproducible. After updating the state and URL, the page calls getMaintenancePlansForComparison again with the new billing frequency, replacing the plans in state when the response arrives. No persistent client storage (such as localStorage) is used; the URL is the single source of truth for the selected billing frequency. If the user uses the browser's back/forward buttons to navigate between different URL states with different billing_frequency parameters, a listener reacts to URL changes: it recalculates the selected billing frequency from the URL using the latest filter options and re-calls getMaintenancePlansForComparison as needed. Loading and error status flags live in the page state to control which components are visible at any given time (loading indicators, error messages, or the comparison table), and these flags are always derived from the active API requests and responses rather than any hardcoded values.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and high-level state management for the comparison page. On load, it retrieves billing frequency options, derives the selected billing frequency from the URL, fetches maintenance plans for that frequency, and exposes this state to child components. It also listens for events from child components (filter changes, retries) and for URL changes (back/forward navigation) to keep UI and URL in sync.

Displays the available billing frequency options (such as monthly and yearly) returned by getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions. Highlights the currently selected option, and allows the user to switch between options. When an option is selected, it emits an event with the selected value so the controller can update the URL and reload plans.

Renders the list of MaintenancePlan records returned from getMaintenancePlansForComparison as a comparison table. Each MaintenancePlan is displayed as a column or major row section, showing fields such as name, description, pricing corresponding to the selected billing frequency, support_hours_per_month, response_sla_hours, includes_monitoring, includes_security_updates, and is_active where applicable. Provides a 'Select plan' action for each plan. Supports scrolling across many plans, working together with the sticky header/column layout controller.

Controls the behavior of sticky headers and key columns within the comparison table. Ensures that column headers and essential identifying information for each MaintenancePlan remain visible as the user scrolls vertically or horizontally. Responds to scroll events from the comparison table but does not initiate any data fetching.

Handles navigation when a user selects a maintenance plan. Receives the selected MaintenancePlan.id from the comparison table and constructs a URL for maintenance_signup.html that includes the maintenance_plan_id query parameter. Initiates navigation to that URL so that the signup page can use the plan identifier.

Displays a loading state (e.g., spinner or message) when maintenance plans are being fetched via getMaintenancePlansForComparison and hides itself when loading is complete. It is controlled by a loading flag in page state rather than any hardcoded condition.

Shows an error message and a retry action if getMaintenancePlansForComparison fails for the current billing frequency. When the retry action is triggered, it notifies the controller to call getMaintenancePlansForComparison again with the current selected billing frequency.

Shows an error message and a retry action if getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions fails. When the retry action is triggered, it notifies the controller to call getMaintenancePlanFilterOptions again, after which the page can recompute the selected billing frequency and reload plans.

Displays a message when getMaintenancePlansForComparison returns an empty array for the currently selected billing frequency, indicating that there are no active plans for this filter. Encourages the user to select a different billing frequency via the filter component; it may provide a shortcut interaction to focus or highlight the billing-frequency-filter component.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a full-width, dark hero-like header for the comparison context, followed by a spacious comparison area. Within a centered max-width container, the top header band is laid out in two columns: a dominant left block for the page title and short supporting line, and a narrower right block for the billing frequency filter and a brief explanatory sentence. Below this header, the plans comparison table occupies the main vertical space, with all states (loading, error, empty) swapping into the same area. A subtle, sticky behavior keeps the filter and core table controls accessible while scrolling, and a low-profile selection navigator emerges near the bottom of the viewport when a plan is selected.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a full-width, dark hero-like header for the comparison context, followed by a spacious comparison area. Within a centered max-width container, the top header band is laid out in two columns: a dominant left block for the page title and short supporting line, and a narrower right block for the billing frequency filter and a brief explanatory sentence. Below this header, the plans comparison table occupies the main vertical space, with all states (loading, error, empty) swapping into the same area. A subtle, sticky behavior keeps the filter and core table controls accessible while scrolling, and a low-profile selection navigator emerges near the bottom of the viewport when a plan is selected.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page background spans 100% width and near full viewport height, with a max-width content column (roughly 1200–1440px) centered. The header band (title + description + filter) uses roughly the top 30–40% of the visible content height on first load, with generous top and bottom padding (64–96px). Horizontally, the header uses a 12-column grid: about 7/12 for the left title block and 5/12 for the right description + filter. The comparison table then spans the full 12 columns beneath, with large vertical margins (64–96px) separating header, table, and the page bottom. The sticky bottom selection navigator is a shallow band (~10–15% of viewport height) that spans full width but floats above the dark background so it feels lightweight.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low-density and premium. The header has very few elements with generous breathing room: a large, bold title, one supporting line, and a compact filter control. The comparison table is the densest area but still respects comfortable column widths, roomy cell padding, and high line-height. States like loading, error, and empty are shown as single, centered blocks within the table region, surrounded by abundant negative space to avoid feeling cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a modified Z-pattern adapted for this page: the user lands on the top-left page title, moves to the top-right billing frequency filter, then scans diagonally down into the main comparison table. Within the table, the eye moves horizontally across plan columns and vertically down feature rows. When a plan is selected, attention is pulled toward the lower edge of the viewport where the sticky selection navigator and primary CTA appear as bright, neon-accented elements against the black background. Throughout, contrast and spacing guide the eye rather than heavy borders or visual noise.

layout.overall_description: The Maintenance Plan Comparison page sits on a pure black canvas with a centered, max-width content band. At the top of this band, a hero-like header stretches horizontally, with a massive, high-contrast page title on the left and a compact, refined control block on the right that explains the billing context and houses the billing frequency filter. Generous top and bottom padding around this header creates a cinematic, editorial feel. As you scroll down, the main comparison area fills the screen: a wide, multi-column table of plans rendered in clean lines, with plenty of whitespace between rows and columns so each plan feels premium, not crowded. All system states—loading, errors, or an empty result—appear as single, centered panels in this table zone, framed by negative space. A subtle sticky behavior keeps the top of the table and the filter aligned to the top of the viewport once you scroll past the hero header, making it easy to explore long comparisons. When you choose a plan, a slim, full-width bar slides into view near the bottom of the viewport, showcasing your selection and a bold neon CTA button, reinforcing the tech-forward, high-contrast aesthetic while keeping the experience focused and uncluttered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller has no direct visual representation. Conceptually it sits behind the scenes, governing what appears in the central content band. It determines whether the comparison area initially shows the loading indicator, the table, or an error state. Visually, its influence is expressed by which of the main-body components occupies the large, central region below the header band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no spatial footprint beyond controlling which central-body component (loading, table, error, empty) is mounted in the main comparison region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing frequency filter is positioned in the top header band on the right side of the main content container, occupying roughly the right 5/12 of the width. It sits in the upper half of this right column, aligned to the right edge of the content band, with a short descriptive line or label above it and generous spacing (24–32px) above and below. The filter control itself (e.g., segmented toggle for Monthly/Yearly) is horizontally oriented, spans about 60–80% of the right column width, and is visually compact yet bold—neon-accented controls on the dark background. On wider screens, its right edge aligns visually with the right edge of the comparison table below, creating a clear vertical alignment. As the user scrolls, once the hero header compresses, the filter remains near the top of the viewport within a slimmer sticky header region, still right-aligned, so it feels like a persistent contextual control rather than a floating widget.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially placed statically within the right side of the top header band; once scrolled past a threshold, it becomes part of a sticky top strip controlled by the sticky-header-layout-controller, remaining pinned near the top-right of the viewport above the comparison table.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans comparison table occupies the primary content area below the header band, spanning the full width of the centered content container (all 12 grid columns) while respecting left and right padding of roughly 48–64px from the viewport edges. It begins after a generous vertical gap (about 64–96px) beneath the header, creating a clear separation between the hero-like intro and the data-heavy content. The table uses columns for each plan that consume most of the horizontal space, with a features/labels column on the far left, and 2–4 plan columns distributed evenly across the remaining width. Each column’s content is vertically spaced with 16–24px padding per row, maintaining an airy, premium feel even though it holds structured data. The table header row sits in line with the sticky header behavior so that its top edge aligns with the sticky filter strip when scrolled, ensuring column labels remain immediately visible. The entire table block has substantial bottom padding (72–120px) before the page end, giving the content room to breathe and leaving visual space for the bottom selection navigator to overlay without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically within the main content area. Its header row can visually dock under the sticky header when the user scrolls, appearing to slide beneath a fixed top band controlled by the sticky-header-layout-controller; the body of the table continues beneath this fixed region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This layout controller governs a subtle transformation of the top of the page. Initially, the hero-like header (title left, filter right) takes up a tall band with generous padding at the top of the content. As the user scrolls down into the comparison table, the controller compresses this band into a much slimmer sticky strip at the very top of the viewport. In this compressed state, a reduced version of the page title or section label appears left-aligned, while the billing frequency filter remains on the right, both sitting above the comparison table. The result looks like a sleek, dark, horizontal bar, spanning the full width of the content band (and optionally edge-to-edge for extra drama), preserving the modern, cinematic feel while keeping essential context and controls persistently visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Controls a sticky, fixed-position horizontal strip at the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past the initial header. This strip overlays the top of the scrollable comparison table, maintaining constant visibility of key controls while allowing the table beneath to scroll independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan selection navigator appears as a low-profile, full-width bar near the bottom edge of the viewport once the user selects a plan from the comparison table. It visually floats above the dark background, aligned to the same max-width content band as the table: the main interactive content (selected plan name, price, and CTA) is centered within this band, with equal left and right margins. The bar height is modest—around 64–80px—so it feels like a sleek overlay rather than a heavy footer. Within the bar, the left two-thirds area is used for concise text like the selected plan label and summary, while the right third houses a prominent, neon-accented primary button (e.g., "Continue" or "Sign Up"), right-aligned and vertically centered. Adequate vertical padding inside the bar (16–20px) ensures touch-friendly tap targets while maintaining the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Hidden by default and slides into view as a sticky element fixed to the bottom of the viewport when a plan is selected. It remains fixed while the user scrolls the comparison table, appearing to float above the content, and can dismiss or hide when no plan is selected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator occupies the same spatial footprint as the comparison table, but with a radically simplified presentation. When active, the main comparison area below the header is replaced by a single, centered loading element: for instance, a subtle neon-accented spinner or progress glyph. This indicator is horizontally centered within the content band and vertically centered within the visible portion of the main content area (excluding the header), leaving generous negative space above, below, and to the sides. It sits about mid-screen, with no table outlines or other elements visible, reinforcing focus and maintaining the minimalist, cinematic feel during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the plans-comparison-table within the main scrollable area. It scrolls naturally with the page but due to its central centering and surrounding whitespace, it appears as a focused, standalone state instead of inline noise.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans error state is displayed in the central region where the comparison table would normally appear. It takes the form of a single, compact error panel centered within the content band, with its width limited to roughly 50–60% of the container to maintain a premium look. The panel is horizontally centered and placed with generous margins from the header above and the bottom of the viewport below (48–96px), sitting roughly in the visual center of the page. Inside this panel, a bold, high-contrast error headline appears at the top, with a short explanatory paragraph beneath and an optional "Retry" or similar action button. The panel edges blend into the dark background, possibly with only a thin neon accent line or subtle glow, keeping it visually aligned with the overall high-contrast aesthetic while clearly signaling a distinct state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the plans-comparison-table within the main content region when an error occurs. It scrolls with the page but because it is vertically centered and solitary, it stands as a clear, focused state rather than part of a list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter options error state appears in close proximity to the billing frequency filter, within the top header band or sticky header strip. When the filter fails to load its options, a concise error message is displayed directly below the filter control, aligned to the same right-side column as the filter itself. This message uses small but high-contrast text and may include a thin neon accent icon to indicate an issue. The vertical spacing between the filter control and the error text is tight (8–12px) so the relationship is obvious, but there remains ample padding (24–32px) below the combined block to maintain the airy composition of the header. The rest of the page layout remains intact, with the comparison area either hidden or in a neutral state until valid filter data is available.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the billing-frequency-filter within the header and sticky header contexts. It moves with the header content and participates in the sticky behavior governed by the sticky-header-layout-controller, remaining visible near the top-right when active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans empty state occupies the main comparison table region when no plans match the current filters or when there are simply no plans to display. It presents a single, centered message block, similar in placement to the error state but with a calmer, neutral tone. This block is horizontally centered, with a width of about 50–60% of the content band, and positioned roughly in the middle of the viewport beneath the header, surrounded by ample dark negative space. The content includes a short, confident headline (e.g., "No plans match your filters"), a brief explanatory line, and optionally a small, secondary action like "Reset filters". The typography remains large enough to be readable from a distance, with 16–24px vertical spacing between lines, preserving the premium, editorial feel of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the plans-comparison-table inside the main scrollable area when active. It scrolls naturally with the page, but due to its centered positioning and isolation, it reads as a focused state rather than part of the table.

name: Maintenance Plan Signup
title: Maintenance Plan Signup
description: Page where users review their selected maintenance plan and submit an onboarding request with company details.

core_features: Read the maintenance_plan_id URL parameter to determine which maintenance plan the page should load.
Call getMaintenancePlanSignupContext with maintenance_plan_id to retrieve the selected plan details and signup_intro_copy.
Display a summary of the selected maintenance plan, including billing frequency, pricing, support hours, and key inclusions from the plan object.
Show signup_intro_copy as contextual guidance above the onboarding form.
Provide a form that collects company_name, primary_website_url, and preferred_start_date required for a MaintenancePlanSignup.
Validate required fields before submission and show inline or summary validation errors without calling submitMaintenancePlanSignup if validation fails.
On a valid submission, call submitMaintenancePlanSignup with maintenance_plan_id, company_name, primary_website_url, and preferred_start_date.
Handle the submitMaintenancePlanSignup response by updating the UI for success (using success, signup, and message from the response) or showing a submission error.

Display a confirmation panel after successful submission, using the returned signup snapshot data (plan_name_snapshot, billing_frequency_snapshot, monthly_price_snapshot, support_hours_per_month_snapshot) and the message field.
Provide a 'Back to plans' navigation link from the confirmation state to care_maintenance.html.
user_workflows: Standard signup flow: 1) User arrives from care_maintenance.html or maintenance_plans.html with maintenance_signup.html?maintenance_plan_id=<id>. 2) On page init, the page reads maintenance_plan_id and triggers getMaintenancePlanSignupContext. 3) While loading, the page shows a loading state instead of the form. 4) When getMaintenancePlanSignupContext succeeds and returns an active plan, the plan summary and signup_intro_copy are shown with the onboarding form underneath. 5) User fills in company_name, primary_website_url, and preferred_start_date. 6) User clicks the Request onboarding submit button, triggering client-side validation. 7) If validation passes, submitMaintenancePlanSignup is called and the submit button is disabled while waiting. 8) On success, the page updates to show a submission confirmation and hides or disables the form. 9) User can click the Back to plans link to return to care_maintenance.html.

Missing or invalid plan flow: 1) User opens maintenance_signup.html without a maintenance_plan_id URL parameter or with a parameter value that causes getMaintenancePlanSignupContext to fail or return an inactive plan. 2) The page detects the missing or invalid maintenance_plan_id and shows an error state instead of the form and plan summary. 3) The error state explains that a valid maintenance plan must be selected. 4) A link is provided to care_maintenance.html so the user can choose a plan and come back with a valid maintenance_plan_id.

Submission error and retry flow: 1) User completes the form correctly and clicks Request onboarding. 2) submitMaintenancePlanSignup is called, but the response indicates success is false or the call fails. 3) The page shows a submission error message via the error notification component, keeps the form data in place, and re-enables the Request onboarding button. 4) User can adjust form fields if needed and click Request onboarding again to retry submission.
interactions: On page load, the page-init logic reads the maintenance_plan_id parameter from the URL query string and passes it to the plan-context-loader component.
If maintenance_plan_id exists, the plan-context-loader calls getMaintenancePlanSignupContext and sets UI state to loading until a response is received.
If getMaintenancePlanSignupContext returns a valid, active plan, the plan-summary-panel and signup-intro-text components are populated with the plan object and signup_intro_copy.
If getMaintenancePlanSignupContext fails or indicates an inactive or missing plan, the error-notification component displays an appropriate error and the signup form is not rendered.
User can type into the company_name, primary_website_url, and preferred_start_date fields; each change updates internal form state in the maintenance-plan-signup-form component.
When the user clicks the Request onboarding button, the maintenance-plan-signup-form runs client-side validation for required fields and basic URL and date formats.
If validation fails, the form does not call submitMaintenancePlanSignup and instead triggers the error-notification component to show validation messages.

If validation passes, the maintenance-plan-signup-form calls submitMaintenancePlanSignup with the current maintenance_plan_id and form field values, and the submission-state-controller marks the submission as in progress to disable the form and button.

When submitMaintenancePlanSignup responds with success true, the submission-state-controller stores the returned signup object and message, sets submissionSuccess state, and switches the view to show the signup-confirmation-panel instead of the form.

If submitMaintenancePlanSignup responds with success false or returns an error, the submission-state-controller sets submissionError state and triggers error-notification to show a submission error while keeping the form visible and editable.
In the confirmation view, when the user clicks the Back to plans link (back-to-plans-link component), the browser navigates to care_maintenance.html without additional parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the maintenance_plan_id URL parameter. On initialization, the page parses maintenance_plan_id from the URL query string. If maintenance_plan_id is missing, the page enters a missingPlan state: it skips getMaintenancePlanSignupContext, shows an error-notification that a plan must be selected, and presents navigation back to care_maintenance.html. If maintenance_plan_id is present, the page enters a loadingPlan state and calls getMaintenancePlanSignupContext(maintenance_plan_id). If the call succeeds and returns a valid active plan, the page transitions to ready state, where plan data (plan) and signup_intro_copy are available to bound components. If the call fails or indicates an invalid/inactive plan, the page transitions to invalidPlan state, where the form and summary are hidden and an error-notification is shown. Separately, when the user submits the form, submission-state-controller manages transient submission states: idle (no submission yet), submitting (submitMaintenancePlanSignup call in progress), submissionSuccess (last submission succeeded and a MaintenancePlanSignup object is available), and submissionError (last submission failed). URL parameters are not modified by submission; the confirmation view depends on in-memory page state driven by the submitMaintenancePlanSignup response, while all plan-related context continues to rely on maintenance_plan_id from the URL. No localStorage is used; all necessary state is maintained in memory for the active page view and derived from the URL parameter and API responses.

components.functionality: Reads the maintenance_plan_id from the URL query string on initial load and decides whether to trigger loading of plan context or show a missing-plan error state.

Calls getMaintenancePlanSignupContext with the maintenance_plan_id from page-init and stores the returned plan object and signup_intro_copy in page state, exposing loading, success, and error flags to other components.
Displays the dynamic signup_intro_copy returned by getMaintenancePlanSignupContext above the onboarding form when a valid plan is loaded.

Shows a concise summary of the selected maintenance plan using the plan object from getMaintenancePlanSignupContext, including the plan name, billing_frequency, monthly_price or yearly_price as appropriate, support_hours_per_month, response_sla_hours, includes_monitoring, and includes_security_updates.

Provides input fields for company_name, primary_website_url, and preferred_start_date, validates these fields, and on successful validation calls submitMaintenancePlanSignup with the current maintenance_plan_id and form data.

Manages the state of the signup submission process, including marking when a submission is in progress, handling the response from submitMaintenancePlanSignup, storing the returned signup object and message, and toggling between showing the signup form and the confirmation panel.

When submission-state-controller indicates a successful submission, this component displays a confirmation message using the message field from submitMaintenancePlanSignup and surfaces key fields from the returned MaintenancePlanSignup (plan_name_snapshot, billing_frequency_snapshot, monthly_price_snapshot, support_hours_per_month_snapshot, company_name, primary_website_url, preferred_start_date) so the user can verify what was submitted.
Provides a clickable link in the confirmation state that navigates the user back to care_maintenance.html so they can review other maintenance plans.

Displays error messages for missing or invalid maintenance_plan_id, failures from getMaintenancePlanSignupContext, and validation or submission errors from submitMaintenancePlanSignup. It can be triggered by other components and optionally dismissed by the user.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page mirrors the mockup’s hero pattern: a full-width, nearly full-height hero band on a pure black background, with a centered max-width content container using a 12-column grid. Inside this, content is arranged in two main horizontal zones: a slim top region where the back navigation link sits aligned with the main grid, and a taller hero body split into two columns. The left column hosts the large signup headline and concise intro copy; the right column vertically stacks the plan summary panel, any error notification, and the signup form (or confirmation panel after submission). Supporting/system components (init, loader, state controller) are structurally present but visually invisible, simply coordinating which of the primary blocks show at any point.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page mirrors the mockup’s hero pattern: a full-width, nearly full-height hero band on a pure black background, with a centered max-width content container using a 12-column grid. Inside this, content is arranged in two main horizontal zones: a slim top region where the back navigation link sits aligned with the main grid, and a taller hero body split into two columns. The left column hosts the large signup headline and concise intro copy; the right column vertically stacks the plan summary panel, any error notification, and the signup form (or confirmation panel after submission). Supporting/system components (init, loader, state controller) are structurally present but visually invisible, simply coordinating which of the primary blocks show at any point.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section spans 100% of the viewport width and approximately 90–100% of the viewport height. Within the centered content band (e.g., max-width ~1120–1200px), the left column occupies about 60–65% of the width and the right column about 35–40%, following a 7/12–5/12 split. Vertically, there is generous padding from the top of the viewport to the first content (around 64–96px from the page top to the back link, then another 24–32px down to the main headline). The right column’s plan summary occupies the upper part of that column, with the form taking the remaining height. Large negative space surrounds both columns, especially around the left headline and the right column’s vertical stack, preserving the bold and premium feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains intentionally low and spacious. The left headline area is extremely sparse: a dominant H1 and a short intro with ample breathing room. The right column is denser but still airy; the plan summary card is compact and self-contained, while the form fields are grouped with 16–24px vertical spacing, clear section breaks, and generous padding inside the column. Alerts and confirmation messages appear as single, strong bands or panels rather than cluttering the view with multiple small elements. Overall, there is a conscious restraint in the amount of visible content at once, prioritizing clarity and confidence.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey starts top-left with the back-to-plans link (just beneath the global navigation), then drops down and slightly left to the oversized 'Maintenance Plan Signup' headline. From this anchor, the eye follows the modified Z-pattern to the top of the right column where the plan summary panel sits, confirming what the user is signing up for. From there, the flow moves downward through any error notification band into the main signup form where interaction happens. On successful submission, the form area is replaced by a confirmation panel in the same right-column position, keeping the user’s attention anchored in the established focal zone. Supporting system elements (loader, state controller) subtly adjust what appears without altering the underlying flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a dramatic, dark hero screen for the maintenance signup. A pure black background stretches edge to edge and full-height, with a centered content strip that never feels cramped. Beneath the persistent top navigation, a small, understated back-to-plans link anchors to the left, aligning with the main grid and hinting at the larger flow of the site. Below this, the hero body is a two-column composition: on the left, a massive, two-line 'Maintenance Plan Signup' headline dominates, set in high-contrast white, with a short supporting sentence sitting below it and plenty of black space framing both. On the right, a narrower column carries the transactional side: the chosen maintenance plan summary appears as a compact, high-contrast card near the top, followed by any inline error notification bar, and then the main signup form with neatly stacked fields. When the user submits successfully, the form area transforms into a clean confirmation panel in the same right-hand position, preserving the layout logic. The overall impression is minimal yet cinematic: bold typography, neon accent details in the summary and alerts, and ample negative space guiding the eye from left headline to right-side action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This invisible wrapper component defines the full-page hero canvas. It occupies 100% of the viewport width and at least 100% of the viewport height, applying the pure black background and centering a max-width content container (approximately 1120–1200px wide) in the middle of the screen. Within this container, it establishes the main vertical structure: a slim upper band for the back-to-plans link and the primary hero body below. It also sets the 12-column grid and the left/right column split (roughly 7/12 for the left content, 5/12 for the right content), ensuring all visible components align consistently to this grid with horizontal padding of about 48–64px from the viewport edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Structural and non-visible; defines the full-width, full-height hero region and grid without rendering its own visible UI, anchoring all child components within a fixed, centered max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-context-loader shares the same footprint as the right column’s content area. When data is loading, it temporarily occupies the right column from just below the back-to-plans link’s horizontal baseline down through the height that would normally be used by the plan summary and form. In this state, it shows loading skeleton blocks aligned to the right column: a medium rectangular skeleton near the top right (mimicking the plan summary panel’s size) and several full-width skeleton lines beneath it (mimicking form fields). These skeletons sit within the 35–40% width right column, respecting the same inner paddings and vertical gaps (16–24px) as the eventual content. The left column headline area can either show a generic loading title or remain mostly empty, maintaining the sparse hero feel while signaling that the right column content is being prepared.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the main hero body; temporarily replaces the right-column content (plan summary, error alert, form/confirmation) while loading, then yields its space entirely once data is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The signup-intro-text block is anchored in the left column of the hero body, starting roughly 96px below the top of the viewport (accounting for global nav) and 24–32px below the back-to-plans link. It spans about 60–65% of the content container width, from the left grid edge to the central column boundary. Within this block, the main H1 headline ('Maintenance Plan Signup') is oversized and likely wraps over one or two lines, occupying a large vertical slice with generous line spacing. Beneath the H1, a short, multi-line intro paragraph explains what this signup does, set with comfortable line height and a margin of around 24–32px from the headline. The entire block is vertically aligned so that its visual center roughly matches the combined vertical center of the right column’s summary panel and form, creating a balanced, editorial feel with substantial negative space above, below, and to the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left hero column; static and non-sticky, its size and position are driven by its content but constrained to the left 7/12 grid span with consistent outer margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-summary-panel sits at the top of the right column, directly opposite the left-column headline. It occupies roughly the top third of the right column’s vertical space, spanning 100% of that column’s width (about 35–40% of the inner content band) and maintaining 24–32px horizontal padding inside the card. It is separated from the back-to-plans link and the top of the hero body by a vertical gap of around 48px, and from the signup form below by a 24–32px gap. The panel is framed like a badge or trust card: a dark, slightly lifted card on the black background with a subtle border or glow in neon magenta/cyan tones, clearly summarizing the selected maintenance plan (name, price, key inclusions). It visually anchors the right column, giving users an immediate snapshot of what they’re agreeing to before they move down to the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right hero column; static card pinned to the top of the right column’s content stack, with fixed padding and margin and no scrolling behavior independent of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance-plan-signup-form is positioned in the right column directly beneath the plan-summary-panel (and the error-notification if present). It spans the full width of the right column, with its top edge sitting approximately 24–32px below the panel, and extends downward to fill the remaining visible height of the hero body. The form fields are arranged in a single vertical flow with clear grouping: each field or field pair has 16–24px vertical spacing, and key sections (e.g., company details, contact info, website details) may be separated by slightly larger gaps (24–32px), echoing the mockup’s internal spacing rhythm. Padding inside the form’s container is generous (around 24px on all sides) to maintain the airy, premium feel. The primary submit button is aligned to the left within this right column or to the column’s grid baseline, with sufficient space below it (32–48px) before the bottom of the hero section. On small viewports where the form extends beyond the fold, the page scrolls normally; the form does not detach or float, it simply continues the vertical flow of the right column content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right hero column; scrolls with the page as a single vertical block under the plan summary (and alert), with no independent scrolling region or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-state-controller does not render visible UI, but it governs which of the right column’s interactive components are shown at any given time. It conceptually occupies the same spatial region as the form and confirmation panel in the right column. In the default state, it ensures the plan-summary-panel and signup form are visible, while the confirmation panel is hidden. During submission, it may disable the form’s interactive elements and trigger visual states like a loading indicator on the submit button. Once submission succeeds, it hides the form and reveals the signup-confirmation-panel in the exact same spatial slot, maintaining the layout’s balance with the left column. In case of submission errors, it coordinates the display of the error-notification above the form within the same right-column stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logic-only controller with no visual footprint; it dynamically swaps visibility and states of right-column components while preserving their shared grid position and overall layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The signup-confirmation-panel appears in the right column in place of the signup form after a successful submission. It occupies the same width and approximate vertical region as the form did, starting just below the plan-summary-panel (or below an optional slim success indicator) with a 24–32px gap. The panel is visually card-like, using the dark-on-black treatment with subtle neon accents to signal success—a concise heading (e.g., ‘You’re all set’) at the top, a short explanatory paragraph, and possibly key next steps or a button to return to the dashboard or plans. It maintains generous inner padding (24–32px) and vertical spacing (16–24px between elements), echoing the form’s spacing but with fewer elements, resulting in a calmer, more open appearance. The left column headline stays as-is, so the confirmation reads as the right-hand completion state to the original call to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right hero column; dynamically replaces the signup form in the same position when the submission-state-controller switches to the success state, with no change to the surrounding grid or column proportions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-plans-link sits in the upper portion of the content container, aligned to the left grid edge, just below the global navigation bar and above the left-column headline block. It is horizontally positioned flush with the left margin of the left column (same x-position as the headline), and given a moderate vertical margin from the nav (around 32–48px) to separate global and in-page navigation. The link itself is subtle—small to medium text size with a left-pointing icon or chevron—using a lower-contrast color than the primary neon accents but still legible on black. There is about 24–32px of vertical space between this link and the top of the signup-intro-text block, maintaining the linear top-to-bottom flow: back link → hero headline → right column content. It does not intrude into the right column; it remains a left-anchored, slim line element above the primary hero content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline at the top of the main content container; static and non-sticky, it scrolls away with the page as the user moves down, always aligned to the left 7/12 grid span.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-notification appears as a strong, full-width alert bar within the right column whenever there is a validation or submission error. It is positioned directly beneath the plan-summary-panel and above the signup form, spanning the entire width of the right column (35–40% of the overall content band). The bar has a height of roughly 48–72px depending on message length, with 16–24px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding. Its background uses a high-contrast magenta or magenta-red tone that stands out sharply against the black page, with the text in white or near-white for legibility. A small icon may appear at the left of the text, aligned to the right column’s baseline. There is a 16–24px vertical gap separating this bar from the plan summary above and another 16–24px gap before the first form field below, preserving the clean stacked rhythm of the right column’s content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the right hero column; appears and disappears between the plan summary panel and the form as errors occur, without overlaying or shifting out of the overall two-column grid structure.

name: Case Studies
title: Case Studies
description: Browse, filter, sort, and paginate case studies, then open individual case study detail pages.

core_features: Fetch and display available filter and sort options using getCaseStudyFilterOptions.
Display a paginated list of case studies by calling getCaseStudies with URL-driven filters, sort criteria, and page.
Allow users to filter case studies by industry using options returned from getCaseStudyFilterOptions, synced with the 'industry' URL parameter.
Allow users to sort case studies using sort options from getCaseStudyFilterOptions, synced with the 'sort_by' URL parameter.
Allow users to move between pages of case studies via pagination controls, synced with the 'page' URL parameter.
Allow users to open a specific case study detail page by clicking its title, navigating to case_study.html with the appropriate 'slug' URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Browse all case studies:
1. User navigates to case_studies.html without specifying URL parameters.
2. The page reads the current URL (no 'industry', 'sort_by', or 'page' present).
3. The page calls getCaseStudyFilterOptions to retrieve available industries and sort options.
4. The page determines a default sort option from the returned sort options (for example, the first option) if 'sort_by' is not present in the URL.
5. The page calls getCaseStudies with filters derived from the URL (no industry filter), the resolved sort_by value, and an initial page number.
6. The returned items from getCaseStudies are rendered as case study cards in a grid or list.
7. The user scrolls and can change page using pagination controls, which updates the 'page' parameter and reloads the data.

Filter by industry and then sort:
1. User lands on case_studies.html (with or without existing URL parameters).
2. The page calls getCaseStudyFilterOptions to load available industries and sort options.
3. The industry filter control highlights the currently active industry based on the 'industry' URL parameter, if present.
4. The user selects a different industry from the filter control.
5. The industry filter control's change event updates the 'industry' URL parameter and resets 'page' to the initial page number, then triggers a new call to getCaseStudies using the selected industry as part of the filters.
6. The user then selects a sort option (for example, to prioritize conversion uplift or recency) from the sort control.
7. The sort control updates the 'sort_by' URL parameter, resets 'page' to the initial value, and triggers another call to getCaseStudies with the active industry filter and new sort criteria.
8. The grid/list of case study cards updates to reflect the chosen industry and sort order.

Arrive with a preselected industry from another page:
1. From index.html or another page, the user clicks a link to view work for a specific industry. That link navigates to case_studies.html with an 'industry' URL parameter set.
2. On load, the page reads the 'industry' URL parameter and treats it as the active industry filter.
3. The page calls getCaseStudyFilterOptions and then highlights the matching industry option (if it exists) as selected.
4. The page calls getCaseStudies with filters including the specified industry, the resolved sort_by (from URL or default), and the page number (from URL or initial value).
5. The user sees case studies already filtered by the preselected industry and can further change sorting or pagination. Any change updates the corresponding URL parameters and refreshes data via getCaseStudies.

Paginate through filtered results:
1. User has an active industry filter and sort option set via URL parameters and/or UI selections.
2. The page calls getCaseStudies with the current filters, sort_by, and page number.
3. Pagination controls are rendered based on total_count and page_size returned by getCaseStudies.
4. The user selects a different page (next, previous, or a specific numbered page).
5. The pagination component updates the 'page' URL parameter and triggers a new getCaseStudies call with the same filters and sort_by but the updated page value.
6. The case study grid/list updates to show items for the selected page.

Open a case study detail page:
1. User is viewing the list of case studies.
2. Each case study card includes an interactive title bound to that case study's slug.
3. The user clicks a case study title.
4. The click handler reads the associated caseStudy.slug and navigates to case_study.html?slug={caseStudy.slug}.
5. The new page (case_study.html) handles rendering of the individual case study details based on the slug parameter.
interactions: Selecting an industry filter option updates the 'industry' URL parameter, resets the 'page' parameter to the initial page, and triggers a refresh of the case study list via getCaseStudies.
Clearing or resetting the industry filter (if supported by the UI) removes the 'industry' parameter from the URL, resets 'page', and reloads data via getCaseStudies without an industry filter.
Selecting a sort option updates the 'sort_by' URL parameter, resets the 'page' parameter to the initial page, and triggers getCaseStudies with the updated sort_by value.
Changing the current page through pagination controls updates the 'page' URL parameter and calls getCaseStudies with the existing filters and sort_by but the new page value.
Reloading or sharing the URL preserves the current list state (industry filter, sort order, and page) because these are encoded in the query parameters.
Clicking a case study card title triggers navigation to case_study.html with the corresponding 'slug' parameter, without modifying other parameters.
Initial page load triggers getCaseStudyFilterOptions once to populate industry and sort options before or alongside the first getCaseStudies call.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is entirely URL-driven and does not use localStorage or other client-side persistence. The query parameters 'industry', 'sort_by', and 'page' define the list state. On every load or URL change: (1) The page parses the URL for 'industry', 'sort_by', and 'page'. (2) It calls getCaseStudyFilterOptions to obtain valid 'industries' and 'sort_options'. (3) If 'sort_by' is not present or does not match any returned sort option value, the page selects a default sort (for example, the first available sort option) without writing to the URL until the user explicitly changes it. (4) If 'industry' is present, its value is used as a filter passed into getCaseStudies; if absent, no industry filter is applied. (5) If 'page' is missing or invalid (for example, not a positive integer), the page treats it as the initial page (such as 1) for the internal call to getCaseStudies. (6) The page calls getCaseStudies with an object of filters derived from 'industry' (and potentially other filter dimensions, if added later), the resolved 'sort_by' value, the resolved page number, and a page_size defined outside the scope of this description. (7) When the user interacts with filter controls, sorting controls, or pagination, the respective query parameter(s) in the URL are updated and the page reissues getCaseStudies using the new state. This ensures that bookmarking or sharing the URL reproduces the same list configuration.

components.functionality: Coordinates reading and updating URL parameters, fetching filter options via getCaseStudyFilterOptions, and fetching case studies via getCaseStudies. Exposes derived state (currentIndustry, currentSortBy, currentPage, filterOptions, caseStudies, totalCount, pageSize) to child components.

Displays a selectable list (or dropdown) of industries using data from getCaseStudyFilterOptions. Reflects the currently active industry based on the 'industry' URL parameter and notifies the state manager when the selection changes.

Displays available sort options for the case studies list using data from getCaseStudyFilterOptions. Highlights the active sort option based on the 'sort_by' URL parameter or the resolved default and notifies the state manager when a new sort option is selected.

Renders the current page of case studies returned by getCaseStudies as interactive cards or rows. Each card shows key data fields (such as title, summary, and metrics) from the CaseStudy model and provides a clickable title to navigate to the case study detail page.

Displays a textual summary of the current result set, such as how many case studies exist in total and which range is currently visible, based on total_count, page, and page_size data coming from getCaseStudies.

Shows navigation controls for moving between pages of case studies using total_count, page, and page_size returned from getCaseStudies. When the user selects a different page, it triggers an update to the 'page' URL parameter and a new call to getCaseStudies.

Handles navigation to individual case study detail pages when a user interacts with a case study title in the list. Constructs the URL case_study.html?slug={caseStudy.slug} and performs the navigation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s full-width, dark hero band and two-column logic, but adapts it for browsing. Above all components sits the persistent global header (logo left, CTA + menu right). The Case Studies section begins with a tall, hero-like control band where the left column introduces the results via the case-studies-summary, and the right column stacks the industry-filter-control above the sort-options-control. Below this introductory band, the case-studies-list occupies the main vertical flow, with pagination-controls closing the section at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s full-width, dark hero band and two-column logic, but adapts it for browsing. Above all components sits the persistent global header (logo left, CTA + menu right). The Case Studies section begins with a tall, hero-like control band where the left column introduces the results via the case-studies-summary, and the right column stacks the industry-filter-control above the sort-options-control. Below this introductory band, the case-studies-list occupies the main vertical flow, with pagination-controls closing the section at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout uses a centered max-width container on a pure black background, following a 12-column grid. The upper control band (summary + filters/sort) occupies roughly the top 25–35% of the initial viewport height, with a 7/12 width left column and a 5/12 width right column. The case-studies-list then uses the full container width below, with large card tiles laid out in a 2-column grid on desktop to preserve spaciousness. Pagination-controls sit centered beneath the list with generous vertical margin. Invisible logic components (state-manager and navigation-handler) do not take any visual space.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page remains low-density and editorial. The control band shows only essential text and two compact controls, surrounded by ample negative space (48–96px vertical gaps between bands, 24–32px between stacked elements). The list favors fewer, larger case study tiles per row with generous padding and line-height, rather than cramped grids. Pagination is clean and minimal, with only a few clear actions, maintaining the premium feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the global header’s logo and CTA, then drops into the Case Studies section at the top-left summary text. From there, the flow moves to the right column where industry filter and sort controls invite interaction, mirroring the mockup’s left-headline/right-supporting pattern. After any filter/sort action, attention naturally falls downward to the bold, spacious case-studies-list tiles. Finally, the gaze ends at the centered pagination controls, before looping back up to adjust filters if needed—forming a modified Z-pattern repeated at the section level.

layout.overall_description:
Inside a full-width, black viewport, the familiar agency header sits at the very top.
