Directly beneath, the Case Studies section starts with a hero-like content band: on the left, the case-studies-summary spans roughly two-thirds of the width, set in confident, bright typography with plenty of breathing room from the top and sides. On the right, taking the remaining third, the industry-filter-control and sort-options-control form a clean vertical stack, aligned to the right edge of the container, each full-width within that column and separated by comfortable spacing. This upper band feels like a focused control console for exploring work, with large negative space around and between elements.

Below this band, the case-studies-list occupies the main body of the page. Case study tiles are arranged in a spacious two-column grid within the centered container, each tile wide and tall enough to feature bold titles and short descriptions without feeling crowded. Vertical spacing between rows is generous, keeping the page airy even when many items are shown. At the very bottom of the list, the pagination-controls sit centered horizontally with a clear gap above and below, acting as the quiet endpoint of the scroll. The entire composition preserves the cinematic, high-contrast, minimal-yet-bold feel of the mockup: dark background, neon accents, oversized type, and a clear left-to-right then top-to-bottom reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates entirely behind the scenes and does not render any visible UI. It sits logically at the top of the Case Studies page structure, coordinating filters, sorting, pagination, and navigation. It has no visual footprint and therefore does not consume any width or height in the layout; all spacing and alignment are determined by the visible components it orchestrates.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely functional; it does not affect layout flow or occupy space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The industry-filter-control lives in the upper right column of the Case Studies section’s hero-like band. It occupies the full width of the right column, which spans roughly 5/12 of the centered content container and is aligned flush with the right edge. Vertically, it is placed near the top of this band, aligned roughly with the first line of the summary on the left, with about 64px of top padding from the section’s upper boundary. The control itself is a single, clean horizontal element (e.g., a dropdown or pill row) with 16–24px internal padding, and at least 24px of clear space below it before the sort-options-control. Generous empty black space surrounds it on the right and below, preserving the minimal, premium atmosphere.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of a right-column control stack that can become a sticky sub-header on scroll (locked just below the global nav) on larger screens, ensuring filters remain accessible while the list scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-options-control is positioned directly beneath the industry-filter-control in the same right-hand column of the top band. It matches the filter control’s width, stretching across the full 5/12 column and aligning both its left and right edges. A vertical gap of approximately 24–32px separates it from the filter above, maintaining a clear visual hierarchy between the two. The control can present its options as a compact segmented control or dropdown aligned to the right, but with ample horizontal padding so it doesn’t feel cramped. Below it, there is another 48–64px of empty space before the main case-studies-list begins, clearly visually separating “controls” from “results.”
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Grouped with the industry filter in a shared sticky container on larger viewports, so both scrolling and state changes feel anchored at the top of the results area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case-studies-summary anchors the left side of the Case Studies section’s top band. It occupies roughly 7/12 of the centered container width, aligned to the left edge, mirroring the mockup’s oversized headline block. Its top baseline sits slightly higher than the right-hand controls, creating a subtle step-down effect from left to right that reinforces the left-to-right reading flow. The component may include a bold label (e.g., “Case Studies”), a large count, and a short line of descriptive text stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps between lines. There is at least 64–96px of breathing room above it from the global header, and 48–64px of vertical space beneath it before the first row of case study tiles begins, creating an airy editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll; it remains at the top of the Case Studies section while the list and pagination scroll below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case-studies-list starts directly beneath the summary and right-column controls, spanning the full width of the main content container (inside the same centered max-width). A vertical gap of roughly 48–64px separates it from the controls band above, clearly signaling a transition from meta-controls to actual content. On desktop, the list is arranged as a spacious two-column grid: each tile is approximately 5/12 of the container width, with a consistent horizontal gutter of about 1/12 between columns, aligning with the underlying 12-column grid. Vertically, each row of tiles is separated by at least 48px, and each tile has generous internal padding (24–32px) and large typography to echo the bold hero style. The list extends downward, becoming the dominant scrolling area of the page, framed by consistent left and right margins and surrounded by ample black negative space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within the page; it scrolls normally beneath the global header and any sticky filter/sort container. On smaller viewports, tiles collapse to a single column while preserving generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the very bottom of the Case Studies section, immediately following the last row of case study tiles. A vertical gap of about 64–96px separates them from the bottom edge of the list, providing a visual pause before the user decides to navigate further. They are horizontally centered within the content container and occupy only the middle third to half of the width, so they feel compact and unobtrusive against the wide black background. Individual page numbers or next/previous buttons are arranged in a single horizontal row with 16–24px spacing and clear hit areas, maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. Below the controls, an additional 64–96px of bottom padding ensures the page doesn’t feel abruptly cut off at the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the end of the scrolling content; they come into view only after the user has reached the bottom of the current result set.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation-handler is a non-visual layer that ties interactions on the case-studies-list (such as clicking a tile) to the appropriate case study detail routes. It does not render any UI and therefore has no width, height, or positional requirements in the visual grid. Conceptually, it is attached to each list item as an invisible behavior, ensuring transitions feel immediate while leaving the clean, minimal visual layout untouched.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and event-driven; it overlays behavior on existing visual components without affecting spacing or alignment.

name: Case Study Detail
title: Case Study Detail
description: Displays detailed information, metrics, and shortlist controls for a single case study identified by a URL slug.

core_features: Load full case study details using getCaseStudyDetail based on the slug URL parameter.
Display key quantitative metrics from the case study (e.g., conversion_rate_increase_percent and other_metrics) in a dedicated metrics section.
Show whether the current case study is already in the user’s shortlist using the is_in_shortlist flag returned by getCaseStudyDetail.
Allow users to add or remove the current case study from their shortlist via a single toggle button that calls addCaseStudyToShortlist or removeCaseStudyFromShortlist.
Display related_case_studies returned by getCaseStudyDetail and allow navigation to their detail pages via updated slug URL parameters.
Provide navigation back to the full case study listing (case_studies.html) and forward to the shortlist overview (shortlist.html).
Handle loading and error states for fetching case study data, including retry options that re-trigger getCaseStudyDetail.
user_workflows:
View a case study detail:
1. User navigates to case_study.html?slug={slug} from case_studies.html, index.html, or shortlist.html.
2. Page controller reads the slug from the URL.
3. Page controller calls getCaseStudyDetail(slug).
4. While waiting, a loading indicator is shown.
5. On success, the case study detail, metrics, shortlist status, and related case studies are rendered.
6. User reads the narrative and metrics, and can then navigate to related case studies or other pages.

Add a case study to shortlist:
1. User is on case_study.html?slug={slug} with data loaded via getCaseStudyDetail.
2. The shortlist toggle button shows the state based on is_in_shortlist (e.g., "Add to shortlist" when false).
3. User clicks the shortlist toggle button when the case study is not in the shortlist.
4. The button’s click handler calls addCaseStudyToShortlist(case_study_id) using the id from case_study.
5. On success, the UI updates the local shortlist state (is_in_shortlist becomes true) and the button label updates to reflect removal capability.
6. An inline status message confirms that the case study was added.

Remove a case study from shortlist:
1. User is on case_study.html?slug={slug} and getCaseStudyDetail has returned is_in_shortlist = true.
2. The shortlist toggle button shows the state for removal (e.g., "Remove from shortlist").
3. User clicks the button.
4. The button’s click handler calls removeCaseStudyFromShortlist(case_study_id).
5. On success, the UI updates local state so is_in_shortlist becomes false and the button label changes back to the add state.
6. An inline status message confirms that the case study was removed.

Navigate to a related case study:
1. User scrolls to the related case studies list rendered from related_case_studies.
2. User clicks on one of the related case study entries.
3. The click handler navigates to case_study.html?slug={related_case_study.slug}.
4. The page controller reads the new slug and calls getCaseStudyDetail with it.
5. The page re-renders with the newly selected case study’s details and metrics.

Navigate between listing and shortlist:
1. User clicks the "Back to all case studies" link.
2. Browser navigates to case_studies.html without additional parameters.
3. From the case study detail header or navigation area, user clicks the "View shortlist" link or header icon.
4. Browser navigates to shortlist.html, where the full shortlist is displayed (handled on that page).
interactions: Page load with slug URL parameter: triggers reading of the slug and calling getCaseStudyDetail(slug).
Missing or invalid slug: triggers an error state with an explanatory message and a link back to case_studies.html; no API call is made if slug is absent.

Click on the shortlist toggle button when is_in_shortlist is false: triggers addCaseStudyToShortlist(case_study_id), temporarily disables the button or shows a loading state until the response is received, then updates the shortlist status and displays a success or error message.

Click on the shortlist toggle button when is_in_shortlist is true: triggers removeCaseStudyFromShortlist(case_study_id), temporarily disables the button or shows a loading state, then updates shortlist status and displays a success or error message.
Click on a related case study item: triggers navigation to case_study.html?slug={selected_slug}, resulting in a fresh getCaseStudyDetail call for the new slug.
Click on "Back to all case studies": navigates to case_studies.html using a standard link element.
Click on "View shortlist" (or header shortlist icon): navigates to shortlist.html using a standard link element.
Click on a retry control in the error state: re-triggers getCaseStudyDetail(slug) with the current slug.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the 'slug' URL parameter and the responses from the assigned interfaces. On initial load, the page controller reads slug from the query string (e.g., case_study.html?slug={slug}). If slug is present, getCaseStudyDetail(slug) is called to obtain case_study, is_in_shortlist, and related_case_studies. The case_study object populates all detail and metrics components. The is_in_shortlist boolean determines the visual state and label of the shortlist toggle button. The related_case_studies array populates the related case study list; clicking a related item updates the slug in the URL, which causes the page controller to call getCaseStudyDetail again with the new slug, replacing the previous state. When addCaseStudyToShortlist or removeCaseStudyFromShortlist is called, their success responses are used to update in-memory UI state (e.g., a local isInShortlist flag and status message text) without changing the URL. The page does not use localStorage or any persistent client-side storage; all persistent shortlist state is managed via the backend and reflected indirectly through getCaseStudyDetail and the add/remove interfaces. If slug is missing, the page sets an internal error state, omits calling getCaseStudyDetail, and displays guidance to navigate back to the case study listing.

components.functionality: Reads the slug query parameter from the URL, coordinates calls to getCaseStudyDetail, manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded case_study, is_in_shortlist, and related_case_studies to child components. It also updates local shortlist state when addCaseStudyToShortlist or removeCaseStudyFromShortlist complete.
Displays a loading indicator while getCaseStudyDetail is in progress and hides itself when the request completes or fails.

Displays errors when slug is missing or when getCaseStudyDetail fails (e.g., network or not found). Provides a retry control that asks the page controller to re-call getCaseStudyDetail with the current slug, and a static link back to case_studies.html.

Shows primary case study information such as title, industry, client_name (if provided), summary, and any other main descriptive fields available on case_study. It does not initiate any data requests by itself; it relies on the page controller to supply case_study.

Displays quantitative metrics from case_study, including conversion_rate_increase_percent and entries in other_metrics, in a structured way so users can quickly evaluate impact. If any metric fields are absent or null, they are simply omitted from display.

Renders a single button to add or remove the current case study from the shortlist. Its label and visual state reflect is_in_shortlist as provided by getCaseStudyDetail and any subsequent updates. When clicked in the 'add' state, it calls addCaseStudyToShortlist; when clicked in the 'remove' state, it calls removeCaseStudyFromShortlist. It notifies the page controller about success or failure so other components can update accordingly.

Shows transient or persistent feedback messages related to shortlist actions (e.g., confirmation of add or remove, or error messages if the operation fails). It listens for shortlist-related events from the page controller and updates its message text accordingly.

Displays a list of related case studies using the related_case_studies array from getCaseStudyDetail. Each list item shows key summary information from the corresponding CaseStudy and acts as a link that updates the slug in the URL to navigate to that case study’s detail page.

Provides functional navigation controls around the case study detail: a link back to case_studies.html, and a link (or icon) to shortlist.html. These links rely on standard browser navigation rather than API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear top-to-bottom flow with a dominant hero-style case study header at the top of the content area, directly beneath the global navigation (not part of this spec). Within this hero band, content is arranged in a two-column layout echoing the mockup: a wide left column for the case study summary (title, client, overview) and a narrower right column for interaction and quantitative details (shortlist controls and metrics). Below this hero band, a secondary full-width section presents related case studies, followed by a low-profile bottom row for case study navigation links. System states (loading, error) occupy positions above or overlaying this flow without disrupting the underlying structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear top-to-bottom flow with a dominant hero-style case study header at the top of the content area, directly beneath the global navigation (not part of this spec). Within this hero band, content is arranged in a two-column layout echoing the mockup: a wide left column for the case study summary (title, client, overview) and a narrower right column for interaction and quantitative details (shortlist controls and metrics). Below this hero band, a secondary full-width section presents related case studies, followed by a low-profile bottom row for case study navigation links. System states (loading, error) occupy positions above or overlaying this flow without disrupting the underlying structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero case study header occupies nearly the full initial viewport height on desktop, with a max-width centered content band (e.g., 1200–1440px) and generous left/right padding (48–64px). Horizontally, the left summary column spans roughly 7/12 of the container width, and the right column (shortlist + metrics) takes the remaining 5/12. The top error banner, when present, spans the full content width but uses a relatively shallow height. The related case studies section occupies a substantial band below the hero with ample vertical spacing (72–120px) above and below to maintain a premium, airy feel. The bottom navigation links sit in a slimmer band with extra negative space around them. Loading overlays can cover the full viewport but keep the same centered focus.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and deliberate, mirroring the original hero: large, high-impact typography for the case study title on the left; more text but still relaxed line spacing on the right metrics and descriptive areas. Key elements like the shortlist toggle and main headline have breathing room around them, with vertical spacing in 16–32px increments and larger 48–64px gaps between the hero band and the lower sections. The metrics are grouped and structured but not crowded—likely in a compact grid or vertical stack with generous padding within a subtle panel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a modified Z-pattern anchored by the case-study context: the eye lands first in the top-left hero area on the bold case study title, travels to the top-right where the shortlist toggle lives, then drops back down into the body of the left column for the summary/description, and finally moves across to the right column metrics. From there, the gaze naturally flows downwards into the related case studies section and ends at the bottom navigation links. Loading and error states briefly intercept this flow at the top or center but maintain alignment with the primary axis and do not introduce competing focal points.

layout.overall_description: The Case Study Detail page presents as a dark, cinematic canvas with a centered content band and a hero-like header dedicated to the selected project. Beneath the global navigation, a tall hero section stretches nearly to the bottom of the initial viewport, divided into a wide left column and a narrower right column. The left side is dominated by a large, bold case study title and concise summary copy, spaced generously so the typography breathes against the black background. On the right, aligned with the top of the title, a neon-accented shortlist toggle and its status message sit above a metrics panel that showcases key performance numbers in a compact but airy layout. Below this hero band, the screen opens into a full-width section for related case studies, arranged in a clean row or grid with substantial black space around each item, maintaining the minimalist yet dramatic vibe. At the very bottom, simple navigation links to previous/next case studies sit centered with wide vertical margins, acting like quiet pagination controls. When loading or error states appear, they overlay or precede this structure in a way that is visually striking but still respectful of the underlying grid and flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is non-visual and does not occupy any visible space on the page. It sits conceptually at the top of the Case Study Detail page, orchestrating whether the user sees the loading indicator, the error banner, or the main content layout (summary, metrics, shortlist controls, related items, and navigation). In terms of layout, it exists outside the visual grid and does not alter spacing or alignment of other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; occupies no layout space and does not affect document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the loading indicator appears as a centered overlay on top of the entire case study detail layout. It covers the viewport with a semi-transparent black scrim slightly lighter than the background, allowing a subtle hint of structure beneath while focusing attention on the indicator. The indicator itself is positioned at the exact center of the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, with a circular or ring-style motion matching the neon magenta–cyan color palette. It occupies a compact area (roughly 96–128px in diameter) but is surrounded by generous empty space, reinforcing the minimal, high-end feel. No other components are interactable while this overlay is visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, full-viewport overlay positioned above all other content; centered both horizontally and vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner appears directly beneath the global navigation bar, at the very top of the case study content column. It spans the full width of the main content band (aligned with the same max-width and left/right padding as the hero), forming a horizontal strip roughly 56–72px tall depending on message length. The banner uses high-contrast styling against the black backdrop (e.g., a deep red or magenta edge accent) but remains flat and minimal, with comfortable padding (16–24px vertical, 24–32px horizontal) around the text. Below the banner, a generous gap (32–48px) separates it from the top of the hero case study summary, preserving the airy feel even in error states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal page flow; pushes the hero content downward when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case study summary forms the left column of the hero band. It sits directly under the error banner (if present) or under the global navigation area, aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. This column occupies approximately 60–65% (around 7/12) of the container’s width. The top of this block features the case study title in oversized, bold typography that spans one to three lines, mirroring the dramatic hero headline from the mockup. Below the title, with 24–32px of vertical spacing, are secondary details like client name, industry, and a concise one- or two-paragraph overview, set in a comfortable line length and line height. The summary content is vertically centered within the hero band on taller viewports: there is substantial top and bottom padding (64–96px from the container’s top and bottom) so the block feels suspended in space rather than anchored to the top. On narrower screens, this column stacks above the metrics/right column but still maintains generous vertical spacing between title, description, and subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in main flow; participates as the left column of a responsive two-column hero layout and stacks above the right column on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metrics panel occupies the lower portion of the hero’s right column. This right column starts at the same top baseline as the case study summary’s title but consumes only about 35–40% (around 5/12) of the container’s width, aligned to the right edge of the content band. At the top of this column sits the shortlist controls (toggle button and status message), and the metrics panel is positioned directly beneath them with 24–32px of vertical separation. The panel itself reads as a subtle, slightly elevated block against the black background—either with a very dark gray fill or a faint outline and inner glow using the neon accent colors. Inside, key metrics (e.g., conversion rate, traffic lift, ROI) are arranged in a tidy vertical stack or a 2-column mini grid with strong numeric emphasis and smaller labels below. Each metric item has 16–24px vertical spacing, and the panel has ample internal padding (24–32px all around) to maintain the low-density aesthetic. Vertically, the overall right column content is roughly aligned to the vertical center of the hero, mirroring the left column’s centering, so title and controls sit near the upper half while the metrics panel visually anchors the right side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right hero column; remains aligned with shortlist controls and vertically centered alongside the summary on large screens, stacking below the summary on small screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist toggle button is positioned at the top of the hero’s right column, horizontally aligned with the first line of the case study title on the left. It sits flush toward the right edge of the content container but respects a comfortable right padding (32–48px from the container edge). The button is visually prominent, styled as a pill or rounded-rectangle with neon magenta–cyan accents against the dark background, and sized with generous padding (8–12px vertical, 20–32px horizontal). It is either left-aligned within the right column or paired with the status message to its immediate right or below, but the overall block (toggle + status) occupies only the upper portion of the right column, leaving clear space above the metrics panel. A vertical gap of 16–24px separates the button from any label or the status message when stacked.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; stays anchored to the top of the right column and aligned with the left column title baseline on large screens, shifting above metrics and stacking more centrally on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist status message is visually coupled with the shortlist toggle button, forming a compact cluster at the top of the hero’s right column. On wider screens, the message appears directly to the right of the toggle button, aligned vertically to its center, with a small horizontal gap (12–16px) so the button remains dominant. The text is set in a smaller, secondary type size with muted gray or subtly tinted color that contrasts gently against the black background, indicating state without overpowering the button. When space is constrained (medium to small screens), the status message stacks directly below the toggle button, left-aligned to it, with 8–12px of vertical spacing. In all configurations, the combined block of toggle + status sits 24–32px above the metrics panel, maintaining the clear vertical rhythm of the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; position is responsive relative to the toggle button (inline on large screens, stacked below on smaller screens).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related case studies list forms the main content band directly below the hero section. It spans the full width of the centered content container, maintaining the same max-width and left/right padding as the hero. A generous vertical gap (72–96px) separates it from the bottom of the hero summary/metrics to preserve the airy, premium feel. Within this section, a subtle section label or title may appear at the top left with 0–16px margin above the first row of related items. The list itself presents related case study cards in a horizontal row or responsive grid (e.g., 2–3 items per row on desktop), each card occupying roughly 30–45% of the container width with substantial horizontal and vertical spacing (24–32px between cards). Cards are minimalist, relying on typography and small accent lines or borders rather than heavy imagery, and they sit comfortably amid large areas of black negative space above and below, echoing the editorial, low-density aesthetic of the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main page flow; forms a distinct section below the hero that reflows into fewer columns as the viewport narrows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The case study navigation links occupy the bottom-most section of the page’s main content area, appearing beneath the related case studies list. A large vertical margin (64–96px) separates them from the related section, allowing them to feel like quiet, final controls rather than part of the main content. The links (e.g., “Previous case study”, “Next case study”, or a back-to-list link) are horizontally centered within the content container. On wider screens, previous and next links may sit side by side with 24–32px between them, using understated typography with subtle neon accents on hover. The overall height of this section is modest, but ample top and bottom padding (32–48px) ensures the links are not crowded by the footer below. The black background continues, with no additional framing, so the links appear to float in space as refined text actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main flow; positioned at the bottom of the content stack and remains centered regardless of viewport width, with links stacking vertically on smaller screens.

name: Shortlist
title: Shortlist
description: Displays the user's saved case studies, allows opening each case study's detail page, removing items, and reviewing or comparing shortlisted projects before contacting the agency.

core_features: Load the current user's shortlist and associated case studies using getShortlistItems on page initialization and after changes.
Display all shortlisted case studies in a configurable list (sorting, optional filtering and layout controlled by URL parameters).
Allow navigation to an individual case study detail page by clicking its title (case_study.html?slug={caseStudy.slug}).
Allow removing a case study from the shortlist via removeCaseStudyFromShortlist(case_study_id).
Support a comparison-focused view where selected shortlisted case studies are highlighted and grouped based on URL parameters.
Provide an empty-state handling when the shortlist contains no items.
user_workflows:
View shortlist from header navigation:
1) User clicks the 'Shortlist' link/icon in the header (navigates to shortlist.html with no or existing query parameters).
2) Page initializes and calls getShortlistItems with no parameters.
3) The shortlist list component renders all returned items, applying any sort/filter/view state derived from the URL parameters.
4) User reviews the list and may adjust sort/filter/view options, which update the URL and trigger a re-render using the already-loaded data.

Open a case study detail page:
1) From the shortlist list, user clicks on a case study title.
2) The click handler reads the related caseStudy.slug from the bound CaseStudy object.
3) Browser navigates to case_study.html?slug={caseStudy.slug}.
4) The Shortlist page does not modify shortlist data during this navigation; it simply routes to the detail page.

Remove a case study from the shortlist:
1) User clicks a 'Remove from shortlist' control associated with a specific shortlist item.
2) The click handler retrieves the bound CaseStudy.id for that row.
3) The page calls removeCaseStudyFromShortlist with parameter case_study_id = CaseStudy.id.
4) When the response indicates success, the page either:
   - Re-calls getShortlistItems to refresh the full list, or
   - Removes the corresponding item from the in-memory list and re-renders the shortlist list component.
5) If the response indicates failure, a non-blocking error message is shown and the list is left unchanged.

Adjust shortlist view and comparison via URL state:
1) User changes a sort option (e.g., by conversion rate, by title, by most recent) using a control in the filters/header component.
2) The change handler updates the URL query parameter (e.g., ?sort={selectedOption}) without leaving shortlist.html.
3) The shortlist list component re-renders, reordering the currently loaded items in the client according to the selected sort option.
4) User optionally filters by industry; the filter change handler updates the URL query parameter (e.g., ?industry={selectedIndustry}) and re-renders the list client-side using the CaseStudy.industry field.
5) User toggles comparison checkboxes for certain case studies; the handler updates a URL query parameter (e.g., ?compare={commaSeparatedSlugs}), keeping state across reloads and navigation.
6) The comparison component reads the compare parameter and highlights or groups those case studies for side-by-side review, using only the data already loaded by getShortlistItems.

Clear the entire shortlist:
1) User clicks a 'Remove all from shortlist' control when there are items present.
2) The click handler iterates through the current in-memory shortlist items.
3) For each item, it calls removeCaseStudyFromShortlist with case_study_id set to that item's CaseStudy.id.
4) After all calls complete, the handler re-calls getShortlistItems.
5) If the refreshed list is empty, the empty-state component is displayed.
interactions: Click header navigation link 'Shortlist': navigates to shortlist.html (preserving or resetting query parameters based on implementation rules) and triggers page initialization logic including getShortlistItems.

Page load / refresh: on initial load and on browser refresh at shortlist.html, the page parses URL parameters (such as sort, industry, view, compare) and calls getShortlistItems once; the resulting data is then filtered, sorted, and grouped on the client according to those parameters.
Click case study title: triggers navigation to case_study.html?slug={CaseStudy.slug} using the slug bound from the CaseStudy entity associated with that shortlist item.

Click 'Remove' on a shortlist item: invokes removeCaseStudyFromShortlist with the bound CaseStudy.id; on success, the item is removed from the client-side list or the list is reloaded via getShortlistItems.

Click 'Remove all' in shortlist header: iterates through all current items, calling removeCaseStudyFromShortlist for each CaseStudy.id; once complete, calls getShortlistItems again and displays the empty state if no items remain.

Change sort option (e.g., via dropdown or segmented control): updates a sort URL parameter (for example sort=title, sort=conversion_rate_increase_percent, or sort=published_at) and re-renders the client-side ordering of the list using fields from CaseStudy.

Change filter by industry (e.g., using selectable options): updates an industry URL parameter (e.g., industry=ecommerce | saas | fintech | b2b_services | nonprofit | other | all) and re-renders the list client-side to include only CaseStudy entities whose industry matches the parameter, or all when unset.

Toggle comparison mode for a case study (e.g., checkbox): updates a compare URL parameter to add or remove the associated CaseStudy.slug; the comparison view component reads the parameter and updates which items are shown in the comparison group.

Change layout mode (e.g., detailed vs. compact rows): updates a view URL parameter (e.g., view=detailed or view=compact); the list component uses this parameter to decide which fields from CaseStudy to display while still using the same underlying data.

Pagination or page-size adjustments (if implemented): changing a page or per_page URL parameter updates which subset of the client-side list is visible, while getShortlistItems continues to be called only once to retrieve the complete shortlist for the user.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Shortlist page relies on URL query parameters for all client-visible state. When the page loads at shortlist.html, the initialization logic parses the current window.location.search to derive state variables such as: (1) sort: determines ordering of shortlist items client-side (e.g., by CaseStudy.title, CaseStudy.conversion_rate_increase_percent, or CaseStudy.published_at). If sort is absent, a default ordering rule is applied purely in the client. (2) industry: when present, the list component filters items to those whose CaseStudy.industry matches the parameter; when absent or set to a special value (such as 'all'), no industry filter is applied. (3) view: controls whether the list renders a detailed or compact layout using the same CaseStudy and ShortlistItem data; if absent, a default view is used. (4) compare: contains a comma-separated list of CaseStudy.slug values to be emphasized for comparison. The comparison component reads this parameter to determine which shortlist items to treat as 'selected', and it renders a grouped or side-by-side view using only case studies whose slugs are listed. (5) page and per_page (optional): control client-side pagination; if absent, all items or a default page size is shown. User interactions (sort changes, filter changes, comparison toggles, layout changes, pagination) update these URL parameters using navigation APIs without leaving shortlist.html, then trigger a re-render of components using the already-fetched data from getShortlistItems. Modification of shortlist membership does not use URL state; it uses removeCaseStudyFromShortlist with the relevant CaseStudy.id and then refreshes or updates the in-memory list. Navigation to case_study.html passes the CaseStudy.slug value via the slug query parameter, as in case_study.html?slug={CaseStudy.slug}.

components.functionality: On page initialization and after any shortlist mutation, this component calls getShortlistItems to retrieve the current user's Shortlist container and all associated ShortlistItem and CaseStudy records, then exposes the data to child components.

Parses and manages shortlist-related URL query parameters (sort, industry, view, compare, page, per_page). It exposes derived state (currentSort, currentIndustryFilter, currentViewMode, comparedSlugs, currentPage, currentPageSize) to other components and updates the URL when user interactions occur.

Displays a functional header for the shortlist, including the total count of CaseStudy items currently returned by getShortlistItems (after any filtering), a textual summary of active filters/sort, and optional controls such as 'Remove all' and layout toggle. It listens to shortlist data and URL-derived state.

Provides controls for the user to select sorting (e.g., by title, by conversion metrics, by publish date) and to filter by CaseStudy.industry. When controls change, it notifies the shortlist-state-controller to update the corresponding URL parameters and trigger a re-render of the list.

Renders the list of shortlisted case studies using data from getShortlistItems, filtered and ordered according to the current state derived from URL parameters (sort, industry, page, per_page). It also passes necessary CaseStudy and ShortlistItem data to each row component and determines which items are marked as 'in comparison' based on the compare parameter.

Represents a single shortlisted case study row. Displays key data from CaseStudy (such as title, industry, summary, and available metrics) and possibly metadata from ShortlistItem (such as added_at). Provides interactive controls: clicking the title navigates to the detail page (case_study.html?slug={CaseStudy.slug}), a remove button triggers removeCaseStudyFromShortlist, and a comparison toggle updates the compare URL parameter via the state controller.

Uses the compare URL parameter to identify which CaseStudy records from the current shortlist are selected for comparison. It then arranges their key attributes (such as industry and performance metrics) side-by-side or grouped, without any additional API calls. It updates live as compare selections change.

Reads current page and per_page values from URL-derived state and renders pagination controls to move between pages or adjust visible items per page. On interaction, it updates the corresponding URL parameters and signals the shortlist-list component to re-render using the already-loaded data from getShortlistItems.

When getShortlistItems returns no items, this component displays a functional empty-state message indicating that the shortlist has no case studies and provides a navigation control to go back to relevant browsing pages (for example, case study listings), using only standard navigation with URL parameters (no API calls).

Centralizes calls to removeCaseStudyFromShortlist. Receives removal requests from item rows or the header 'Remove all' action, calls removeCaseStudyFromShortlist(case_study_id) for each requested item, handles success or error responses, and on successful changes triggers the shortlist-data-loader to refresh via getShortlistItems or updates the local list representation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s full-width, dark hero band and two-column hierarchy, but adapts it for a 'Shortlist' utility view. At the very top sits the persistent navigation bar (logo left, primary CTA and circular menu right). Directly beneath, a hero-like shortlist header band spans the full width of the centered max-width container, using a 12-column grid split roughly 7/12 on the left for the main shortlist summary and 5/12 on the right for filters and high-level controls. Below this header band, content flows vertically: the shortlist list occupies the main central column, with each item laid out as a spacious horizontal row. A comparison view section sits below the list when active, followed by pagination controls centered at the bottom. An empty state replaces the list and comparison sections entirely when there are no items. Data/state/mutation components either operate invisibly or appear as overlays (loader, toasts) on top of this core layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s full-width, dark hero band and two-column hierarchy, but adapts it for a 'Shortlist' utility view. At the very top sits the persistent navigation bar (logo left, primary CTA and circular menu right). Directly beneath, a hero-like shortlist header band spans the full width of the centered max-width container, using a 12-column grid split roughly 7/12 on the left for the main shortlist summary and 5/12 on the right for filters and high-level controls. Below this header band, content flows vertically: the shortlist list occupies the main central column, with each item laid out as a spacious horizontal row. A comparison view section sits below the list when active, followed by pagination controls centered at the bottom. An empty state replaces the list and comparison sections entirely when there are no items. Data/state/mutation components either operate invisibly or appear as overlays (loader, toasts) on top of this core layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses a full-viewport-height hero-like top section, with the navigation bar occupying about 10–12% of the viewport height and the shortlist header band consuming roughly 20–30%, leaving the remaining vertical space to the list, comparison area, and pagination. Horizontally, the main content is constrained to a centered max-width container (e.g., 1120–1280px) sitting within a full-width black background. Inside this container, a 12-column grid is implied: the header summary on the left uses about 60–65% width (7/12), with filters/controls using 35–40% (5/12). List rows span the full container width with generous vertical padding, while the comparison section forms a full-width band below. Large vertical margins (72–120px) separate the header band from the list and the list from pagination, with internal spacing increments based on the 8px grid (16–32px between related elements).
layout_strategies.content_density: The density stays deliberately low, echoing the mockup’s premium, editorial feel. The shortlist header uses oversized, bold typography and very few lines of text, with ample breathing room around the headline and summary. Filters and controls are visually compact but spaced out so they never crowd the right column. Each list item row has substantial vertical padding (e.g., 24–32px) and clear separation from adjacent rows, with subtle dividers or shadows rather than dense card stacks. The comparison view shows fewer, larger tiles rather than many small thumbnails. Empty state messaging uses one or two strong typographic elements instead of dense instructions. Overall, negative space is treated as a core design element, keeping the page visually dramatic yet simple.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a modified Z-pattern similar to the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left logo in the navigation bar, then travels along the top to the bright magenta primary CTA and circular menu icon on the right. From there, the gaze drops down and left to the dominant shortlist header title ('Your Shortlist' or similar) set in oversized type. After absorbing this primary message and any count summary, attention moves across to the top-right where filters and view controls live. Below this band, the eye naturally flows down the vertical stack of shortlist items, left-aligned titles drawing attention first, then across each row to action buttons (view, remove, compare) on the right. If a comparison view is active, its band at the bottom becomes a final focal area, with horizontally arranged comparison cards. Pagination controls at the very bottom center act as the end-of-page anchor. Any loader overlays and mutation toasts momentarily interrupt this flow but are positioned to feel layered on top rather than reordering the core hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: The Shortlist page sits on a pure black canvas with a centered, max-width content band that follows a cinematic, two-column hero pattern at the top. A slim navigation bar runs across the very top: the colorful neon logo anchors the left, while on the right a bright magenta primary CTA button and a circular menu icon float with comfortable padding, echoing the main site’s hero. Below this, the shortlist itself is introduced in a hero-like header band that spans nearly the full viewport width within the container. On the left, occupying roughly two-thirds of the content width, an oversized, bold 'Shortlist' headline and concise supporting line summarize how many case studies are saved and what the user can do next. On the right, in a narrower column, filters, sort controls, and any view toggles are stacked with generous spacing, aligned to the top so they visually pair with the headline while remaining secondary. As you scroll, a tall, spacious list of shortlisted case studies fills the page: each row is a wide, low-density horizontal strip with project name and metadata on the left and clear, neon-accented actions on the right. Below the list, a comparison band appears when multiple items are selected, using a darker panel and a multi-column layout to let users visually compare their chosen projects at a glance. Pagination controls are centered beneath this band, with ample breathing room above and below. When the shortlist is empty, the list and comparison sections vanish, replaced by a centralized, hero-like empty state message in the same bold, high-contrast typographic style. Throughout, loaders and feedback toasts appear as subtle overlays, ensuring the core layout remains stable and consistent with the bold, modern, tech-driven aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader sits at the page root and, when active, overlays the entire visible viewport (covering navigation, header, list, and comparison areas) with a semi-opaque black veil that slightly dims the underlying content without changing the core layout. At the exact center of the viewport, a neon-accented circular loader or minimal progress indicator appears, sized modestly (roughly 56–72px diameter) so it does not feel heavy. The overlay edges align with the browser viewport, not just the content container, reinforcing the full-bleed, cinematic black background. When inactive, this component collapses entirely and contributes no visible height or width to the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering the entire viewport when loading, positioned above all other components using a high z-index; non-rendered or zero-size when inactive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state controller is a non-visual logic component that lives behind the scenes of the shortlist page. It occupies no physical space in the layout, does not affect grid or spacing, and does not introduce any visual elements. All layout decisions for visible components (header, list, empty state, comparison view) are computed based on the state it manages, but the controller itself is effectively invisible within the page structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no spatial footprint, no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header summary forms the hero-like body of the shortlist page and sits directly below the global navigation bar, inside the centered max-width container. It spans the full width of the container horizontally and about one-quarter to one-third of the viewport height vertically, with generous top and bottom padding (roughly 64–96px total). Within this area, the content is split into two columns using a 12-column grid: the header summary occupies approximately 7/12 of the width on the left. The main shortlist title is set in very large, bold type (similar in scale to the hero headline on the homepage), breaking across one or two lines and aligned to the left edge of the content grid. Below the title, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space, a concise line or two of supporting text summarizes the number of saved case studies and their purpose. Ample negative space surrounds these lines, especially on the right side of this left column, reinforcing the dramatic, minimal feel. The entire header summary area is visually dominant and is the first thing seen under the nav.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section that scrolls away naturally; optionally, its top edge may serve as an anchor for sticky controls if implemented, but the summary content itself does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters and controls share the same horizontal band as the header summary and occupy the right-hand column, aligned within the same centered max-width container. This right column uses roughly 5/12 of the content width and is aligned flush to the right edge of the grid. Its content is top-aligned so that the primary filter dropdown, tag pills, or sort selector sits horizontally in line with the top baseline of the header summary’s text on the left. Below the primary filter row, additional controls (such as compare toggle, clear shortlist, or view mode switch) are stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps to keep the column light and breathable. Controls are arranged either in one or two short horizontal lines rather than long, wrapping rows, to maintain the clean, minimal look. Overall, this right column visually feels like a slim vertical control panel balancing the oversized left column headline. On smaller breakpoints, the filters stack below the header summary but still maintain generous vertical spacing from it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static by default; can optionally become a sticky sub-bar on scroll, pinning a condensed row of key filters to the top of the viewport while the user scrolls the list, but the base layout assumes it scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shortlist list section begins immediately below the header band, separated by a generous vertical gap (around 48–72px) to preserve the sense of sectional hierarchy. It sits within the same centered max-width container and spans the full internal width of the grid. The list is a vertical stack of shortlist-item-row components, each row running from the left to the right content margin. There is ample vertical padding inside the list area so that the first item doesn’t feel crowded against the header, and similar spacing before the comparison view or pagination at the bottom. Between rows, subtle separators (thin hairlines or slight luminance shifts) help delineate items while retaining the low-density, editorial feel rather than a boxed card grid. The list visually reads as a tall, scrolling column of spacious rows, anchored to the left with consistent left alignment of item titles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrolling section that expands vertically as more items are present; no fixed or floating behavior, but it defines the main scrollable content of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each shortlist item row is a wide, horizontal strip inside the shortlist list that uses the same 12-column grid and a two-part structure reminiscent of the hero layout. The left portion of the row occupies about 7–8/12 of the width and contains the case study title in a bold, medium-large type, along with key metadata (industry, services, tech stack) arranged beneath or beside it with 8–16px vertical spacing. This left content is left-aligned, with generous padding from the row’s top and bottom edges (24–32px) and from the container’s left edge. The right portion of the row, about 4–5/12 of the width, aligns its content to the right edge and vertically centers it relative to the title block. Here, primary actions like 'View case study' are rendered as neon-accented buttons, with secondary actions like 'Remove from shortlist' and a compare checkbox or toggle sitting either to the right or just below, separated by 8–16px gaps. The background of each row remains predominantly black, with subtle hover effects (slight glow or border) to emphasize interactivity. Ample horizontal space between the text block on the left and actions on the right preserves the minimal, premium aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width rows that respond to hover/focus states but do not float or stick; rows simply stack vertically and scroll with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison view is a dedicated band that appears below the shortlist list when at least two items are selected for comparison. It spans the entire width of the centered content container and is separated from the last list item by a substantial vertical margin (48–64px), visually marking it as a distinct section. The band’s background can be slightly differentiated (e.g., a subtle elevation or a very dark, slightly lighter black) to create a panel feel while maintaining the overall dark aesthetic. Inside, a title and brief instruction line sit on the left at the top, following the same left alignment as other headings. Below, a horizontal comparison layout displays selected case studies as evenly spaced columns—e.g., each compared item takes 3–4/12 of the grid width, allowing up to three or four items to appear side by side without feeling cramped. Each column shows key comparison attributes (results, timeline, industry, services) stacked vertically with clear spacing between rows. If more items are selected than fit in one row, horizontal scrolling or a second row is used, but the spacing remains generous. A close or 'clear comparison' control sits aligned to the right of the section header, maintaining the balance of the two-column hero pattern at a smaller scale.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that appears and disappears based on selection state; it scrolls with the page and does not overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned at the very bottom of the shortlist content stack, appearing beneath the comparison view when it is present, or directly under the list otherwise. They are centered horizontally within the max-width container and separated from the content above by a generous vertical gap (32–48px) that allows them to stand alone without visual clutter. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal row of page numbers and arrows, with ample 8–16px spacing between elements for touch-friendly targets and visual clarity. The entire pagination block has a low visual weight—thin neon accents for the active page and hover states—but benefits from the surrounding negative space, making it easy to spot as the natural end of the page. Some extra padding (24–32px) below the controls ensures a comfortable margin before the bottom of the viewport on most screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky element at the end of the main scrolling content; always appears below the list and comparison sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state takes over the main content area beneath the navigation bar when there are no shortlisted items. In this mode, the header summary can remain to introduce the concept of a shortlist, but the list and comparison sections are replaced entirely by a centralized, hero-like empty state block. This block is vertically centered within the remaining viewport space (between the header summary and the bottom of the screen) and horizontally centered within the max-width container. A bold, oversized headline (e.g., 'Your shortlist is empty') sits in the center, aligned either center or left depending on the overall design language, with a brief, softer supporting line below explaining how to add case studies. Spacing between headline and body text uses 16–24px, and an optional primary CTA button (e.g., 'Browse case studies') may appear below with 24–32px of padding. The area around this message is mostly pure black negative space, echoing the original hero’s dramatic simplicity, with no list rows or pagination visible. This makes the empty state feel intentional and on-brand, rather than like a missing piece of UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static centered block that replaces the list and comparison view when active; scroll is minimal or unnecessary if content fits comfortably within the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mutation handler is primarily non-visual, but it can surface transient feedback to the user when shortlist items are added, removed, or updated. When visual, it displays small toast notifications that float above the main content. These toasts appear in the lower-right corner of the viewport, about 24–32px from the right and bottom edges, and stack upward if multiple are shown. Each toast is a compact, dark rectangle with subtle neon accents on the edge or icon, containing one or two short lines of text. The toasts never push content around; instead, they overlay it briefly (a few seconds) and then fade out. When no mutation is occurring, this component has no spatial footprint and does not affect layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating, fixed-position overlay for temporary notifications, anchored to the bottom-right of the viewport; otherwise non-visual with zero layout impact.

name: Blog / Insights
title: Blog / Insights
description: Search, filter, sort, and browse articles and insights, then navigate to full article detail pages.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for articles using getArticleFilterOptions.
Display a searchable, filterable, and sortable list of articles using searchArticles.
Provide a search field that updates the article list based on a keyword query.
Provide a year filter (and additional filters like tags and categories) that refine the article list.
Provide sort controls that reorder the article list according to available sort options.
Support pagination of article results using searchArticles paging information.
Keep key state (query, year, sort, page) synchronized with URL parameters for sharable and revisitable views.
Allow navigation to the article detail page when users click an article title.
user_workflows:
Initial load from navigation:
1. User navigates to blog.html (via header, footer, or a direct link without URL parameters).
2. On page mount, the page calls getArticleFilterOptions to retrieve years, tags, categories, and sort_options.
3. The page reads URL parameters query, year, sort, and page (if present).
4. The page builds a filters object (including year, tags, and categories if already selected in this session) and calls searchArticles with: query (from URL or empty), filters, sort_by (from URL or default from sort_options), page (from URL or default), and a configured page_size.
5. The article list and pagination controls render using the searchArticles response.

Accessing the page with a preset search query (e.g., from the homepage link):
1. User clicks a link such as "View all articles" on the homepage that navigates to blog.html with a query URL parameter.
2. On page mount, the blog page reads the query parameter and populates the search field with this value.
3. The page calls getArticleFilterOptions.
4. The page calls searchArticles with the query from the URL, existing filter values (if any), the sort value from the URL (if any), the page value from the URL (if any), and the configured page_size.
5. The user sees article results already filtered by the provided query and can further adjust filters, sort, and pagination.

Searching for articles from the Blog / Insights page:
1. User types text into the search input of the search form.
2. User submits the form.
3. The search-form component normalizes the text to a query string and updates the URL parameter query to this value; it also resets page to the first page in the URL.
4. The state controller detects the URL parameter change and calls searchArticles with the updated query, current filter selections, current sort, and page set to the first page.
5. The article list updates to show only articles matching the new query.

Filtering by year:
1. User opens the year filter control and selects a specific year from the options loaded by getArticleFilterOptions.
2. The filter component updates the URL parameter year to the selected year and resets page to the first page.
3. The state controller detects the URL change and calls searchArticles with the same query and sort values but with filters updated to include the selected year.
4. The article list updates to show articles published in the selected year only.
5. If the user clears the year selection, the component removes year from the URL, resets page to the first page, and searchArticles is called again with the year filter removed.

Filtering by tags and categories:
1. User selects one or more tags and/or a category from filter controls populated via getArticleFilterOptions.
2. The filter component updates its internal filter state (tags and categories) and triggers a new search without requiring a full page reload.
3. The state controller calls searchArticles with the current query, year (from the URL), updated tags and categories (from the component’s state), current sort (from the URL), and page reset to the first page.
4. The article list updates to show only articles matching the combined filters.
5. If the user deselects tags or categories, the component updates its state, resets the page to the first page, and triggers searchArticles again.

Sorting article results:
1. User opens the sort control and selects a sort option from the list returned by getArticleFilterOptions.sort_options.
2. The sort control updates the URL parameter sort to the selected option’s value and resets page to the first page.
3. The state controller detects the URL change and calls searchArticles with existing query and filters but using the new sort_by value.
4. The article list updates to reflect the new ordering.

Navigating through paginated results:
1. User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page number).
2. The pagination component updates the URL parameter page to the chosen page number.
3. The state controller detects the URL change and calls searchArticles with the current query, filters (year, tags, categories), current sort, and updated page.
4. The article list updates to show the corresponding page of results.

Opening an article detail page:
1. User scans the article list and clicks on an article title.
2. The article-list component reads the corresponding article.slug from the selected item.
3. The browser navigates to article.html with slug set as a URL parameter (e.g., article.html?slug={article.slug}).
4. The article detail page loads and uses the slug parameter to fetch and display full article content (outside the scope of this page).

Using the browser’s back/forward buttons to navigate state:
1. User applies search, filters, sort, and pagination; each action updates URL parameters.
2. User presses the browser’s back or forward button.
3. The URL parameters change accordingly.
4. The state controller listens for URL changes, reconstructs the query, filters, sort, and page from the URL, and calls searchArticles with these values.
5. The page state (search field, filters, sort control, pagination, and article list) updates to match the historical URL state.
interactions: Typing into the search input updates the local query value; submitting the form updates the query and page URL parameters and triggers a new searchArticles call.
Selecting or clearing a year in the year filter updates the year and page URL parameters and triggers a new searchArticles call with the updated year filter.
Selecting or clearing tags updates the internal tag filter state, resets the page to the first page, and triggers a new searchArticles call with updated tag filters.
Selecting or changing a category updates the internal category filter state, resets the page to the first page, and triggers a new searchArticles call with updated category filters.
Selecting a sort option updates the sort and page URL parameters and triggers a new searchArticles call ordered by the selected sort option.
Clicking a pagination control updates the page URL parameter and triggers a new searchArticles call for that page of results.
Clicking an article title navigates to article.html with the article’s slug as a URL parameter.

Reloading the page or directly accessing a URL with query, year, sort, or page parameters re-initializes the UI (search field, filters, sort control, pagination) from the URL and triggers searchArticles to reproduce the same result set.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary state is controlled by the URL parameters query, year, sort, and page, combined with internal filter state for tags and categories. On every load or URL change: 1) The page reads query, year, sort, and page from the URL. If any are absent, it uses empty or default values determined at runtime (e.g., the first sort option from getArticleFilterOptions and the first page). 2) It calls getArticleFilterOptions once per page load (or when a refresh is required) to retrieve available years, tags, categories, and sort options. 3) It builds a filters object for searchArticles that includes: the year from the URL (if present) and any selected tags and categories from internal component state. 4) It calls searchArticles with: query (from URL or empty), filters (constructed as above), sort_by (from URL or from a default derived from getArticleFilterOptions.sort_options), page (from URL or default to the first page), and a configured page_size value. 5) When the user interacts with search, year, sort, or pagination controls, the corresponding URL parameters are updated first; a listener watches for URL changes and uses them to trigger new searchArticles calls, ensuring the UI stays in sync with the URL. Tag and category selections are kept in internal state and passed to searchArticles.filters; if desired, this can be extended to additional URL parameters without changing the core interaction patterns described here.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and synchronizes URL parameters (query, year, sort, page) with calls to getArticleFilterOptions and searchArticles; exposes current articles, filters, sort options, and pagination data to child components.
Provides a text input for article search; initializes its value from the query URL parameter and, on submission, updates the URL query and page parameters to trigger a new searchArticles call.

Displays filters for year, tags, and categories using options from getArticleFilterOptions; synchronizes year selection with the year URL parameter and manages tags and categories as internal filter state. On any filter change, it resets the page to the first page and triggers a new search via the state controller.

Displays available sort options retrieved from getArticleFilterOptions and reflects the currently selected sort as defined by the sort URL parameter. When the user selects a sort option, it updates the sort and page URL parameters and triggers a new searchArticles call via the state controller.

Shows the total number of matching articles and the current range of items displayed based on total_count, page, and page_size from searchArticles. Updates whenever new searchArticles results are loaded.

Renders the list of articles returned by searchArticles, including title, summary (if available), tags, category, estimated read time, and publication date. Clicking an article title navigates to article.html with slug set from the article’s slug field.

Provides controls to navigate between pages of article results using searchArticles.page, searchArticles.page_size, and searchArticles.total_count. Updates the page URL parameter when the user selects a different page and triggers a new searchArticles call via the state controller.
Handles the case when searchArticles returns zero items by showing an appropriate empty state and offering actions such as clearing filters or modifying the search query.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow with clear bands: a full-width hero for the Blog/Insights title and intro, a slim full-width control band for search/state/sort/summary, and then a two-column content band where the left column holds the article list and pagination, and the right column is a vertical filter sidebar. This mirrors the hero pattern (dominant left content, supporting right content) while adapting it to a listing context.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow with clear bands: a full-width hero for the Blog/Insights title and intro, a slim full-width control band for search/state/sort/summary, and then a two-column content band where the left column holds the article list and pagination, and the right column is a vertical filter sidebar. This mirrors the hero pattern (dominant left content, supporting right content) while adapting it to a listing context.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero spans 100% width and roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height, with a centered max-width container (e.g. 1200–1440px) and generous padding (48–64px) from the viewport edges. Within the main content band, the grid behaves like 12 columns: the article list occupies about 7/12 (≈60–65%) of the width on the left, and the filter sidebar about 5/12 (≈35–40%) on the right. Vertical space between sections (hero → control band → results area) stays large (72–96px) to retain the premium, low-density feel; internal spacing is built on 8px increments with 16–32px gaps between stacked elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout stays spacious and low-density, especially in the hero and control band: few elements, large type, and generous negative space. The article list area is denser but still airy, with clear separation between cards. The filter sidebar groups controls vertically with ample breathing room, avoiding cramped sidebars. The control band uses a minimal number of controls, laid out on a single horizontal line (or two loose lines on smaller screens) to avoid a cluttered toolbar feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows an adapted Z-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left hero title, moves to the top-right hero intro, then drops down into the control band where attention is pulled rightwards to the high-contrast search field. From there, the gaze moves down-left into the main article list (the primary content mass), and then rightward to the filter sidebar. Within the results area, the flow is vertical: list items scan from top to bottom, ending at pagination. Filters remain visually accessible but secondary, sitting to the right with lighter visual weight than the main list.

layout.overall_description: The Blog/Insights page opens on a full-width, dark hero section with a pure black background and neon-accent typography. Within a centered max-width container, the left side of the hero is dominated by an oversized, two-line page title like “Insights for Digital Leaders,” set in bold, high-contrast type and occupying roughly the left 60–65% of the width. On the right 35–40%, a shorter, multi-line introductory paragraph explains what kind of content the user can expect, set with comfortable line spacing and floating within generous negative space. Below this hero, a slim but wide control band spans the page: on the left, a compact state toggle and subtle results summary; on the right, a large, high-contrast search field and a smaller sort control aligned with it. This band feels like a functional echo of the top navigation in the original mockup. Beneath that, the main content area splits into two columns: a broad left column containing the vertically stacked article cards and pagination, and a narrower right column that acts as the filter sidebar. The article list column feels like the primary reading area, while the filter sidebar is visually lighter and slightly recessed, with vertically spaced filter blocks. Throughout, ample black negative space around sections and between components preserves a cinematic, editorial tone, while the neon accent colors are reserved for focus states, highlights, and key interactive elements like the search field, active filters, and pagination.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-state-controller appears in the control band directly beneath the hero section, aligned to the left edge of the main content container. It sits on a single horizontal row, positioned just inside the left margin and occupying roughly the first 25–30% of the row’s width. Visually, it behaves like a compact set of pill-style toggles (e.g., “All”, “Featured”, “Case Studies”), laid out horizontally with 16–24px gaps. There is generous padding above and below (24–32px) so the control doesn’t feel cramped, and it aligns vertically with the article-results-summary that sits immediately to its right, forming a cohesive, low-density left control cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control band; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-search-form is the most visually prominent element in the control band. It is positioned on the right side of the same horizontal row as the blog-state-controller, aligned to the right edge of the centered content container. The search field spans approximately 40–50% of the container width, giving it a long, cinematic, command-bar feel. It sits at the same vertical level as the state controller, but because of its size and neon-accent focus state, it visually anchors the right side of the row. There is generous negative space above (32–40px from the hero) and below (24–32px before the results area), ensuring the search form feels important yet not crowded. On very wide screens, it leaves a comfortable gap between itself and the sort control on its left; on narrower screens, the sort control may stack beneath it while still remaining part of the same right-aligned cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the control band; on smaller screens it can expand to nearly full width and stack above the sort control while maintaining right alignment within the container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-filter-bar functions as a vertical sidebar on the right side of the main results area. It begins immediately below the control band, aligned with the right edge of the content container, and occupies roughly 35–40% of the container width, mirroring the narrower right column in the hero. The filter sidebar runs down the page, with filter sections (e.g., categories, tags, date ranges) stacked vertically. Each section is separated by generous vertical spacing (24–32px), with internal controls (checkboxes, chips, etc.) grouped in 8–16px increments. The sidebar’s top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the article-list, making the two columns feel like a cohesive grid. The background remains the same pure black as the page, while subtle outlines or glowing divider lines and neon accents on active filters distinguish it from the article list without adding heavy visual blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within its column: once the user scrolls past the control band, the filter-sidebar’s top edge pins to a small offset below the viewport top (e.g., 24–32px), so filters remain visible while the article list scrolls alongside.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-sort-control lives in the control band on the right side, visually grouped with the search form but given lower emphasis. It is positioned immediately to the left of the search field or directly beneath it on narrower screens. On desktop, it might occupy about 15–20% of the row width, appearing as a compact dropdown or segmented control for sorting (e.g., “Newest”, “Popular”). It aligns vertically with the search field, forming a tidy right-aligned cluster.
