There is a modest horizontal gap (16–24px) between sort and search to maintain clarity and avoid a crowded toolbar look. The control uses smaller typography and less contrast than the search field, ensuring it reads as a secondary refinement rather than the primary interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control band; on smaller screens it stacks beneath the search form but retains right alignment and comfortable vertical spacing (~16–24px).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-results-summary appears in the control band on the left side, adjacent to the blog-state-controller. It sits either directly to the right of the state toggles on the same horizontal line or immediately beneath them with a slight vertical offset, depending on available width. On desktop, it occupies roughly 20–25% of the content width and is aligned left, with its baseline matching the controls on the right for a clean visual rhythm. The summary is a single, concise line (e.g., “24 insights, filtered by Fintech”), styled in smaller, subtle typography with muted color, ensuring it does not compete with the bold hero text or the search field. There is at least 16px of horizontal separation from the state controller to keep the text legible and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control band, scrolling with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-list dominates the left column of the results area below the control band, occupying roughly 60–65% of the container width and running vertically down the page. It begins at the same vertical position as the article-filter-bar on the right, preserving a clean top alignment across the two columns. Articles are presented as vertically stacked cards or rows, each spanning the full width of the left column with generous vertical separation (32–48px) to maintain the low-density, editorial feel. Each card uses strong left alignment, bold titles, and consistent spacing around metadata and excerpts, leaving generous black negative space on the right of each card before the filter-sidebar begins. The list extends downward as the primary scrollable content, with the filter-sidebar visible alongside on larger screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it scrolls normally while the filter-sidebar may remain sticky on the right. On smaller screens where columns collapse, the article-list stacks above the filter-bar, still taking full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-pagination-controls sit directly beneath the article-list, aligned with the left column and respecting the same left margin as the article cards. There is a substantial vertical gap (32–48px) between the last article card and the pagination, allowing the controls to breathe and feel intentional rather than tacked on. The controls occupy roughly 40–50% of the left column’s width and can be arranged as a row of page numbers with previous/next buttons. They are primarily left-aligned to echo the strong left anchoring of the headline and list, but with enough horizontal spacing between elements (12–16px) to maintain clarity. Contrast and accent colors are used sparingly here, reserved for the current page indicator and hover/focus states, so pagination is easy to use but does not overpower the content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the end of the article list; it scrolls out of view as the user moves back up the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results-state replaces the article-list and pagination-controls in the left column whenever filters or search yield no matches. It is positioned in the same space where the first article card would normally appear, starting at the top of the left column beneath the control band. The empty state content is centered within the left column (not the entire viewport), with ample vertical padding (48–72px) above and below to create a calm, intentional moment. It may include a concise headline, short explanation, and a subtle call-to-action (e.g., “Clear filters”), all stacked with 16–24px spacing. The right-side article-filter-bar remains visible and unchanged, emphasizing that adjusting filters or search can resolve the state. On wide screens, the empty state’s center line still aligns visually with the filter sidebar to maintain the two-column structure; on small screens, when columns stack, the empty state appears above the filter controls, occupying full width but maintaining generous negative space around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual replacement for the article list within the left column; it scrolls normally and does not affect the filter-sidebar’s sticky behavior on the right.

name: Article Detail
title: Article
description: Displays a single article based on a slug URL parameter, including full content, metadata, reading list status, and related articles, with controls to add or remove the article from the user's reading list.

core_features: On initial load or when the slug URL parameter changes, call getArticleDetail with the current slug to retrieve article, is_in_reading_list, and related_articles, and bind this data to the page state.
Render full article metadata, including title, publication date, estimated read time, and any tags or category returned in the Article entity.
Render the full article content field, preserving its internal structure (headings, paragraphs, images, and other rich content as provided by the backend).
Display a reading list toggle control whose label and state are determined by is_in_reading_list, and use addArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList to update the user's reading list.
Display related_articles returned from getArticleDetail as a navigable list, each item linking to article.html with the appropriate slug in the URL parameters.
Provide navigation back to the main article listing page (blog.html) and to the user's reading list page (reading_list.html) without altering data, using standard link navigation.
Handle error states when slug is missing or when getArticleDetail fails, by showing an appropriate error message and offering navigation back to the blog listing.
user_workflows: Workflow: Read an article from the blog listing. 1) User clicks an article link on blog.html or index.html that navigates to article.html with ?slug={articleSlug}. 2) Article Detail page parses slug from the URL and calls getArticleDetail(slug). 3) On a successful response, the page displays the article metadata, full content, tags, and related_articles. 4) User scrolls through the article, optionally clicking tag links or related article links to navigate. 5) If needed, user clicks the back-to-articles navigation link to return to blog.html.

Workflow: Save article to reading list. 1) While viewing article.html, user sees the reading list toggle control whose state reflects is_in_reading_list from getArticleDetail. 2) If the article is not in the reading list, the control appears as an action to save the article. 3) User clicks the control, triggering addArticleToReadingList with article.id from the current Article. 4) On success, the component updates local reading list state to reflect that the article is now saved (is_in_reading_list becomes true) and optionally surfaces the message returned by addArticleToReadingList. 5) User may then use the header navigation link to go to reading_list.html, where the article will be visible in their reading list.

Workflow: Remove article from reading list. 1) While viewing article.html for an article already in the reading list, the toggle control indicates that the article is saved. 2) User clicks the control to remove it from the reading list. 3) This triggers removeArticleFromReadingList with the current article.id. 4) On a successful response, the component updates local reading list state so that is_in_reading_list becomes false and the control reverts to the unsaved state. 5) If the user later navigates to reading_list.html, this article will no longer be listed.

Workflow: Open a related article. 1) At the bottom or side of the article, the user sees a list of related_articles provided by getArticleDetail. 2) Each related article item links to article.html with the corresponding slug URL parameter. 3) When the user clicks one of these items, the browser navigates to the new URL (article.html?slug={relatedSlug}). 4) The page detects the slug change, calls getArticleDetail again with the new slug, and replaces all article and reading list state with the new response. 5) The user continues reading the new article with updated content, tags, and reading list toggle state.
interactions: On page load or when the slug URL parameter changes, the page triggers getArticleDetail using the slug value; the response populates article content, tags, estimated_read_time_minutes, published_at, is_in_reading_list, and related_articles.
Scrolling interaction allows the user to read the full article content; no additional API calls are made on scroll.

Clicking the reading list toggle when the article is not saved triggers addArticleToReadingList with the current Article.id; while waiting for a response, the component may show a loading state for the button and, on success, updates the in-memory is_in_reading_list flag.

Clicking the reading list toggle when the article is already saved triggers removeArticleFromReadingList with the current Article.id; on success, the button state changes to indicate the article is no longer saved.

Clicking a related article item navigates the user to article.html with a new slug URL parameter, which causes the page to call getArticleDetail for that slug and refresh the displayed article and reading list status.
Clicking the Back to all articles link navigates directly to blog.html without calling any interfaces on this page.
Clicking the Reading list link or icon in the header navigates to reading_list.html without calling any interfaces on this page.

If getArticleDetail fails (for example, due to an invalid slug), the page displays an error message and offers navigation back to blog.html; no addArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList calls are made in this state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of URL-driven state is the slug query parameter (article.html?slug={value}). When the page initializes, it reads slug from the URL. If slug is present, the page calls getArticleDetail(slug) and stores the resulting article, is_in_reading_list, and related_articles in in-memory state bound to the components. If slug is missing or empty, the page enters an error state and does not call getArticleDetail, instead prompting the user to navigate back to blog.html. When navigation occurs to another article via related articles, the browser URL changes to a new slug; the page detects this change (for example via a route or URL-change listener), clears existing article-specific state, and calls getArticleDetail again with the new slug. The is_in_reading_list field is initialized only from getArticleDetail and is then kept in sync with the addArticleToReadingList and removeArticleFromReadingList responses. These two interfaces are called only with article_id from the currently loaded Article; they do not change the URL. The page does not use localStorage or any other persistent client-side storage; all authoritative reading list membership is managed by the backend via the provided interfaces and the result of getArticleDetail for the current slug.

components.functionality: Initializes and refreshes the page's article data by reading the slug URL parameter and calling getArticleDetail, then exposes article, is_in_reading_list, and related_articles to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read slug from the URL; if present, call getArticleDetail(slug); on success, store article, is_in_reading_list, and related_articles in the page state; on error, transition to an error state.

onUrlChange: When the slug parameter changes (for example, user navigates to another article.html?slug=...), clear current article-related state, call getArticleDetail with the new slug, and update the bound state fields.

onDataRefreshRequest: Optional handler that can be triggered after addArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList if a full refresh is needed; calls getArticleDetail(slug) again to re-sync state.

components.functionality: Displays the article's basic metadata such as title, publication date, estimated reading time, category, and tags, using the Article entity provided by the data loader.
event_handlers: onDataBind: When article data becomes available or changes, render the title, published_at, estimated_read_time_minutes, category, and tags.

onTagClick: When a tag is clicked, navigate to blog.html (or another configured listing page) with a query parameter reflecting the selected tag, without calling getArticleDetail; this interaction leaves the current page if implemented, or can be handled by the parent router.

components.functionality: Renders the full article.content field as readable body content, preserving its structure, and enables standard reading interactions such as scrolling.
event_handlers: onDataBind: When article content is available, render article.content into the content area.

onInternalLinkClick: If the article content includes internal links to other articles (for example links encoded with slugs), navigate the browser to the corresponding URLs, which will trigger the data loader via the new slug.
onScroll: Optional handler used only for UI behaviors such as sticky headers; it does not trigger any of the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Shows and controls the article's membership in the user's reading list by reflecting is_in_reading_list and calling addArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList as needed.
event_handlers: onDataBind: When article and is_in_reading_list are available from getArticleDetail, configure the button label and state (saved vs not saved).

onClickAdd: If is_in_reading_list is false and article.id is available, call addArticleToReadingList with article_id = article.id; while the request is in flight, mark the button as loading; on success, update local is_in_reading_list to true and optionally show the success message from the response; on failure, keep is_in_reading_list as false and show an error indication.

onClickRemove: If is_in_reading_list is true and article.id is available, call removeArticleFromReadingList with article_id = article.id; while the request is in flight, mark the button as loading; on success, update local is_in_reading_list to false; on failure, keep is_in_reading_list as true and show an error indication.

components.functionality: Displays related_articles returned by getArticleDetail and allows the user to navigate to another article by clicking an item, which updates the slug in the URL.
event_handlers: onDataBind: When related_articles are available from getArticleDetail, render a list where each item shows key metadata such as title and estimated_read_time_minutes.

onRelatedArticleClick: When a related article item is clicked, navigate the browser to article.html with slug set to the related article's slug; this URL change triggers article-detail-loader to call getArticleDetail for the new article.

components.functionality: Provides a direct link for the user to return to the main blog article listing page.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to blog.html as defined in the outgoing_connections configuration; no API interfaces are called by this component.

components.functionality: Provides access to the user's reading list page via a link or icon in the shared site header.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to reading_list.html as defined in the outgoing_connections; no API interfaces are called by this component on this page.

components.functionality: Handles and displays error conditions for the Article Detail page, such as missing slug or failed getArticleDetail responses.
event_handlers: onMissingSlug: When the page initializes and no slug parameter is found in the URL, display an error message explaining that the article cannot be found and show navigation options (for example a button linking back to blog.html).
onLoadError: When getArticleDetail returns an error, show an error message and provide a link or button back to blog.html; disable the reading list toggle and related-articles-list in this state.
onRetryClick: Optionally, if the slug is present but the request failed, allow the user to retry by calling getArticleDetail(slug) again via article-detail-loader.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s structure: a full-width dark canvas with a centered max-width content band. At the top sits the persistent header bar (logo + primary actions, including the header-reading-list-link). Below that, the article uses a hero-like header section in a two-column layout (60–65% left, 35–40% right), followed by a taller content area where the left column holds the article body and the right column becomes the related-articles-list side rail.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s structure: a full-width dark canvas with a centered max-width content band. At the top sits the persistent header bar (logo + primary actions, including the header-reading-list-link). Below that, the article uses a hero-like header section in a two-column layout (60–65% left, 35–40% right), followed by a taller content area where the left column holds the article body and the right column becomes the related-articles-list side rail.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header bar occupies a slim band at the top (~10–12% of viewport height). The article hero header claims the upper portion of the remaining viewport (roughly 40–50% of viewport height on first load) with very generous padding (64–96px from top of content band and 32–64px internal spacing). The article body and related list below extend vertically with large section margins (72–120px), preserving a lot of negative space around the primary text columns.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area (article-header + reading-list-toggle-button + metadata) is intentionally sparse, dominated by an oversized article title and a small amount of metadata. The article body is denser but constrained to a narrower reading column with comfortable line-height and margins, while the right column remains relatively light with a concise, vertically spaced related-articles-list. Overall, the density stays low to medium, never crowding the dark background.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The reading flow echoes the mockup’s modified Z-pattern: the eye lands first on the top-left logo, then moves to the top-right header-reading-list-link and global actions. From there, attention drops down to the large article title on the left, then shifts right to the supporting metadata and reading-list-toggle-button. As the user scrolls, the focus flows down the left article-content-body, with occasional lateral shifts to the right column’s related-articles-list for further navigation.

layout.overall_description: The article detail page sits on a pure black background with a centered, max-width content band that runs the full width of the hero and body sections. A slim, persistent top navigation bar anchors the page, with the logo on the left and key navigation (including the header-reading-list-link) on the right, echoing the original hero’s header. Beneath this, a spacious hero-like article header fills most of the initial viewport height, split into two unequal columns: the left 7/12 column presents a bold, oversized article title and key metadata stacked vertically, while the right 5/12 column holds a compact cluster of supporting metadata and the reading-list-toggle-button, separated by generous vertical spacing. A subtle back-to-articles-link sits above the title on the left, aligned to the content grid, giving an understated but clear way out. As the user scrolls, the layout transitions into a long-form reading area that preserves the same grid: the article-content-body runs down the page in the left 7/12 column with a controlled line length, while the right 5/12 column becomes a vertically stacked related-articles-list that aligns to the top of the content and can remain visible for much of the scroll. Large strips of black negative space frame the text columns on both sides and between sections, maintaining the cinematic, minimalist feel. Loading and error states occupy the central content area, replacing the hero/body content while preserving the overall structure and alignment within the max-width band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as an overlay within the main content area, starting just below the global top navigation bar and extending down to cover both the hero header and the article body region. It spans the same max-width content band as the article, centered horizontally on the page with 32–48px of dark margin separating it from the viewport edges. The loader’s primary visual (spinner, skeleton, or loading indicator) is centered both horizontally and vertically within this content band, roughly in the middle of where the article title and first paragraphs would appear, reinforcing the sense that the page is preparing the main hero content. The surrounding area remains mostly empty black, keeping the density low and preserving the dramatic, minimalist feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the article-header, article-content-body, reading-list-toggle-button, and related-articles-list areas while data is loading, without obscuring the global top navigation bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header occupies the top section of the main content band beneath the global navigation, styled as a hero. It is contained within a centered max-width container (e.g., 1120–1280px) and uses a two-column layout aligned to a 12-column grid: the left column spans about 7/12 of the width, and the right spans about 5/12. The top of the article header sits approximately 96px below the global header bar, creating a generous gap of negative space. In the left column, the article title is set as a massive, two- to three-line H1 that dominates the page, with any subtitle or key metadata (author, date, read time) stacked beneath it with 16–24px vertical spacing. This block is left-aligned and runs from near the top of the hero section down toward its middle. In the right column, beginning roughly aligned with the vertical center of the title block, additional metadata and status information can be stacked vertically, but with ample gaps (24–32px) between items to keep the area visually light. The overall header height can range from 40–60% of the viewport, depending on title length, with large black margins above and below to emphasize the hero feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the normal document flow, anchoring the top of the article content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article content body begins directly beneath the article header, sharing the same centered max-width container and grid alignment. It is visually separated from the header by a generous vertical gap of roughly 64–80px. On desktop, the main text column is aligned with the left 7/12 grid used in the hero, but the actual text line width is slightly narrower (around 5–6 of those 7 columns) to maintain an optimal reading measure; this creates a subtle margin of black space on the right side of the text within the left column. The content is left-aligned and extends vertically down the page, with consistent 24–32px spacing between paragraphs, images, and subheadings. The right 5/12 of the grid is reserved for the related-articles-list, allowing the user’s primary reading path to stay on the left while glancing right for navigation options. The bottom of the article body is cushioned by a large margin (80–120px) before the page footer, keeping the layout airy and premium.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-scrolling behavior, forming the main vertical reading column; aligns to the left portion of the persistent 12-column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-toggle-button is positioned within the hero-like article header section, in the right 5/12 column, near the top of that column’s content stack. Horizontally, it is right-aligned to the inner edge of the max-width container, creating a subtle tension with the left-aligned title. Vertically, it sits either at the top or slightly below the top of the right column’s metadata block, with about 16–24px spacing from any labels or descriptions above or below it. The button is visually prominent via color and size but occupies a compact footprint (e.g., 40–48px tall, 120–200px wide), maintaining the mockup’s minimalist density. On smaller screens, when columns collapse, this button moves below the title and primary metadata, centered or left-aligned, with about 24–32px of spacing beneath the headline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero header’s right column; scrolls away with the header as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-list occupies the right-hand column of the main content area, starting just below the article header and visually aligned with the top of the first paragraphs of the article-content-body. It sits within the same max-width container, spanning the right 5/12 of the 12-column grid, leaving a clear vertical gutter between it and the article text. This list is a vertically stacked set of related article items, each taking the full width of the right column, with 24–32px vertical spacing between items to keep the column light and scannable. The entire related-articles-list column is shorter and less dense than the article body, leaving intentional black space at the bottom once the user scrolls past the list. On wide screens, the list visually reads as a side rail to the main text; on narrow screens, it drops below the article-content-body with a large separator gap of 64–80px to clearly mark the transition from primary content to secondary navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static on narrow viewports; on larger viewports it may adopt a sticky behavior within its column, remaining visible near the top-right of the viewport while the article-content-body scrolls, as long as it does not collide with the footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-articles-link is placed at the top-left of the article content band, directly beneath the global navigation bar and above the article header. It aligns with the left edge of the article title (the left 7/12 column start) and sits with a comfortable margin of about 48–64px below the bottom of the header bar, creating a small pre-header zone. The link itself is compact and understated, occupying only as much width as its text requires, and is separated from the article title by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. This positioning makes it easy to spot when arriving on the page without competing with the dominance of the H1. On smaller screens, it remains the first element within the article area, still above the title, preserving the same relative structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element that scrolls away with the page; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-reading-list-link resides in the global top navigation bar, on the right side, grouped with other primary navigation actions. It is right-aligned within the header’s max-width container, typically appearing as a text link or compact icon+label element with 16–24px horizontal spacing from neighboring actions. Vertically, it is centered within the header bar’s height, which is relatively slim (56–72px), keeping the bar minimal and unobtrusive. The dark background of the header matches the page, while the link likely uses the brand’s accent color to echo the magenta–cyan neon palette and subtly connect to the reading-list-toggle-button below in the hero. This placement ensures the link is always visible regardless of scroll position.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the persistent top navigation bar, always visible at the top of the viewport as the user scrolls the article.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-error-state replaces the normal article content area when an error occurs. It is centered within the same max-width content band that would hold the article header and body, starting beneath the global navigation bar. The error message block is horizontally centered and constrained to roughly 4–6 columns in width within the 12-column grid, creating a compact, easily readable panel surrounded by generous black space. Vertically, it is aligned around the center of the viewport (leaving at least 96px of margin beneath the header bar), so the message appears in the natural focal area where the article title would have been. Supporting actions (like a retry or back link) are stacked below the main message with 16–24px spacing. No article-header, article-content-body, reading-list-toggle-button, or related-articles-list are shown when this state is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that occupies the space where the article would normally render, fully replacing the article content region when visible.

name: Reading List
title: Reading List
description: Page for viewing and managing the user’s saved articles, with options to open articles and remove them from the reading list.

core_features: Load the current user's reading list items using getReadingListItems and display associated article details.
Allow users to open any saved article by clicking its title, navigating to article.html with the article slug as a URL parameter.
Allow users to remove individual articles from the reading list using removeArticleFromReadingList.
Support optional filtering and sorting of reading list items based on article attributes (e.g., tags, category, date added, estimated reading time), controlled via URL parameters.
Support pagination of the reading list based on URL parameters (page and per_page), applied to the loaded items.
Handle empty states (no items at all, or no items matching current filters) with clear messaging.
user_workflows:
View reading list:
1. User opens reading_list.html (via header navigation or direct URL).
2. On page initialization, the page controller calls getReadingListItems.
3. The response provides the ReadingList container and an array of items, each with ReadingListItem + Article.
4. The controller reads URL parameters (e.g., sort, filter_tag, filter_category, min_read_time, max_read_time, page, per_page).
5. The controller applies the filter and sort logic to the items in memory.
6. The controller derives pagination from the filtered list and current page/per_page values.
7. The current page of items is passed to the list component for display.
8. If no items are available or none match filters, the empty-state component is shown.

Open an article from the reading list:
1. User scans the list of saved articles.
2. User clicks on an article title in a list item row.
3. The click handler constructs a URL: article.html?slug={article.slug}.
4. Browser navigates to this URL; no change to server-side reading list is made by this action.
5. If the user navigates back to reading_list.html, the page reinitializes, re-calls getReadingListItems if needed, and restores the list based on URL parameters.

Remove a single article from the reading list:
1. User identifies an article in the list to remove.
2. User clicks the remove action for that article.
3. A confirmation step (inline or dialog) is shown, referencing the selected article.
4. On confirmation, the controller calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the article_id from the associated Article entity.
5. On success (success=true), the controller either:
   - Removes the corresponding item from the local items collection and re-applies sorting/filtering/pagination, or
   - Re-calls getReadingListItems to synchronize the list.
6. The list is re-rendered without the removed article; if no items remain on the current page, pagination may shift to the previous page according to URL state.
7. On failure (success=false), an error message from the response is displayed in a non-blocking way.

Filter and sort the reading list:
1. User adjusts controls in the toolbar (e.g., chooses a sort option or selects a tag/category filter).
2. The toolbar component updates the browser URL query string (e.g., sort, filter_tag, filter_category, min_read_time, max_read_time) via navigation or history APIs.
3. The page controller monitors URL changes (or handles the change event) and re-reads the updated parameters.
4. The controller re-applies filtering and sorting to the in-memory items previously loaded via getReadingListItems.
5. The first page of the filtered/sorted results is displayed (page parameter may be reset to 1 when filters change).
6. The empty-state component is shown if no items match the current filter criteria.

Paginate through reading list items:
1. User sees pagination controls indicating current page and total pages derived from the filtered list.
2. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next, previous, specific page index).
3. The pagination component updates the page parameter (and optionally per_page) in the URL.
4. The page controller detects the URL change, recalculates which subset of filtered items belongs to the new page, and passes them to the list component.
5. The list component re-renders to show items for the selected page.

Bulk removal of multiple articles (optional behavior using the existing interface repeatedly):
1. User selects multiple list items via selection controls on each row.
2. Bulk actions bar becomes active when at least one item is selected.
3. User activates a bulk remove action.
4. A confirmation message lists the number of articles to be removed.
5. On confirmation, the controller iterates over the selected article_ids and calls removeArticleFromReadingList for each one.
6. After all calls complete (or as each call succeeds), the controller updates the local items collection or re-calls getReadingListItems.
7. Filters, sorting, and pagination are re-applied, and the list view updates accordingly.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Trigger: Page load of reading_list.html.
- Behavior: Invoke getReadingListItems; parse URL parameters for sort/filter/pagination; compute and render the current list view.
- State: Stores ReadingList metadata and the full items array in page-level state.

Click article title:
- Trigger: User clicks an article title in a list item.
- Behavior: Navigate to article.html?slug={article.slug}.
- State: No mutation to reading list; URL of reading_list.html may remain in browser history for back navigation.

Click remove on a list item:
- Trigger: User clicks the remove icon/button for a specific saved article.
- Behavior: Show confirmation; on confirmation, call removeArticleFromReadingList(article_id); on success, update the in-memory items or re-fetch via getReadingListItems.
- State: ReadingListItem for that article is removed from local state; URL parameters remain unchanged unless pagination needs adjustment (page index may be updated if the current page becomes empty).

Change sort option:
- Trigger: User selects a new sort option (e.g., by date added, by article published date, by estimated read time).
- Behavior: Toolbar updates the sort parameter in the URL query string (e.g., sort=added_desc or sort=read_time_asc) and can reset page=1.
- State: Page controller re-applies sorting to the current items and re-renders the first page of sorted results.

Change filter option:
- Trigger: User selects or clears filters, such as a specific tag, category, or read-time range.
- Behavior: Toolbar updates filter-related parameters in the URL (e.g., filter_tag, filter_category, min_read_time, max_read_time) and resets page=1.
- State: Page controller filters the in-memory items according to the filter parameters and re-computes pagination.

Change page via pagination controls:
- Trigger: User clicks next/previous or a specific page number.
- Behavior: Pagination component updates page (and optionally per_page) in the URL query string.
- State: Page controller reads the new page value, computes the correct subset of filtered items, and passes it to the list component for rendering.

Toggle selection for bulk actions:
- Trigger: User toggles selection for individual items, or uses a "select all on page" control.
- Behavior: Selection state is stored in page-level state (not in URL, to keep URLs focused on display state rather than transient interaction state).
- State: Bulk-actions component is shown or hidden depending on whether any items are selected.

Bulk remove confirmation:
- Trigger: User clicks a bulk-remove button when multiple items are selected.
- Behavior: Confirmation UI summarises the action; on confirm, controller calls removeArticleFromReadingList for each selected article_id; on completion, list state is updated.
- State: Selected items are removed from the local items list; selection state is reset.

URL parameter change (via back/forward navigation or manual edit):
- Trigger: User uses browser back/forward buttons or manually modifies the URL query string.
- Behavior: Page controller listens for URL changes and re-applies sort, filter, and pagination rules to the already loaded items (re-fetch via getReadingListItems if deemed necessary for consistency).
- State: Displayed items and pagination update to match the URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Reading List page uses URL query parameters to control how the loaded items are presented. The underlying ReadingList and ReadingListItems are always obtained via getReadingListItems; URL parameters never directly influence what the server returns, but they determine client-side state and views.

Supported URL parameters (functional description):
- sort: Controls sorting order of items after retrieval. Example values (implementation-defined, but consistent across the app):
  - sort=added_desc: Sort by ReadingListItem.added_at descending (most recently saved first).
  - sort=added_asc: Sort by ReadingListItem.added_at ascending.
  - sort=published_desc: Sort by Article.published_at descending.
  - sort=read_time_asc: Sort by Article.estimated_read_time_minutes ascending.
  If sort is missing or invalid, a default sort order is used (defined by the implementation but not hardcoded here).

- filter_tag: Optional filter to only display items where the Article.tags collection contains the specified tag. If missing, no tag-based filtering is applied.
- filter_category: Optional filter on Article.category. If missing, no category-based filtering is applied.
- min_read_time and max_read_time: Optional numeric constraints on Article.estimated_read_time_minutes. When present, only items whose estimated_read_time_minutes fall within the specified inclusive range are displayed. Either parameter can be used alone.

- page: 1-based index for which subset of the filtered items to display. If page is missing or invalid, it defaults to the first page. If the current page becomes out of range because the number of available items decreased (for example, after removing items or tightening filters), the controller adjusts the effective page index to the highest valid page.
- per_page: Number of items displayed per page. If missing, an implementation-defined default value is used. If provided and valid, it determines how many filtered items are shown on each page. Changing per_page via the URL resets the effective page to the first page.

Flow of state logic:
1. On page load or URL change, parse all relevant query parameters.
2. Call getReadingListItems (initially, and optionally on subsequent changes if a full refresh is needed) to obtain the ReadingList and complete items array.
3. Apply filters (filter_tag, filter_category, min_read_time, max_read_time) to derive the filtered_items array.
4. Apply sorting rules based on sort to filtered_items, resulting in sorted_items.
5. Derive pagination metadata using page and per_page, slicing sorted_items to get the subset for the current view.
6. Pass the paginated subset and metadata (total items count, total pages, current page) to list and pagination components.
7. URL parameters are considered the sole source of truth for view state (sort, filter, pagination). UI components update these parameters in response to user actions rather than maintaining separate internal copies of this state.
8. Transient UI states (e.g., which items are selected for bulk actions, whether confirmation dialogs are open) are stored only in in-memory page state and are not reflected in the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL-based state, filtering, sorting, pagination, and mutation of the reading list. Acts as the central orchestrator for child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when reading_list.html loads; invokes getReadingListItems, parses URL parameters, and computes the initial view model (filtered, sorted, and paginated items).

onUrlChange: Called when the URL query string changes (via user interaction or browser navigation); re-parses parameters, re-applies filters and sorting, and recomputes pagination based on the already loaded items (and optionally re-calls getReadingListItems for freshness).

onRemoveArticleRequested: Receives an article_id from a child component; triggers the confirmation flow. On confirmation, calls removeArticleFromReadingList(article_id) and, on success, updates local items or re-fetches via getReadingListItems.

onBulkRemoveRequested: Receives a list of article_ids from the bulk-actions component; confirms the action and iteratively calls removeArticleFromReadingList for each id. After completion, updates local state or re-fetches via getReadingListItems, then clears selection state.
onItemsUpdated: Internal handler used after any mutation (single or bulk removal) to re-apply current URL-based filters, sorting, and pagination to the updated items collection.

components.functionality: Provides controls to adjust sorting and filtering of reading list items; updates URL parameters to drive view state.
event_handlers: onSortChange: Triggered when the user selects a different sort option. Updates the sort parameter (and resets page) in the URL, causing the page-controller onUrlChange handler to recompute the view.
onFilterTagChange: Triggered when the user selects or clears a tag filter. Updates the filter_tag parameter (and resets page) in the URL.
onFilterCategoryChange: Triggered when the user selects or clears a category filter. Updates the filter_category parameter (and resets page) in the URL.
onReadTimeRangeChange: Triggered when the user adjusts minimum or maximum estimated read time constraints. Updates min_read_time and/or max_read_time parameters in the URL and resets page.
onClearFilters: Clears all filter-related parameters (filter_tag, filter_category, min_read_time, max_read_time) from the URL and resets page and sort to their defaults via URL updates.

components.functionality: Displays the current page of reading list items and delegates item-specific interactions to row components.
event_handlers: onRenderItems: Called when the page-controller provides the paginated subset of items; renders a list of reading-list-item-row instances.
onItemTitleClick: Bubbles up from child rows when an article title is clicked; handled within this component by navigating to article.html?slug={article.slug}.
onItemRemoveClick: Bubbles up from child rows when the remove action is clicked; forwards the associated article_id to the page-controller via onRemoveArticleRequested.

onItemSelectionChange: Bubbles up when a row's selection state changes; updates the selection collection maintained by the page-controller or a dedicated selection state, and notifies the bulk-actions component.

components.functionality: Represents a single reading list entry, showing core article details and providing actions for opening the article and removing it.
event_handlers: onTitleClick: When the title area is clicked, emits an event with the associated Article.slug to the parent list component, which performs the navigation to article.html?slug={slug}.
onRemoveClick: When the remove action is activated, emits an event containing the associated Article.id (article_id) to the parent list component, which delegates removal to the page-controller.
onSelectionToggle: When the row's selection control is toggled, emits the item id and new selection state to the list component so that it can manage bulk selections.

components.functionality: Allows navigation between different pages of the filtered and sorted reading list.
event_handlers: onPageChange: Triggered when the user selects a different page. Updates the page parameter in the URL query string.
onPerPageChange: Optional; triggered when the user changes how many items they want to see per page. Updates the per_page parameter and resets page to the first page in the URL.

components.functionality: Shows a clear message when there are no items to display, either because the reading list is empty or because no items match the current filters.
event_handlers: onCheckEmpty: Triggered whenever the page-controller recomputes the items array; if the total count is zero or the count of filtered items for the current filter set is zero, this component is displayed instead of the list.

onClearFiltersFromEmpty: Optional; if filters are causing an empty result, this handler allows the user to clear filters by delegating to the toolbar's onClearFilters behavior (which updates the URL).

components.functionality: Provides actions that apply to multiple selected reading list items at once, such as bulk removal.
event_handlers: onSelectionUpdated: Called whenever the overall selection set changes (from item-row interactions) so the component can display the count of selected items and enable/disable bulk actions.
onBulkRemoveClick: Triggered when the user initiates a bulk removal; emits the list of selected article_ids to the page-controller's onBulkRemoveRequested handler.

components.functionality: Confirms single or bulk removal actions before calling removeArticleFromReadingList via the page-controller.
event_handlers: onOpenForSingle: Opens the confirmation dialog with the context of a single article (identified by article_id).
onOpenForBulk: Opens the dialog summarizing multiple selected articles for bulk removal (identified by a list of article_ids).
onConfirm: When user confirms, delegates to the page-controller to execute removeArticleFromReadingList for the relevant article_id(s).
onCancel: Closes the dialog without making any API calls or changing the reading list.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The Reading List page follows the mockup’s hero-first, linear vertical flow. At the very top, the page-controller forms a bold, dark hero band for the Reading List title and contextual info, using a two-column layout within a centered max-width container (around a 7/12 left and 5/12 right split). Directly below this, the filter-sort toolbar spans the content width as a slim horizontal control band. Under the toolbar sits the main content area: either a vertical stack of reading-list-item-row components inside the reading-list-items-list container, or a centered empty state when there are no items. Pagination controls anchor the bottom of the content stack. Bulk actions appear as a floating bottom bar above content when needed, and the remove confirmation appears as a centered modal overlay above everything.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The Reading List page follows the mockup’s hero-first, linear vertical flow. At the very top, the page-controller forms a bold, dark hero band for the Reading List title and contextual info, using a two-column layout within a centered max-width container (around a 7/12 left and 5/12 right split). Directly below this, the filter-sort toolbar spans the content width as a slim horizontal control band. Under the toolbar sits the main content area: either a vertical stack of reading-list-item-row components inside the reading-list-items-list container, or a centered empty state when there are no items. Pagination controls anchor the bottom of the content stack. Bulk actions appear as a floating bottom bar above content when needed, and the remove confirmation appears as a centered modal overlay above everything.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses a full-width, pure black background with a centered max-width content band (approx 1120–1280px wide). Vertically, about 20–25% of the initial viewport height is used by the hero-style page-controller, 10–15% by the toolbar plus upper breathing room, and the remainder by the list or empty state plus pagination. Horizontally, layouts inside the content band mostly follow a 12-column grid with a dominant left column (around 60–65% width) and a narrower right side (35–40%) for secondary information or controls. Large vertical spacing (48–96px) separates major sections, while internal spacing between elements sticks to 16–32px increments to keep the minimalist yet dramatic feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page remains spacious and low-density, focusing on legibility and a premium, editorial look rather than cramming information. The hero heading is oversized with plenty of surrounding negative space. The toolbar is compact but not cluttered, with clear separation between filter and sort controls. Each reading-list-item-row has generous vertical padding and controlled line lengths, showing only key metadata and actions. The number of items per page is modest, with ample breathing room between rows and a large buffer above the pagination to avoid visual crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s modified Z-pattern. The eye starts top-left on the page title in the hero band, glances to the top-right hero-side details (such as saved count or short explanation), then drops down to the toolbar directly below. From there, the flow moves down the left-dominant stack of reading list items, with each row read left-to-right: article information on the left, key metadata and actions on the right. Bulk actions at the bottom of the viewport draw attention when active through contrast and neon accent color. When triggered, the confirmation dialog becomes the focal point in the center of the screen, temporarily overriding all other flows.

layout.overall_description: The Reading List page sits on a pure black canvas with a centered, wide content band that feels like a cinematic strip across the screen. At the top of this band, a hero-style header announces the Reading List with bold, oversized typography on the left and a concise description plus saved-article stats on the right, echoing the original two-column hero layout. Below, a slim but clearly defined toolbar runs horizontally, aligning with the same left and right edges, offering filtering and sorting without overwhelming the space. The main list area forms a clean vertical column of generously spaced rows, each row subtly lit against the dark background and internally structured with a strong left-right split: titles and article details dominate the left, while read-time, date and action buttons sit neatly on the right. When there are no items, the center of this content area transforms into a calm, centered empty state with a short message and a bright call-to-action, surrounded by abundant negative space. Pagination rests at the bottom of the content band, lightly emphasized and centered, while any bulk actions slide in as a floating dark bar pinned to the bottom of the viewport, accented with neon-colored buttons. A confirmation dialog, when invoked, appears as a compact, high-contrast panel centered over a dimmed backdrop, maintaining the minimalist, high-end aesthetic while clearly guiding the user through destructive actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller occupies the top section of the Reading List page as a hero-like band spanning the full browser width, sitting directly below the sitewide navigation (not part of this component). Its background is the same pure black as the rest of the page, but the content inside is visually elevated with oversized typography and neon-accented elements. A centered max-width container (around 1120–1280px wide) is used, with generous top and bottom padding of roughly 72–96px to create a strong, spacious header presence. Within this container, content is organized into a two-column layout following a 12‑column grid: the left side takes about 7 of 12 columns (roughly 60–65% of the container width) and the right side about 5 of 12 columns (35–40%). The left column holds the main page title Reading List in very large type, possibly on two lines, with a subtle, short subheading directly below, both left-aligned. The right column, aligned to the top of the left heading block, presents supporting details such as the total number of saved articles, a short descriptive line explaining what the Reading List is, and possibly a small label or status indicator styled like a trust or quality badge. Horizontal alignment between columns is crisp, with a clear vertical gutter of around 32–40px between them. There is ample negative space above the title and below the two columns so that the hero feels airy and premium, not crowded. When scrolling, this section scrolls away normally, serving as a visually impactful top-of-page intro.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the top of the scrollable page content; it defines the initial viewport impression but does not remain fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar sits directly below the page controller hero band, separated by a vertical gap of about 32–48px to maintain visual breathing room. It is contained within the same centered max-width content band, aligning its left and right edges exactly with the hero content above. The toolbar forms a low-height horizontal strip (about 56–64px tall) with dark tones slightly lifted from the pure black background to indicate interactivity without forming a heavy panel. Inside this strip, content is arranged in a single row that follows a subtle left-right logic: filter controls (such as tags, status toggles or search) cluster on the left half, while sorting and view options (like sort dropdown and layout toggles) sit on the right half. The left and right groups are separated by flexible space so they can stretch to fill the row on wide screens while still collapsing gracefully on smaller widths. All controls align vertically around a central baseline, and there is around 16–24px of internal padding above and below the interactive elements. The toolbar feels lean and unobtrusive, with plenty of blank space around each control so the layout stays consistent with the minimalist, spacious aesthetic. A modest gap of about 24–32px separates the bottom of the toolbar from the top of the first list item.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a horizontal control band that scrolls with the content by default; it can optionally become sticky at the very top of the viewport after the user scrolls past the hero, maintaining alignment with the content band while keeping filters and sorting always accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The items list occupies the central content zone of the page, directly beneath the toolbar and above the pagination section. It is confined within the same centered max-width container as the hero and toolbar, creating a unified vertical column from top to bottom. The list spans nearly the full width of the container, with only standard horizontal padding (around 24–32px) on either side to prevent text from hitting the edges. Within this area, reading-list-item-row components are stacked vertically in a single column, each separated by a consistent vertical gap of roughly 16–24px. There is generous vertical padding between the top of the list and the toolbar above (about 24–32px) and between the bottom-most row and the pagination controls below (about 32–48px), allowing the section to feel open and easy to scan. The overall effect is a tall, refined vertical flow of rows floating over a dark background, with enough negative space that the page never feels like a dense table or dashboard, but more like an editorial list of curated items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content section; its height expands based on the number of items, pushing pagination downward. It does not fix or float but forms the main body of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each item row appears as a horizontally stretched, low-profile card within the list area, visually separated from the pure black background by a slightly lighter dark surface or a subtle neon-tinged outline and soft shadow. The row spans nearly the full width of the content band, with rounded corners and internal padding of roughly 24px left and right and 16–20px top and bottom. Internally, the row is laid out as a two-column structure reflecting the 12‑column grid: the left content zone occupies about 8 of 12 columns (around 65–70% of the width) and the right zone about 4 of 12 columns (30–35%). On the far left edge within the left zone, a compact checkbox for selection is aligned vertically with the main text, but visually minimized so the focus remains on the article information. Immediately to the right of the checkbox, the article title appears in a medium-large, high-contrast type, limited to one or two lines. Beneath the title, in smaller text, is key metadata such as source, author and a short snippet or tag list, all left-aligned and spaced with 4–8px increments. The right zone is right-aligned and contains secondary metadata and actions arranged vertically: read time and date added at the top, and beneath them horizontally aligned action buttons such as an open article button (primary styling with neon accent) and a remove button (subtle, possibly text-only or ghost). There is clear vertical separation (8–16px) between the metadata and action button row so they don’t feel cramped. On hover or focus, the entire row subtly brightens or shows a neon border glow, strengthening the tech-forward aesthetic. Each row has enough height (typically 80–120px total) for breathing room, distinguishing one item from the next.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard list row, expanding vertically if content wraps to multiple lines. It is fully clickable for opening the article except for designated action controls, which have their own hit areas.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit at the bottom of the main content column, directly underneath the last item row with a vertical gap of about 32–48px to keep them visually separated but still associated. They share the same centered max-width alignment as the rest of the content and occupy a slim horizontal band roughly 56–64px tall. Within this band, the primary pagination elements (previous and next arrows, individual page numbers, and current-page indicator) are centered horizontally, forming a compact cluster. The layout allows ample space between numbers (8–16px) so they feel tappable and not cramped. Optionally, an items-per-page selector or total-count label can be right-aligned on the same baseline, leaving a flexible gap between the central pagination cluster and the right-side label. The area around the pagination remains mostly empty black space above and below, reinforcing its tertiary importance while keeping it easy to find at the end of the scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the bottom of the content flow; it moves up or down depending on the number of list items and does not stick to the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the same spatial region as the items list and pagination when there are no saved articles. It is positioned below the toolbar, centered within the max-width content band. A large vertical padding (roughly 96–128px) above and below the empty state content creates the impression that it floats in the middle of the available viewport area. The content itself is vertically stacked and center-aligned: at the top, a simple, modern icon or abstract shape (optional) that matches the neon accent palette; below that, a concise headline in medium-large type explaining that there are no items in the Reading List; followed by a short descriptive sentence in smaller text; and finally a prominent call-to-action button (for example, to discover articles or browse the blog) in a neon magenta or cyan, centered horizontally. The width of this content block is limited to about 40–60% of the content band, ensuring that even on wide screens, text lines remain short and readable and that black negative space surrounds the block, echoing the cinematic, minimalist style of the hero section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed only when there are no items; it replaces the items list and pagination in the vertical flow. It scrolls normally if the viewport is short but typically fits comfortably within a single screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk actions bar appears only when one or more list items are selected. Visually, it is a horizontal bar that spans the full width of the viewport or at least the full width of the content band, anchored near the bottom edge of the screen while overlaying the content with a small vertical offset. Its height is around 56–64px, with a solid, slightly lighter dark background and a thin neon top border to distinguish it from the underlying content. Inside, the layout follows a left-right distribution: on the left side, aligned to the content band’s left margin, a short label displays the count of selected items; on the right side, aligned to the content band’s right margin, a row of action buttons appears, with the primary destructive action (such as Remove selected) styled in a bright neon accent and a secondary action (such as Cancel or Deselect all) in a more subdued outline or text style. There is generous horizontal padding (around 24px) between the edges of the bar and its contents, and buttons have 16–24px spacing between them. The bar lightly overlays the bottom portion of the items list, with enough transparency or shadow to indicate it floats above but without fully obscuring content behind it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the bottom of the viewport when active, remaining visible as the user scrolls through the list. It overlays the content rather than pushing the layout upward, ensuring persistent access to bulk actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove confirmation dialog appears as a modal overlay centered in the viewport, above all other page elements. When triggered, the entire screen is covered with a semi-transparent black scrim that dims the background while still allowing the underlying hero, list and bulk actions bar to be faintly visible, reinforcing the layered, cinematic aesthetic. In the center of the screen, a compact dialog panel is displayed, with a dark surface slightly lighter than the background and a thin neon border or accent bar at the top. The panel is rectangular, with a width of roughly 30–40% of the viewport (capped at around 480–560px) so it feels focused but not cramped. Internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides ensures a calm, generous layout. The content inside is vertically stacked: a succinct title at the top (e.g., Confirm removal), a short explanatory message below it describing the consequence (such as removing selected articles from the list), and a horizontal row of two action buttons at the bottom right of the dialog. The primary destructive button uses the neon accent color and is placed to the right of a more neutral cancel button. There is a comfortable 16–24px gap between the message and the button row, and between the buttons themselves. The dialog is centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport and does not stretch to the edges even on small screens, maintaining clear margins around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal overlay that locks page scrolling while visible. It sits above a dimmed backdrop, dismissible only through its explicit actions or close affordance.

name: Project Estimator
title: Project Estimator
description: Configurable estimator where users select project types and options, see a live cost estimate, and optionally save it with their contact details.

core_features: Load dynamic estimator configuration (project types and option sets) using getProjectEstimatorOptions and bind them to the estimator form.
Allow users to select one or more project types and map those selections into the ProjectEstimate flags includes_mobile_app, includes_marketing_website, and includes_other_project_types.
Allow users to configure design complexity, integrations, analytics, and content creation using the options returned by getProjectEstimatorOptions, mapped to ProjectEstimate fields.
Allow users to enter a target budget which is passed to calculateProjectEstimatePreview and stored in ProjectEstimate.target_budget.
Call calculateProjectEstimatePreview whenever relevant estimator inputs change and display the returned total_estimated_cost and line_items in real time.
Indicate when total_estimated_cost from calculateProjectEstimatePreview exceeds target_budget using the exceeds_target_budget flag.
Provide a form to collect contact_name and contact_email and call saveProjectEstimate with the current estimator configuration to persist a ProjectEstimate record.
Provide a navigation control that sends the user to contact.html with topic=project_inquiry to continue the conversation about the configured estimate.
user_workflows:
Initial load with optional preselection from other pages:
1) User lands on project_estimator.html, possibly with URL parameters includes_mobile_app and/or includes_marketing_website.
2) The page parses URL parameters to initialize the estimator state (for example, sets includes_mobile_app to true if the corresponding URL parameter is present and truthy).
3) The page calls getProjectEstimatorOptions to retrieve project_types and all option groups (design_complexity_options, integration_options, analytics_options, content_creation_options).
4) Using the loaded options and the URL-derived state, the estimator form is initialized (e.g., relevant project types preselected, default option values chosen according to implementation rules).
5) With the initialized state, the page calls calculateProjectEstimatePreview to render the initial total_estimated_cost and line_items.

Adjusting project types and options to explore pricing:
1) User selects or deselects project types (for example, toggling the options that correspond to includes_mobile_app or includes_marketing_website).
2) The project-type selector component updates the mapped boolean fields on the in-memory ProjectEstimate representation and updates the URL parameters to reflect the current selection.
3) User adjusts design complexity, integrations, analytics, and content creation options using the sets returned by getProjectEstimatorOptions (each selection updates a corresponding ProjectEstimate field value).
4) User optionally edits the target budget value.
5) On any relevant change, the page calls calculateProjectEstimatePreview with the current ProjectEstimate configuration and target_budget.
6) The estimate preview panel updates with the new total_estimated_cost, detailed line_items, and the exceeds_target_budget status, allowing the user to iteratively refine their configuration.

Staying within a target budget:
1) User enters or changes a target budget in the budget input field.
2) The budget input component updates ProjectEstimate.target_budget and synchronizes this value into the URL (for shareable state) if that behavior is implemented.
3) The page triggers calculateProjectEstimatePreview with the updated target_budget.
4) The estimate preview displays whether exceeds_target_budget is true.
5) If exceeds_target_budget is true, the UI highlights this state and the user can lower-cost options (for example, changing design_complexity or integrations_option) using the same form controls.
6) Each adjustment triggers another calculateProjectEstimatePreview call until the user is satisfied with a configuration that meets or aligns with their budget.

Saving an estimate with contact details:
1) After configuring the project and viewing a preview, the user fills in contact_name and contact_email in the save estimate form.
2) On submit, the form gathers the current ProjectEstimate-relevant fields (includes_mobile_app, includes_marketing_website, includes_other_project_types, design_complexity, integrations_option, analytics_option, content_creation_option, target_budget) along with the contact fields.
3) The page calls saveProjectEstimate with these values.
4) If the response indicates success, the page can display a confirmation message using the returned message and may expose or internally store the returned estimate (ProjectEstimate) for subsequent use within the same session.
5) If the response indicates failure, the form displays an error state and allows the user to correct inputs and resubmit.

Escalating from self-service estimate to direct contact:
1) From the estimate preview area or save confirmation area, the user clicks a CTA such as a button that represents 'Talk to us about this estimate'.
2) The page navigates to contact.html with a URL parameter topic=project_inquiry.
3) The receiving contact page can then use the topic parameter to initialize the correct inquiry context (outside the scope of this page).
interactions: On page load, a data-fetch interaction initializes the estimator by calling getProjectEstimatorOptions and then calls calculateProjectEstimatePreview with default or URL-derived values.

Checkbox or toggle interactions on project type options update ProjectEstimate boolean properties (includes_mobile_app, includes_marketing_website, includes_other_project_types) and then trigger calculateProjectEstimatePreview.

Radio button or select interactions for design_complexity, integrations_option, analytics_option, and content_creation_option update the corresponding ProjectEstimate fields and then trigger calculateProjectEstimatePreview.

A numeric or currency-formatted input interaction for target_budget updates ProjectEstimate.target_budget and then triggers calculateProjectEstimatePreview; this interaction may also update or add a target_budget query parameter in the URL.

The estimate preview area responds to changes in the calculateProjectEstimatePreview response: it updates displayed line_items and total_estimated_cost and conditionally renders a warning or highlight if exceeds_target_budget is true.

The save estimate form includes text input interactions for contact_name and contact_email and a submission interaction; on submit it performs client-side validation where applicable and then calls saveProjectEstimate.

After saveProjectEstimate resolves, the page shows either a success state (using the returned success flag and message) or an error state, and may disable or adjust the save button to prevent duplicate submissions if desired.
A navigation interaction on a CTA element redirects the user to contact.html with the query parameter topic=project_inquiry when clicked.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven by a combination of URL parameters and in-memory component state, without using localStorage. On initial load, the page parses the incoming URL parameters includes_mobile_app and includes_marketing_website and converts them into boolean values used to initialize the in-memory representation of a ProjectEstimate. If additional estimator parameters are encoded in the URL (such as design_complexity, integrations_option, analytics_option, content_creation_option, includes_other_project_types, or target_budget), the state manager component can also parse and apply those values, provided they map to allowed enum values or numeric types defined by the ProjectEstimate model and interfaces.

Whenever the user changes a project-type selection, the corresponding boolean field (includes_mobile_app, includes_marketing_website, or includes_other_project_types) is updated in memory and optionally reflected back into the URL query string so that the current state can be bookmarked or shared. For example, turning on the mobile app option can cause the URL parameter includes_mobile_app to be set to a truthy value; turning it off can remove the parameter or set it to a falsy value.

Similarly, modifications to configuration options (design_complexity, integrations_option, analytics_option, content_creation_option) can be mirrored into URL parameters whose names match the ProjectEstimate field names. When the target_budget input changes, the numeric value is stored both in the in-memory ProjectEstimate.target_budget field and, if implemented, in a target_budget URL parameter.

Every time the effective estimator state changes (any of the boolean flags, configuration enums, or target_budget), the page calls calculateProjectEstimatePreview using the current field values and target_budget. The returned total_estimated_cost, line_items, target_budget, and exceeds_target_budget values are stored within the estimate preview component's local state. This preview state is not written to the URL but is derived from the URL and form state.

When the user submits the save estimate form, the current in-memory ProjectEstimate-related fields plus contact_name and contact_email are passed to saveProjectEstimate. The response includes a ProjectEstimate record and a success flag. The page can store minimal transient state to reflect that the estimate has been successfully saved but does not persist this to the URL. Any subsequent navigation away from the page (for example to contact.html?topic=project_inquiry) uses only URL parameters to communicate context to the next page.

components.functionality: On initial page load, fetches dynamic configuration for the estimator by calling getProjectEstimatorOptions. It exposes project_types, design_complexity_options, integration_options, analytics_options, content_creation_options, and base_pricing_notes to other components and manages loading and error states for these option sets.

Parses URL query parameters on load (including includes_mobile_app, includes_marketing_website, and any additional estimator-related parameters) and initializes the in-memory estimator state that mirrors ProjectEstimate fields. It also updates the URL query string when the user changes relevant form fields so that the estimator state can be restored when the page is revisited with the same URL.

Displays the available project_types returned by getProjectEstimatorOptions as a list of selectable controls (for example, checkboxes). It maps user selections to the boolean fields includes_mobile_app, includes_marketing_website, and includes_other_project_types on the in-memory ProjectEstimate representation. Every selection change triggers an update to the estimator state and may update relevant URL parameters. After updating the state, it triggers a recalculation of the estimate via calculateProjectEstimatePreview.

Provides grouped controls for selecting design_complexity, integrations_option, analytics_option, and content_creation_option. Each group is populated from the corresponding option arrays (design_complexity_options, integration_options, analytics_options, content_creation_options) returned by getProjectEstimatorOptions, and each option includes dynamic labels and incremental_cost values from the API. Selecting an option updates the respective enum field on the ProjectEstimate state. After each change, this component triggers calculateProjectEstimatePreview with the updated configuration so the user sees the new estimated cost and line_items.

Contains an input for the user's target budget. When the user enters or updates a value, the component validates it according to numeric rules defined by the implementation, updates ProjectEstimate.target_budget, and optionally updates a target_budget parameter in the URL. It then triggers calculateProjectEstimatePreview with the new target budget so that exceeds_target_budget can be evaluated.

Subscribes to changes in the estimator configuration and calls calculateProjectEstimatePreview with the current state whenever relevant fields change (project type flags, configuration enums, or target_budget). It displays the returned total_estimated_cost and iterates through line_items to show the breakdown. It also reads exceeds_target_budget to conditionally display a visual or textual indicator that the estimate is above or within the target budget. This component does not directly modify URL parameters; it only reflects the current calculation.

Collects contact_name and contact_email along with the current estimator configuration to save a ProjectEstimate. On submission, it compiles includes_mobile_app, includes_marketing_website, includes_other_project_types, design_complexity, integrations_option, analytics_option, content_creation_option, target_budget, contact_name, and contact_email into a payload and calls saveProjectEstimate. It then handles the returned success flag, message, and ProjectEstimate object to show a confirmation or error state and may disable the submit button or display a snapshot of the saved estimate state.

Provides a clickable control that navigates the user to contact.html with the query parameter topic=project_inquiry. It can be rendered near the estimate preview or after a successful save so that users can easily move from self-service estimation to direct consultation about their project.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The estimator page keeps the mockup’s full-width, dark hero band with a centered max-width content container and a two-column layout. Inside this main estimator band, all interactive estimator controls are grouped on the larger left column, while the financial outcome and save/contact actions are grouped on the narrower right column. The flow is still linear top-to-bottom, but the key experience is concentrated in this hero-like estimator section: headline (outside current components) at the top-left, then left column form sections stacked vertically, and right column panels stacked vertically.
