layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The estimator page keeps the mockup’s full-width, dark hero band with a centered max-width content container and a two-column layout. Inside this main estimator band, all interactive estimator controls are grouped on the larger left column, while the financial outcome and save/contact actions are grouped on the narrower right column. The flow is still linear top-to-bottom, but the key experience is concentrated in this hero-like estimator section: headline (outside current components) at the top-left, then left column form sections stacked vertically, and right column panels stacked vertically.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The estimator hero band spans nearly the full viewport height on desktop, with ~10–15% reserved for the top navigation and the remaining height given to the estimator content. A centered container (around 1120–1280px max-width) is split into roughly a 7/12 left column (about 60–65% width) and a 5/12 right column (about 35–40%). The columns are separated by a generous gutter (32–48px). Vertical padding of 80–112px is used above and below the estimator content, with 24–40px vertical spacing between stacked components inside each column to preserve the spacious, premium feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a low-density, premium aesthetic: few major modules, each given ample breathing room. Each form section appears as a clean, minimally bordered or borderless panel with 24–32px internal padding, generous line spacing, and clear separation from the next section. The right column contains only two primary blocks (estimate preview and save form) plus a subtle contact link, avoiding clutter. Labels, inputs, and numbers are large and readable, with no tight packing or multi-column field layouts inside a single panel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left with the page headline and then moves to the top-right area where the estimated total is surfaced in the estimate preview panel, mirroring the logo → CTA pattern from the mockup. From there, the user is drawn down and left into the main control stack (project type, configuration options, budget), and then back to the right column to confirm or refine via the save estimate form and contact link. This creates a modified Z-pattern: estimator title (top-left) → estimated total (top-right) → main controls (mid-left) → save/contact actions (lower-right). The most visually dramatic elements are the headline and the estimate preview panel, punctuated with neon accent colors on the dark background.

layout.overall_description: The Project Estimator page appears as a dark, cinematic hero section occupying most of the viewport height, with a pure black background and neon magenta–cyan accents. A centered, wide content band is split into a larger left column and a slightly narrower right column following a 7/5 grid split. At the left, beneath the (external) oversized “Project Estimator” headline, a vertical stack of generously spaced panels walks the user from project type selection through configuration options and budget input. Each panel feels like a sleek, minimal card: wide, uncluttered, and aligned flush with the left column edge. On the right, aligned with the top of the control stack, a bold estimate preview panel acts as the hero’s numerical focal point—like a premium stats badge—with large typography displaying the current cost estimate. Beneath it, a compact save estimate form lets users capture their configuration, and at the bottom-right edge sits a subtle, text-based contact link functioning as the secondary route to speak with the agency. The entire composition mirrors the original mockup’s dramatic, low-density layout: big left, focused right, lots of controlled black space, and a clear diagonal visual journey from top-left narrative to top-right numbers and down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-fetcher component is non-visual and occupies no dedicated space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the estimator hero section, initializing data before any of the visible panels render. It does not displace any content; instead, it runs in the background as the left and right columns appear. If needed, a subtle loading state is reflected within the visible panels themselves (e.g., skeleton states in the project-type selector and estimate preview), not as a standalone UI element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Background-only behavior; it attaches to the estimator section lifecycle without any visual footprint or position in the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-manager component also remains invisible and does not claim visible space in the layout. It logically spans the entire estimator hero section, syncing the state of the left-column controls and the right-column estimate preview with URL parameters. It behaves as an underlying layer, updating and reading state as the user interacts, without any explicit on-screen representation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Background-only behavior; no fixed position or rendered surface, operating behind all visible components across the full estimator area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-type selector is the first visible interactive block in the left column of the estimator hero. It sits directly beneath the large page headline, aligned flush with the left edge of the central content container and spanning the full width of the left column (roughly 60–65% of the container). There is generous top padding—around 32–48px—from the headline above, reinforcing the airy feel. The selector itself appears as a wide horizontal or grid-like panel with large, tappable project-type options (e.g., tiles or pill buttons), with 24–32px internal padding and clear separation from the container’s edges. Below it, a vertical gap of about 32–40px separates this block from the configuration options form, clearly marking it as the first decision step in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, participating in the 12-column grid as a 7/12 span on desktop. On scroll, it moves normally with the page content, remaining above the other left-column panels.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration-options form sits directly below the project-type selector in the left column, maintaining the same left alignment and width (full left column, about 7/12 of the grid). It is separated from the selector by a generous vertical gap of roughly 32–40px. Internally, options are stacked in a single column to preserve the spacious, editorial style, with each option group separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing. The panel has 24–32px internal padding from its borders, and ample black negative space between fields ensures it does not feel dense even if several options are present. A slightly lighter dark background or subtle outline may set this panel apart from the page background without breaking the minimalist aesthetic. Below this form, another 32–40px gap leads into the budget input panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, extending the same 7/12 width. It scrolls naturally with the main content and does not overlap or float relative to other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-input panel is the final major form block in the left column, positioned directly beneath the configuration-options form with approximately 32–48px of vertical spacing. It shares the same width as the other left-column panels (about 60–65% of the content container) and aligns perfectly with their left edge, creating a clean vertical column. Inside, one or a small number of key inputs (e.g., sliders, numeric fields) are centered within the panel with generous breathing room, making the budget interaction feel focused and important. The panel height is moderate, with at least 24–32px padding on all sides, and additional empty black space below helps visually close the left-side flow before the page’s next section begins. Its proximity to the bottom of the viewport on typical laptop screens gives the impression that users can adjust budget and immediately see changes reflected in the neighboring estimate preview panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, occupying the same 7/12 span. It scrolls with the rest of the left content, while the right column’s estimate preview may remain in view if implemented as sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-preview panel is the focal element in the right column and visually parallels the trust/award badge from the original mockup. It is positioned near the top of the right column, vertically aligned with the top of the project-type selector (or just slightly above), and spans the full width of the right column (about 35–40% of the container, a 5/12 grid span). There is a clean vertical alignment between the top of this panel and the main headline block across the grid, creating a strong left-right balance. The panel itself appears as a bold, high-contrast card: dark background with neon accent borders or typography, displaying the current total estimate in oversized numbers occupying the upper half of the panel, with smaller supporting breakdown or notes below. The panel has 24–32px internal padding and sits within generous outer margins—roughly 16–24px from the container’s right edge and 24–32px from the top of the estimator content band. Below it, a 32–40px gap separates it from the save-estimate form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, this panel can behave as a sticky element within the right column: once it reaches a certain offset from the top of the viewport (e.g., 96px below the global nav), it remains fixed while the left-column form sections scroll, ensuring the estimate is always visible. On smaller screens, it reverts to normal static behavior and stacks above or below the form sections depending on responsive design choices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-estimate form is located directly beneath the estimate-preview panel in the right column, separated by around 32–40px of vertical space to maintain visual clarity. It shares the same width and alignment as the estimate panel (full right column, about 5/12 of the grid) and sits slightly lower on the page, roughly mid-height relative to the left column’s content. The form is compact and intentionally minimal—just a few fields like name, email, and label for the estimate—arranged in a single vertical stack with 16–24px between fields. The panel has a modest height with 24–32px internal padding and may feature a subtle top border or faint glow that visually links it to the estimate-preview panel above without overpowering it. Below this form, a smaller vertical gap (16–24px) leads to the contact CTA link, framing the save action as the primary next step and the contact link as an alternate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, following the estimate-preview panel’s width and alignment. If the estimate panel is sticky, this form scrolls underneath it, entering and leaving the viewport while the estimate itself remains in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-cta-link is positioned as a subtle yet visible text link at the bottom of the right column’s content stack, directly beneath the save-estimate form. It is separated from the form by a modest 16–24px of vertical spacing, ensuring it feels like a related, secondary action rather than part of the form itself. The link is right-aligned within the right column, sitting close to the column’s lower-right corner while respecting a 24–32px bottom padding from the edge of the estimator hero section. Its styling echoes the mockup’s magenta/cyan accent color, making it stand out against the black background without overpowering the estimate preview. Spatially, it anchors the eye at the lower-right endpoint of the page’s Z-pattern, providing a clear path from seeing an estimate to initiating a conversation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the right column, aligned to the right edge of the text block. It scrolls naturally with the content and does not stick or float.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Contact page where users can view agency contact details, send a general inquiry, and navigate to book a discovery call or other next steps.

core_features: Load and display contact page content (intro text, contact details, and available inquiry topics) using getContactPageContent.
Provide a general contact form bound to submitGeneralContactInquiry for sending non-specific inquiries.
Use the optional URL parameter `topic` to preselect the inquiry topic in the contact form.
Display confirmation or error states after form submission based on submitGeneralContactInquiry response.
Expose a prominent action to book a discovery call that navigates to discovery_scheduler.html.
Offer guided next-step navigation options (such as discovery call or other pages) based on the selected topic and page context.
user_workflows:
View contact details:
1) User navigates to contact.html (via header, footer, or direct URL).
2) On page load, the application reads the `topic` URL parameter if present.
3) The application calls getContactPageContent.
4) When getContactPageContent resolves, the page displays the intro content along with contact_email, contact_phone, and office_location from the response.
5) User can click the displayed email, phone, or view the office location information as provided by the API.

Submit a general contact inquiry:
1) User lands on contact.html (optionally with `topic` specified in the URL).
2) The application loads data via getContactPageContent and initializes the inquiry topic selection. If `topic` is present and matches a returned general_inquiry_topics.value, that topic is preselected; otherwise, a default selection strategy is applied (e.g., first item in the list).
3) User fills in the form fields: name, email, company (optional), and message. The topic field is bound to the currently selected topic from general_inquiry_topics.
4) User submits the form, triggering submitGeneralContactInquiry with the current form values.
5) While awaiting the response, the form can be disabled and a pending state shown.
6) On success, the page displays a confirmation state using the returned message and ticket_id (without hardcoding values), keeping the user on contact.html.
7) On failure (success=false or transport error), the page shows an error state with an appropriate message and allows the user to retry.

Book a discovery call from the Contact page:
1) User navigates to contact.html and optionally reviews the intro content, contact details, or fills some part of the form.
2) User clicks the 'Book a discovery call' action.
3) The click event triggers navigation to discovery_scheduler.html without additional parameters (as defined in outgoing_connections).
4) The discovery scheduler page then handles slot selection and booking independently.

Guided next step selection based on topic:
1) User arrives on contact.html, either directly or from another page that may pass `topic` in the URL.
2) After loading getContactPageContent, the selected topic is determined (either from `topic` URL parameter or default selection).
3) A guidance component derives recommended next-step actions (such as linking to discovery_scheduler.html, project_estimator.html, or specific service pages) according to a routing configuration that does not hardcode API data values.
4) User can click any of these recommended action links to navigate away to the appropriate page for deeper engagement.
interactions: Page load interaction: On initial load of contact.html, read the `topic` URL parameter, call getContactPageContent, and initialize page state (intro content, contact details, topics list, and selected topic).

Topic selection interaction: When the user changes the selected topic in the form, update the internal selected topic state and optionally update the current URL's `topic` query parameter so the URL reflects the user's selection.

Form input interaction: As the user types into the name, email, company, and message fields, update the local form state bound to submitGeneralContactInquiry parameters without introducing static default values.

Form submission interaction: On form submit, validate required fields on the client side, then call submitGeneralContactInquiry with name, email, company, topic, and message from the form state; handle loading, success, and error states based on the response.

Post-submission interaction: On successful submission, show confirmation content that incorporates the API-returned message and ticket_id. Allow the user to submit another inquiry (by resetting the form state) without reloading the page, or navigate to other pages via provided links.
Discovery call CTA interaction: When the user clicks the discovery call button, perform a navigation to discovery_scheduler.html as specified in the outgoing_connections configuration.

Guided navigation interaction: When the user clicks any suggested next-step link (for example, a link to an estimator or a service page), navigate to that page, optionally including a `topic` URL parameter when appropriate so downstream pages can understand the context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact page state is primarily derived from the getContactPageContent response and the `topic` URL parameter. On page load, the application reads `topic` from the URL (if present). After getContactPageContent returns, the page computes the initially selected topic by checking whether the URL `topic` value matches any general_inquiry_topics.value in the response; if there is a match, that topic is selected, otherwise the implementation falls back to a non-hardcoded default strategy (for example, using the first entry in the returned list). The topic selection state is kept in memory while the user interacts with the page, and may also be synchronized back to the URL via the `topic` query parameter when the user changes the selection, enabling bookmarkable and shareable URLs. Form submission does not depend on additional URL parameters; instead, confirmation or error states are handled through in-page state that uses the response fields from submitGeneralContactInquiry (success, ticket_id, message). No localStorage is used for state persistence; all persistent or shareable state relevant to this page is represented via the URL (e.g., the `topic` parameter) and data returned from the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: On initial page load, read the `topic` URL parameter, call getContactPageContent, and initialize a shared state object containing intro content, contact details, the list of general inquiry topics, and the initially selected topic.
Display the introductory heading and copy for the Contact page to orient the user and explain how to get in touch, using only the dynamic values from getContactPageContent.
Show the agency's primary contact details, including contact_email, contact_phone, and office_location, allowing the user to initiate email or phone contact directly via appropriate link behaviors.

Collect a general inquiry from the user (name, email, company, topic, message) and submit it via submitGeneralContactInquiry, handling validation, loading state, success feedback, and error feedback based on the API response.
Provide a prominent action for users who want to speak directly with the agency by navigating them to the discovery call scheduler when clicked.

Use the current selected topic and page context to determine and display suggested next actions (for example, booking a discovery call, using the project estimator, or reviewing relevant services), without hardcoding any topic values from the API. Clicking a suggestion navigates the user to the corresponding page, optionally including the `topic` parameter where appropriate.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear top-to-bottom flow with a dominant, full-width hero-like contact intro at the top, followed by a secondary contact interaction section. The hero mirrors the mockup’s two-column structure: a large left text block for the contact headline and context, and a right column hosting the contact details panel and the discovery-call CTA. Beneath this hero band, a second full-width section contains the general inquiry form on the left and a next-steps guidance block on the right, preserving the left-dominant / right-supporting pattern from the original design.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear top-to-bottom flow with a dominant, full-width hero-like contact intro at the top, followed by a secondary contact interaction section. The hero mirrors the mockup’s two-column structure: a large left text block for the contact headline and context, and a right column hosting the contact details panel and the discovery-call CTA. Beneath this hero band, a second full-width section contains the general inquiry form on the left and a next-steps guidance block on the right, preserving the left-dominant / right-supporting pattern from the original design.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero contact intro section takes approximately the first full viewport height, with a centered max-width content band. Within the hero, about 60–65% of the width is reserved for the left headline block and 35–40% for the right column (details + CTA). The section below for the form uses similar overall width, with the form occupying about 65–70% of the content width on the left and the next-steps guidance taking the remaining 30–35% on the right. Vertical spacing between the hero and the form section is generous (around 96px), and each section has ample top and bottom padding (64–96px) to maintain the spacious, premium feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is kept intentionally low-density and spacious. The hero shows very few elements: a bold, oversized contact headline on the left and a compact but legible details panel plus a single primary CTA on the right, all surrounded by ample black negative space. The form section below contains more fields but is still visually airy, with clear grouping, comfortable line spacing, and generous margins between field groups. The next-steps guidance is concise and vertically spaced, acting as a light, editorial sidebar rather than a dense information block.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a modified Z-pattern inspired by the mockup: users first encounter the hero, starting at the left with the large, dramatic contact headline, then naturally glance to the right to see the agency’s core contact details and the discovery call button. From there, the flow moves downward, landing on the general inquiry form on the left as the primary interaction, and finally to the right-hand next-steps guidance which contextualizes options like booking a call or exploring other paths. Neon accents and button treatments are used to highlight the primary CTAs within this path without adding clutter.

layout.overall_description: The contact page opens on a full-width, nearly full-height hero set against a deep black background with subtle magenta–cyan accents. Inside a centered max-width container, the content is split into two columns: on the left, a massive, two-line contact headline such as “Let’s build what’s next” dominates about two-thirds of the width, set with generous breathing room above, below, and to the sides. On the right, a narrower column stacks a sleek contact-details panel over a bold discovery-call button, aligned vertically with the headline’s mid-section. The overall impression is cinematic and minimal: very few elements, lots of negative space, and highly legible typography. As you scroll down, the second section mirrors the same centered band and left-right structure. The general inquiry form sits in a broad, softly glowing panel on the left, while a vertical next-steps card on the right outlines alternative actions and expectations. Spacing between sections is large, keeping the layout calm and premium. All elements sit on the same 12‑column grid, with consistent horizontal alignment from hero to form, reinforcing the sense of a coherent, modern, high-contrast design system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that temporarily sits on top of the entire page content. It uses the same pure black background as the main site, optionally with a very subtle radial magenta–cyan glow centered in the viewport to stay aligned with the brand’s cinematic aesthetic. At the visual center of the screen, a compact loading indicator (logo mark plus a minimal spinner or progress bar) is displayed, approximately 10–15% of the viewport width, leaving wide empty margins on all sides. The overlay completely covers the hero and subsequent sections until data is ready, after which it fades out, revealing the underlying two-column hero layout. No other components are visible while this loader is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, full-screen overlay positioned above all other components (z-index top). Centered content; non-scrollable while active. Disappears once initial data load completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact intro section forms the main hero band at the top of the page, stretching full-width horizontally and approximately one viewport height vertically on desktop. Within this band, a centered max-width container (around 70–80% of the viewport width) is aligned to the middle of the screen with generous vertical padding (64–96px above and below the content). Inside the container, a 12-column grid is used: the contact intro occupies the left 7 columns. In this left area, a bold, two-line headline such as “Let’s build what’s next” or “Contact your digital enablers” is placed slightly above the vertical center, taking up roughly 40–50% of the viewport height. The headline text is oversized, spanning nearly the entire width of its 7-column space, with ample negative space to the left edge of the viewport and a comfortable margin below (24–32px) before any supporting one-liner or microcopy. There is no clutter: the left column remains primarily typographic, with no cards or form fields intruding into this area. The right 5 columns in the same row are reserved for the contact-details-panel and the discovery-call-cta-button, which stack vertically and align to the top of the hero’s central band, preserving a clear left-text / right-support composition that visually mirrors the original mockup’s hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flow block, occupying the top of the page beneath any global navigation. No sticky behavior; the entire section scrolls out of view as the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact details panel sits within the hero’s right column, occupying the upper portion of the 5-column span. It is top-aligned relative to the main headline’s mid-height so that its top edge roughly lines up with the second line of the headline on the left, creating a visually balanced horizontal band. The panel is a dark card that fills approximately 80–100% of the right column’s width, leaving a slim margin to the container’s right edge (16–24px) to keep it visually light. Vertically, it takes up about 35–45% of the hero’s height, leaving comfortable negative space both above and below. Inside the panel, the agency’s core contact details (email, phone, location, office hours) are stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps between groups, maintaining readability and aligning to the 8px spacing system. The card uses a subtle border or glow in a magenta–cyan accent along its edges or top border, but the interior remains minimal with generous padding (24–32px on all sides). Below the panel, there is a noticeable gap (24–32px) before the discovery-call-cta-button appears, making the details card the first element the eye encounters on the right side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s right column, participating in the responsive grid. On narrower screens, it stacks above the discovery call CTA and eventually beneath the intro headline when columns collapse.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The general inquiry form resides in the first section directly below the hero. This section is full-width with a pure black background and a clear vertical separation from the hero via a generous top margin (around 96px) and top padding inside the section. Within the centered max-width container aligned with the hero’s edges, the form occupies the left 8 of 12 grid columns (around 65–70% of the width), making it the dominant element of this band. The form is presented as a slightly elevated or subtly outlined panel that spans most of its allotted width, with internal padding of 32–48px on all sides. Form fields are arranged in 1 or 2 columns depending on the viewport, but overall keep a clean, linear flow from top to bottom, with 16–24px vertical spacing between individual fields and 32px between logically grouped sections (e.g., contact info vs. project details). The panel itself consumes a significant vertical area, but is visually softened by ample spacing above and below it within the section, maintaining the same premium, low-density feel as the hero. The form title and a brief guiding sentence appear at the top of the panel, aligned left, with the title echoing the bold typography of the hero but at a smaller scale. The primary submit button sits towards the bottom-left of the form panel, spanning roughly 30–40% of the panel width, visually aligned with the main input column and echoing the neon accent styling used in the discovery-call CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component in the document flow. On desktop it forms a left-dominant column; on tablet/mobile it expands to full-width and stacks above the next-steps guidance.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discovery call CTA button is positioned directly beneath the contact-details-panel within the hero’s right column, aligned to the left edge of that column for a clean vertical line. It sits 24–32px below the bottom of the details panel, occupying roughly 70–100% of the right column’s width depending on desired emphasis (on larger screens it might be 70–80% to preserve some negative space on the right). Visually, it is treated as a primary pill or rectangular button with bold magenta or cyan fill and high-contrast text, standing out against the black background and slightly dimmer details card. Vertical padding inside the button is generous (12–16px), and horizontal padding ensures the label has breathing room while still feeling compact and action-oriented. The button is placed at about mid-height of the hero area when combined with the details panel above, making it easy to catch in peripheral vision once the user scans the right column. In the visual hierarchy, it acts as a clear alternative or complementary next step to sending a general inquiry via the form below, but it remains secondary to the hero headline and co-primary with the form’s main submit button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the hero’s right column. On narrower screens, it stacks directly under the contact-details-panel and, when the layout collapses to a single column, it appears immediately below the details panel and above the scroll to the general inquiry form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The next-steps guidance component shares the same horizontal band as the general inquiry form, occupying the right side of the centered container. On desktop, it sits in the remaining 4 of 12 grid columns (about 30–35% of the width), aligned to the top of the form panel so that their top edges visually line up across the section. This component appears as a slim, vertical card or text block with a subtle outline or gradient accent, but less visually intense than the primary actions; it should not compete with the form or discovery CTA. Inside, a short heading, 2–4 lines of explanatory copy, and a compact list of next-step options (e.g., link to book a discovery call, checklist of what happens after submitting, or alternate contact methods) are stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing. The card leaves generous empty space at the bottom to retain the low-density feel, and its overall height is clearly less than the form’s height, creating an asymmetrical but balanced composition. This right-hand block acts like an editorial sidebar: it is easy to read when glancing over from the form but never overwhelms the main action. On smaller screens, it moves below the form, maintaining the same internal spacing and card structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned sidebar within the form section. It participates in the responsive grid: on desktop it stays to the right of the form; on mobile it stacks below and spans the full container width.

name: Discovery Call Scheduler
title: Discovery Call Scheduler
description: Schedule a 30-minute discovery call by choosing a time zone, selecting an available date and time, entering booking details, and confirming the appointment.

core_features: Read optional URL parameters (month, year, time_zone) to initialize the scheduler view.
Allow the user to select a time zone and reload availability using getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar.
Display a month-level availability calendar indicating which days have discovery call slots.
Allow the user to select a specific date and load available 30-minute slots via getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate.
Highlight and allow quick selection of the earliest available morning slot within the configured morning window (e.g., between 9:00 and 11:00 in the chosen time zone).
Collect booking details (name, email, company, discussion topic) for the selected slot.
Confirm the booking using bookDiscoveryCall and display a booking confirmation summary.
Provide a way to cancel or go back to the contact.html page at any point.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Accessing the scheduler from contact.html:
1. User clicks “Book a discovery call” on contact.html.
2. contact.html navigates to discovery_scheduler.html with optional URL parameters: month, year, time_zone.
3. On page load, the scheduler parses these parameters and calls getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar with the resolved month, year, and time_zone.
4. The calendar is rendered with available days highlighted for the specified month/year and time zone.

Workflow 2 – Changing time zone and viewing availability:
1. User opens the time zone selector and selects a time zone.
2. The scheduler updates the time_zone parameter in the URL without reloading the page.
3. The scheduler calls getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar with the new time_zone (and current month/year).
4. The calendar updates to reflect availability in the selected time zone.
5. If a date was already selected, the scheduler also calls getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate for that date and the new time_zone and updates the slot list.

Workflow 3 – Selecting a date and viewing time slots:
1. User clicks on an available day in the calendar.
2. The selected date is stored in page state and reflected in the URL (e.g., discovery_scheduler.html?month=...&year=...&time_zone=...&date=YYYY-MM-DD).
3. The scheduler calls getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate with the selected date and current time_zone.
4. The page displays the list of available 30-minute slots for that date, grouped or labeled by time of day (e.g., morning/afternoon).

Workflow 4 – Selecting the earliest morning slot:
1. After slots load for a date, the scheduler filters slots to identify which ones fall within the morning window (e.g., between 09:00 and 11:00 in the current time zone).
2. The earliest slot within that morning window is programmatically determined from the returned DiscoveryCallSlot list.
3. The user can either manually click a slot or click an action such as “Select earliest morning slot”.
4. On selection, the chosen slot_id is stored in page state and reflected in the URL (e.g., &slot_id=...).
5. The booking form is activated for that selected slot.

Workflow 5 – Entering booking details and confirming:
1. With a slot selected, the user fills in booking fields: name, email, company, and topic (mapped to the allowed topic enum values).
2. The user clicks the “Confirm booking” button.
3. The scheduler validates required fields and topic selection on the client side.
4. If valid, the scheduler calls bookDiscoveryCall with slot_id, selected_time_zone (constrained to values allowed by the DiscoveryCallBooking model), name, email, company, and topic.
5. If the API returns success, the page displays a confirmation view showing date, time, time zone, and the captured booking details.
6. The confirmation view offers navigation options to go back to contact.html or optionally keep the booking reference visible.

Workflow 6 – Canceling or returning to contact page without booking:
1. At any time before confirmation, the user can click a “Back to contact” link.
2. The page navigates to contact.html without additional parameters (as specified in outgoing_connections).
3. Any unsubmitted selection or form input is discarded, relying solely on URL parameters for state if the user later returns to the scheduler.
interactions:
Time zone selection:
- Interaction: User changes the selected time zone via a dropdown or radio control.
- Effect: Updates the time_zone query parameter and re-fetches availability via getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar. If a date is selected, also re-fetches slots via getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate.

Month navigation in calendar:
- Interaction: User clicks previous/next month controls.
- Effect: Updates month and year query parameters accordingly and re-calls getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar with the new month/year and current time_zone.

Date selection:
- Interaction: User clicks an available date cell in the calendar.
- Effect: Sets the selected date in internal state and in the date query parameter, then calls getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate for that date and current time_zone to populate the slot list.

Earliest morning slot shortcut:
- Interaction: User clicks a control such as “Select earliest morning slot” when viewing slots for a date.
- Effect: The slot list component identifies the earliest available DiscoveryCallSlot that qualifies as a morning slot within the configured time range, sets that slot as selected, and updates the slot_id query parameter.

Manual slot selection:
- Interaction: User clicks on a specific time slot in the slot list.
- Effect: Sets the clicked slot as selected, updates slot_id in the URL, and enables the booking form for that slot.

Booking form input:
- Interaction: User types into the name, email, and company fields, and selects a topic from a controlled list that maps to the DiscoveryCallBooking.topic enum.
- Effect: Component maintains a local form model; validation runs on blur and on submit (e.g., required fields, email format, topic selection).

Confirm booking submission:
- Interaction: User clicks the “Confirm booking” button.
- Effect: If form is valid and a slot is selected, calls bookDiscoveryCall. On success, stores the returned DiscoveryCallBooking and DiscoveryCallSlot objects in state, updates the URL to reflect a confirmation state (e.g., &booking_id=...), and renders the confirmation component. On failure, shows an error state in the booking form component.

Back to contact navigation:
- Interaction: User clicks “Back to contact” link either in the scheduler or in the confirmation view.
- Effect: Browser navigates to contact.html as specified in outgoing_connections; no scheduler state is preserved beyond any parameters passed by contact.html when it is re-opened.
page_functionality.state_logic:
On initial load, the page reads month, year, and time_zone from the URL query string. If any are missing, the scheduler derives sensible defaults (e.g., current month/year and a default time zone) without hardcoding specific values in the UI layer. These resolved values are used to call getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar. The calendar component is always in sync with the month, year, and time_zone reflected in the URL.

When the user changes the calendar month/year, the scheduler updates the month and year query parameters and reloads availability using getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar. Changing the time zone updates the time_zone query parameter and also triggers a fresh calendar fetch. If a date was previously selected (tracked via a date query parameter), getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate is re-run for that date and the new time_zone.

Selecting a date writes that date into the URL (e.g., date=YYYY-MM-DD) and triggers getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate with the current time_zone. The slot list is fully derived from that API response and never relies on static slot definitions. Selecting a slot writes slot_id into the URL so that the selected slot can be restored if the user reloads the page; the slot details are re-derived by re-calling getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate for the current date/time_zone and matching by slot_id.

The booking form state (name, email, company, topic) is kept in memory while the user is on the page and is not stored in the URL to avoid exposing sensitive data. On successful bookDiscoveryCall, the API response including the DiscoveryCallBooking and DiscoveryCallSlot is stored in state, and the page can optionally add a lightweight booking_id parameter to the URL for identification, while still rendering the confirmation from the live API response. At no point does the page rely on localStorage; all restorable state is encoded in URL parameters and re-hydrated via the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Parses and manages scheduler state from URL parameters (month, year, time_zone, date, slot_id, optional booking_id), triggers data loading via getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar and getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate, and updates the URL when the user changes calendar, time zone, or selection.

Allows the user to choose the time zone used for displaying availability and slots. The selected time zone is constrained to values supported by the backend and is used for all calls to getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar and getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate, and for selected_time_zone passed to bookDiscoveryCall.

Displays a month-level view of availability using the output from getDiscoveryCallAvailabilityCalendar, indicating which days are available and how many slots exist, including morning/afternoon counts. Allows navigation between months and selection of a specific date.

For the currently selected date and time zone, calls getDiscoveryCallSlotsForDate to list all available 30-minute DiscoveryCallSlot entries. Supports selection of a specific slot and provides a shortcut action to select the earliest available morning slot within the configured morning time window.

Collects booking details (name, email, company, topic) for the currently selected DiscoveryCallSlot. Validates required fields and maps the selected topic to the DiscoveryCallBooking.topic enum values. On submission, calls bookDiscoveryCall with slot_id, selected_time_zone, and the entered details.

Displays confirmation details after a successful bookDiscoveryCall response, using the returned DiscoveryCallBooking and DiscoveryCallSlot data (date, time, time_zone, and user-provided details). Provides a link to return to contact.html and may show a persistent booking reference derived from the booking entity.
Navigates the user back to contact.html when clicked, either from the main scheduler view or from the confirmation view, as defined in outgoing_connections.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the hero pattern as the scheduler container: a full-width, nearly full-height hero with a global nav bar on top (logo left, CTA/menu right) and a two-column hero body beneath. The left column is reserved for a bold, oversized headline and short explanatory copy about the discovery call. The right column becomes the interactive scheduler pane: first showing time-zone selector + calendar + time slots, then swapping this stack for the booking details form, and finally for the confirmation view. The back-to-contact link sits subtly under the left column, aligned to its left edge.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the hero pattern as the scheduler container: a full-width, nearly full-height hero with a global nav bar on top (logo left, CTA/menu right) and a two-column hero body beneath. The left column is reserved for a bold, oversized headline and short explanatory copy about the discovery call. The right column becomes the interactive scheduler pane: first showing time-zone selector + calendar + time slots, then swapping this stack for the booking details form, and finally for the confirmation view. The back-to-contact link sits subtly under the left column, aligned to its left edge.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero body is centered in a max-width container (around 1200–1320px) with 96px vertical padding and generous side gutters. The left column uses roughly 7 of 12 grid columns (~60–65% width) for the headline and supporting text. The right column uses about 5 of 12 columns (~35–40% width) for the scheduler UI. Within the right column, elements stack vertically: time-zone selector at the top, then calendar, then time slots, all surrounded by ample negative space so they never feel cramped. On later steps, the booking details form and confirmation view occupy the same right-column footprint, each with comfortable top/bottom padding and breathing room.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, premium feel by showing only one primary interactive step at a time in the right column. Each step has a limited number of elements: a compact selector or form, a small heading/instruction, and a single primary CTA. Vertical spacing between components follows the 8px scale (24–32px between stacked blocks, 16–24px between related fields). Large, quiet black areas around the headline and at the outer edges preserve the cinematic, minimalist vibe.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The flow mirrors the modified Z-pattern: the eye lands on the top-left logo in the nav, moves to the top-right CTA/menu, then drops down to the large left-side headline that introduces the discovery call. From there, attention naturally shifts right to the illuminated scheduler pane, reading top-down: time zone selector → calendar → available time slots → primary CTA. When the user advances, the pattern remains: headline left, active step right, with the back-to-contact link as a subtle escape route at the bottom-left, discovered after the core content.

layout.overall_description: The Discovery Call Scheduler page opens on a dark, full-width hero section that nearly fills the viewport height. A slim nav bar sits at the very top, with a colorful agency logo on the left and a bright magenta primary CTA plus circular menu icon on the right, all against a pure black background. Below, the main content sits inside a centered max-width band, divided into two clear columns. On the left, an oversized, two-line headline such as “Schedule Your Discovery Call” spans roughly two-thirds of the width, set in bold, high-contrast type with neon magenta and cyan accents. A single short line of supporting copy sits beneath it, both surrounded by generous black negative space. On the right, occupying the remaining third of the width, the scheduler UI forms a tidy, vertical stack: at the top a time-zone selector, then a compact availability calendar, and finally a list of selectable time slots, all edged with glowing accent states that echo the brand colors. When a time is chosen, this right-hand stack transitions to a clean booking details form in the same space; once submitted, it swaps to a confirmation view. Throughout, only one of these right-column states is visible at a time, preserving a calm, focused composition. At the very bottom of the left column, aligned with the left margin of the headline and sitting above the hero’s lower padding, a subtle “Back to Contact” link provides an understated path back, maintaining the minimalist, editorial feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scheduler-url-state-manager is a non-visual component positioned logically at the root of the hero’s main content area, conceptually spanning the full hero region but not occupying any visible space. It sits behind the scenes, just beneath the global navigation and wrapping the two-column hero body. It determines which interactive module is rendered in the right column: initially the time-zone selector + availability-calendar + date-slot-list stack, then the booking-details-form, and finally the booking-confirmation-view. From a layout perspective, it does not alter the grid or spacing; it simply toggles which right-column component group is visible at any moment while the left headline block and back-to-contact-link remain constant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state container that controls conditional rendering of right-column components; it does not consume layout space and behaves as a logical wrapper around the hero content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-zone-selector appears at the very top of the right-hand hero column, aligned with the top of the main hero content (roughly level with the midpoint of the left-side headline block, given the headline’s size). It sits inside the 5/12-width right column, centered within that column horizontally but left-aligned to its inner edge, spanning about 80% of the right-column width to leave visual breathing room on both sides. The selector consists of a short label above (or to the left on wider screens) and a pill-shaped dropdown input, with approximately 16–24px padding inside and 24px of vertical margin below before the calendar begins. There is 32–40px of empty black space between the selector and the right column’s top boundary, as well as between the selector and the left column, preserving the spacious, premium layout. The selector is visible only in the initial scheduling step; when the user progresses to the booking-details-form or booking-confirmation-view, it is removed from view and its vertical space collapses, so the new content starts near the same top line of the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column at the top of the interactive stack during the availability-selection step; it is conditionally rendered and removed entirely in later steps without leaving residual spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-calendar sits directly beneath the time-zone-selector in the right column, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space on the 8px grid. It spans nearly the full width of the right column (around 90–100% of that column), with its left edge aligned to the selector’s left edge so they appear as a single vertical stack. Vertically, the calendar occupies about one-third to half of the right column’s height, leaving ample black space both above (toward the selector) and below (toward the time slots). Its top aligns visually a bit above the midpoint of the left headline block, pulling attention rightward after the headline is read. The calendar’s header (month navigation) is compact; individual days are laid out in a clean grid with generous cell padding (8–12px inside each cell) and clear hover/selected states in magenta/cyan, echoing the hero’s neon aesthetic. On the initial step, the calendar is a central visual anchor of the right column; once a date/time is confirmed and the flow advances, the calendar disappears entirely, and its vertical space is taken over by the booking-details-form or confirmation view to keep the right column height visually consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column during the availability step, anchored between the time-zone-selector and the date-slot-list; conditionally hidden and removed from the layout when subsequent steps are active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date-slot-list is placed beneath the availability-calendar in the right column, with about 24–32px of vertical separation to maintain the airy spacing. It stretches across the same width as the calendar, aligned to its left edge and capped at the same right-column boundary, occupying roughly the lower third of the right column during the availability step. Time slots are arranged in a vertical list or in two subtle columns depending on available space, but never filling the entire height; the component leaves at least 32–48px of black space between its bottom and the hero’s lower padding to avoid a cramped feeling. Each time slot appears as a pill-shaped button, with generous internal padding (8–12px vertically, 16–24px horizontally) and clear spacing (8–12px) between slots. A primary “Continue” or “Confirm Time” CTA is anchored at the bottom of this component, aligned right within the right column and visually distinct in a bright magenta, sitting about 16–24px below the last slot. When the user selects a time and proceeds, the entire date-slot-list stack is replaced by the booking-details-form; the right column retains a similar overall height so the page doesn’t feel like it’s jumping.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column at the bottom of the availability step’s stack, growing vertically as needed but constrained to keep comfortable space above the hero’s lower padding; fully hidden and removed from the layout when later steps are active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-details-form occupies the same right-column area where the time-zone-selector, availability-calendar, and date-slot-list previously appeared. When active, it snaps into place at the top of the right column, leaving approximately 32–40px margin from the right column’s top boundary and aligning its left edge with the former selector/calendar edge. The form spans about 80–90% of the right column width, with clear, stacked fields (e.g., name, email, company, notes) separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. A short step title or micro-heading (e.g., “Enter your details”) sits above the first field, 8–16px apart, with a subtle, neon-accent underline to tie into the brand. The entire form block occupies roughly half to two-thirds of the right column’s height, leaving comfortable negative space below; the primary submit CTA is positioned at the bottom-right of the form fields, about 24px above the hero’s lower padding. The left headline remains unchanged and visually dominant, while the form reads as a focused, secondary panel. No elements from the previous step remain visible; the form fully replaces the calendar/slots for a clean, single-task view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered panel that replaces the calendar/slots stack within the right column. It is static and scroll-free on typical desktop sizes, relying on spacious vertical layout; on smaller viewports it may cause the page to scroll, but it remains within the same two-column structure until breakpoints trigger a single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-view appears in the right column after successful submission of the booking-details-form, occupying the same footprint as the previous interactive steps. It is aligned to the top of the right column with a 32–40px margin from the top and 80–90% width within that column, mirroring the form’s alignment. The confirmation content is minimal and bold: a prominent confirmation headline (e.g., “You’re booked”) sits at the top, sized smaller than the main page headline but larger than body text, followed by a concise summary block listing the chosen date, time, and time zone. This summary may be presented as a compact, card-like strip with subtle neon outline or accent on the left edge. Below, there is a short line of reassurance or next steps and possibly a subtle secondary action (e.g., “Add to calendar”) aligned to the right. Vertically, the confirmation view uses less space than the form—roughly one-third to half of the right column’s height—deliberately leaving a generous band of empty black space beneath to convey completion and calm. The left column headline remains visible and unchanged, framing the confirmation as the final step in the same hero context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered final-state panel that replaces the form entirely within the right column. It remains static and compact, with no scrolling required under normal desktop conditions, emphasizing a clean finish to the flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-contact-link is placed in the lower-left area of the hero, directly beneath the left column’s supporting text and aligned with the left edge of the main headline. There is a substantial vertical gap—approximately 32–48px—between the last line of the left column copy and this link, so it reads as a subtle, secondary affordance rather than competing with the primary content. Horizontally, it sits well away from the right column, with at least one full grid column of negative space separating it from the scheduler pane. The link is styled in a low-contrast but legible tone (e.g., muted gray or a very subtle cyan), with a simple underline on hover, maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. It occupies minimal vertical height and sits around 48–64px above the hero’s bottom padding, creating a calm buffer of black space below. On narrower screens where columns stack, the link remains below the headline block and above the scheduler content, still aligned left and maintaining a generous top margin to avoid crowding the interactive elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text link anchored under the left-column content within the hero; it does not stick or float but moves naturally with the page scroll, always remaining associated with the left text block.

name: UX Research & Testing
title: UX Research & Testing
description: Page for exploring UX research and testing services, configuring hourly engagements, previewing rates, and submitting an inquiry.

core_features: Load and display UX research and testing service content, including activities and outcomes, using getUxResearchServiceContent.
Provide a pricing section with a toggle between project-based and hourly views, controlled by URL parameters.
Load available UX hourly pricing tiers and notes using getUxHourlyPricingTiers when hourly pricing is active.
Allow users to set an estimated number of hours and preview the applicable hourly pricing tier and effective hourly rate via getUxHourlyRatePreview.
Let users specify a desired hourly rate cap and view which available tiers and commitment options can meet that cap, using data from getUxHourlyPricingTiers.
Expose a 'Talk to us about this plan' interaction that shows or hides an inquiry form, with visibility tracked through URL parameters.
Provide an inquiry form where users choose a subject, write a message, enter contact details, and optionally include estimated hours, a desired hourly rate cap, and a selected pricing tier.
Submit the UX hourly engagement inquiry using submitUxHourlyInquiry and display the returned success or error message.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View UX research & testing service details
1. User navigates to ux_research.html (optionally with URL parameters).
2. On page load, the page calls getUxResearchServiceContent.
3. The service content component renders the returned intro_heading, intro_copy, typical_activities list, and outcomes list.
4. User can read the content; no API calls are triggered by reading.

Workflow 2: Switch between project-based and hourly pricing
1. On initial load, the page reads the pricing_mode URL parameter.
2. If pricing_mode is missing or invalid, a default mode is set in state and reflected in the URL (without calling any additional APIs).
3. The pricing-mode toggle component shows the active mode based on state/URL.
4. When the user clicks the project-based tab, pricing_mode is updated in state and the URL is updated (e.g., ?pricing_mode=project); project-based descriptive content is shown, and no hourly APIs are called.
5. When the user clicks the hourly tab, pricing_mode is updated in state and the URL (e.g., ?pricing_mode=hourly); hourly pricing components become visible and hourly APIs may be invoked.

Workflow 3: Explore hourly pricing tiers and preview rate by hours
1. With pricing_mode=hourly active, the hourly pricing loader calls getUxHourlyPricingTiers once and stores the returned tiers and hourly_pricing_notes in state.
2. The tiers are displayed in a table or list, showing fields from each UxHourlyPricingTier (such as name, min_hours, max_hours, base_hourly_rate, discounted_hourly_rate when present, and requires_commitment_description when present).
3. The user sets an estimated hours value using a numeric input or slider.
4. On each valid change of estimated hours, the estimated_hours state and the hours URL parameter are updated.
5. The component calls getUxHourlyRatePreview with the current estimated_hours.
6. When getUxHourlyRatePreview returns, the page displays the effective_hourly_rate and the applicable_tier data, and stores applicable_tier.id in state so that it can be referenced by other components (such as the inquiry form).
7. If the user navigates away and later returns with a hours parameter in the URL, the page initializes estimated_hours from the URL and immediately calls getUxHourlyRatePreview so the preview is populated automatically.

Workflow 4: Adjust desired hourly rate cap and view commitment/discount options
1. The user enters a desired hourly rate cap value in a numeric input control.
2. The desired_hourly_rate_cap value is stored in state and synchronized to the URL (e.g., ?rate_cap=... along with any existing parameters).
3. The discount options component uses the in-memory tiers from getUxHourlyPricingTiers and the desired_hourly_rate_cap state to compute which tiers have a base_hourly_rate or discounted_hourly_rate less than or equal to the cap.
4. The component displays these qualifying tiers along with their requires_commitment_description (when provided) so the user can see what commitment is needed to reach or stay under their desired cap.
5. The user may optionally choose one of the displayed tiers as the preferred tier for their inquiry. The selected pricing_tier_id is stored in state and synchronized to the URL (e.g., ?tier_id=...).
6. No new API calls are required during this workflow; it operates entirely on existing tier data and local state.

Workflow 5: Open the inquiry form for an hourly UX engagement
1. The user clicks the 'Talk to us about this plan' button in the hourly pricing area.
2. The page sets a boolean state (e.g., show_inquiry_form=true) and updates the URL to include a parameter like show_inquiry=1 (preserving any existing parameters such as pricing_mode, hours, rate_cap, tier_id).
3. The inquiry form component becomes visible.
4. If the user has already selected a pricing tier from the discount options or has an applicable_tier from the last rate preview, the form pre-fills a hidden or read-only reference to that pricing_tier_id.
5. The form also pre-fills estimated_hours and desired_hourly_rate_cap fields in the payload (not necessarily visible inputs) from the current state values.
6. If the user landed on the page with show_inquiry=1 in the URL, the form is shown automatically on load, and state is initialized accordingly.

Workflow 6: Complete and submit the hourly inquiry form
1. The user selects a subject from a dropdown that offers the enum options defined by UxHourlyInquiry.subject (ux_research_hourly_engagement, general_ux_research_question, usability_testing_project).
2. The user enters a free-text message describing their needs, including any context about UX research, testing, or specific questions.
3. The user fills in required contact fields: contact_name and contact_email.
4. The form uses current state to include optional values: pricing_tier_id (if any selected), estimated_hours (from the estimator), and desired_hourly_rate_cap (from the rate cap input).
5. On clicking the 'Send inquiry' button, client-side validation ensures required fields and enum selections are present and well-formed.
6. If validation passes, the form calls submitUxHourlyInquiry with pricing_tier_id (or null/undefined if none), subject, message, contact_name, contact_email, estimated_hours, and desired_hourly_rate_cap.
7. When submitUxHourlyInquiry returns, the page checks the success flag:
   - If success is true, the page can show the returned message from the API and optionally display read-only details from the inquiry object (without altering them).
   - If success is false, the page can display the returned message as an error.
8. Optionally, the page can update the URL with a parameter such as inquiry_submitted=1 so that a success notice can be re-shown on refresh without resubmitting the form (the page must not re-call submitUxHourlyInquiry automatically based solely on this parameter).

Workflow 7: Restore state from URL when revisiting or sharing a link
1. When the page loads, it reads URL parameters such as pricing_mode, hours, show_inquiry, rate_cap, tier_id, subject, and inquiry_submitted (if present).
2. The initial page state is set from these parameters (after validation), so that:
   - The correct pricing tab is active.
   - The estimated hours input shows the hours value.
   - The hourly rate preview is fetched for the specified hours using getUxHourlyRatePreview.
   - The desired hourly rate cap input shows the rate_cap value, which immediately filters available tiers in the discount options component.
   - The selected tier in the discount options panel corresponds to tier_id if it matches an available tier.
   - The inquiry form is shown or hidden based on show_inquiry.
   - The subject dropdown defaults to the subject parameter if it is a valid enum option.
3. This makes it possible to share a link that preserves the user’s configuration without relying on localStorage.
interactions: Click on pricing-mode toggle buttons: switches between project-based and hourly pricing modes. Updates internal state and the pricing_mode URL parameter, without calling any new APIs beyond those tied to the active mode.

Change estimated hours input (slider or numeric field): updates estimated_hours in state, synchronizes the hours URL parameter, and triggers a debounced call to getUxHourlyRatePreview with the current estimated_hours.

Receive getUxHourlyRatePreview response: updates the hourly rate preview display and stores the applicable_tier object (including id) in state for use by other components (e.g., inquiry form default tier selection).

Initial load of hourly pricing: triggers getUxHourlyPricingTiers once when pricing_mode=hourly becomes active; the tiers result is cached in state for use by multiple components (tier list, discount options, inquiry form).

Change desired hourly rate cap input: updates desired_hourly_rate_cap in state, synchronizes the rate_cap URL parameter, and causes the discount options component to recompute which tiers meet the cap using existing tier data.

Select a specific tier as preferred: user selects a tier (for example by clicking a 'Select this tier' control in the discount options panel); this updates a selected_pricing_tier_id in state and the tier_id URL parameter for use by the inquiry form.

Click 'Talk to us about this plan' button: toggles the visibility of the inquiry form, sets show_inquiry in state, and updates the show_inquiry URL parameter accordingly (without losing other parameters).
Change subject dropdown in the inquiry form: updates the subject field in state and may also update a subject URL parameter so the selection can be restored on reload.

Type in inquiry message, contact_name, and contact_email fields: updates the corresponding state fields used to construct the submitUxHourlyInquiry call; these are not required to be synced to URL parameters.

Click 'Send inquiry' button: performs client-side validation and, on success, calls submitUxHourlyInquiry with the current form and configuration values. The page then reacts to the response by showing the returned message and possibly marking the inquiry as submitted in state and via a URL parameter.

Navigate via browser back/forward buttons: triggers state synchronization from the updated URL (e.g., different pricing_mode, hours, show_inquiry), re-invokes getUxHourlyRatePreview if needed, and re-renders the page accordingly without new manual user input.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven by URL parameters and in-memory values derived from API responses. No localStorage is used.

Key URL parameters and their effects:
- pricing_mode: determines whether project-based or hourly pricing content is visible. Accepted values are limited by client logic (e.g., 'project' or 'hourly'). If missing or invalid, a default mode is chosen and reflected in the URL.
- hours: if present and valid, initializes the estimated_hours state for the hourly pricing flow. When pricing_mode=hourly and hours is set, the page calls getUxHourlyRatePreview(estimated_hours=hours) on load.
- rate_cap: if present and valid, initializes the desired_hourly_rate_cap state and the corresponding input field. The discount options component uses this value to filter tiers from getUxHourlyPricingTiers without additional API calls.
- tier_id: if present and matching an id from the tiers returned by getUxHourlyPricingTiers, initializes the selected_pricing_tier_id state. This value is used as the default pricing_tier_id in the inquiry form submission. If the tiers have not yet been loaded, the association occurs after the getUxHourlyPricingTiers promise resolves.
- show_inquiry: if present and truthy according to client rules (for example, '1' or 'true'), the inquiry form is shown on initial load. If absent or falsy, the form is hidden until the user clicks the 'Talk to us about this plan' button.
- subject: if present and matching a valid UxHourlyInquiry.subject enum value, initializes the subject selection in the inquiry form's dropdown.
- inquiry_submitted: if present and truthy, the page may display a submission confirmation message, but must not re-call submitUxHourlyInquiry based solely on this parameter.

URL synchronization rules:
- When the user changes a stateful control that should be shareable (pricing mode, estimated hours, rate cap, selected tier, inquiry form visibility, and optionally subject), the page updates the URL parameters using the History API (e.g., history.replaceState) to avoid a full reload.
- URL updates preserve unrelated parameters whenever possible so that configurations remain consistent; for example, updating hours does not remove pricing_mode or show_inquiry.
- On every page load or popstate event (back/forward navigation), the page re-parses the URL parameters and reconstructs state, then re-fetches data as required (getUxResearchServiceContent always; getUxHourlyPricingTiers and getUxHourlyRatePreview when relevant).

components.functionality: Fetches UX research and testing service content on page load using getUxResearchServiceContent and provides the resulting data to child components responsible for rendering the introduction, typical activities, and outcomes.

Displays the service introduction, typical activities, and outcomes returned by getUxResearchServiceContent. It renders intro_heading, intro_copy, and lists of typical_activities and outcomes, without adding or modifying the textual content.
Lets the user switch between project-based and hourly pricing views. Updates internal state and the pricing_mode URL parameter, and shows or hides the hourly pricing components accordingly.

When pricing_mode=hourly is active, calls getUxHourlyPricingTiers once, stores the returned tiers and hourly_pricing_notes in state, and makes them available to pricing display and discount components.

Displays the list of hourly pricing tiers retrieved from getUxHourlyPricingTiers. Shows each tier's fields such as name, min_hours, max_hours, base_hourly_rate, discounted_hourly_rate (if provided), and requires_commitment_description (if provided), and presents hourly_pricing_notes. Allows the user to select a tier as their preferred option for an inquiry.

Provides an input (such as a slider and/or numeric field) for the user to specify estimated_hours for an hourly engagement. On change, it updates the estimated_hours state, syncs the hours URL parameter, and triggers a debounced call to getUxHourlyRatePreview with the new estimated_hours value.

Shows the current hourly rate preview based on the latest getUxHourlyRatePreview response. Displays effective_hourly_rate and key fields from applicable_tier, and stores applicable_tier.id into shared state for use by other components such as the inquiry form and tier selection synchronization.

Allows the user to enter a desired_hourly_rate_cap. Updates state and the rate_cap URL parameter. Triggers recomputation in the discount-options-panel to determine which tiers from getUxHourlyPricingTiers can meet the desired cap based on base_hourly_rate or discounted_hourly_rate.

Uses the tiers data from getUxHourlyPricingTiers and the desired_hourly_rate_cap state to compute and display tiers whose rates meet the desired cap. Shows relevant tier details and requires_commitment_description. Allows the user to choose a tier as selected_pricing_tier_id, which is then stored in state and reflected in the tier_id URL parameter.

Provides the 'Talk to us about this plan' interaction. When clicked, toggles the visibility of the hourly inquiry form, updates show_inquiry in state, and synchronizes the show_inquiry URL parameter without triggering API calls directly.

Collects inquiry details for an hourly UX research or testing engagement. Uses subject (enum), message, contact_name, and contact_email as required fields. Optionally includes pricing_tier_id (from selected tier or applicable_tier), estimated_hours (from the estimator), and desired_hourly_rate_cap (from rate-cap input). On submit, calls submitUxHourlyInquiry with these values and displays the response message and success state.

Displays the outcome of the submitUxHourlyInquiry call, including the message and success status from the response. May also reference fields from the returned inquiry object for confirmation. Can use the inquiry_submitted URL parameter to decide whether to show a success notice on reload without resubmitting.

Central component responsible for reading URL parameters on load and on browser navigation events, initializing page state (pricing_mode, hours, rate_cap, tier_id, show_inquiry, subject, inquiry_submitted), and updating the URL when these state values change due to user interaction. Ensures that getUxHourlyRatePreview and getUxHourlyPricingTiers are called only when needed based on current state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow, starting with a full-width, nearly viewport-height hero section on a pure black background. Within a centered max-width container, the hero uses a 12-column grid: the left 7/12 is reserved for the massive page title and intro copy (not part of the component list), while the right 5/12 holds the ux-service-summary content. Below the hero, a second full-width band handles pricing and configuration in another two-column layout: the left 7/12 for pricing context and tier list, the right 5/12 for the calculator inputs, previews, discounts, and inquiry CTA/form. Invisible infrastructure components (loaders, URL state manager) sit logically on top of or around these sections but do not disturb the visual grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow, starting with a full-width, nearly viewport-height hero section on a pure black background. Within a centered max-width container, the hero uses a 12-column grid: the left 7/12 is reserved for the massive page title and intro copy (not part of the component list), while the right 5/12 holds the ux-service-summary content. Below the hero, a second full-width band handles pricing and configuration in another two-column layout: the left 7/12 for pricing context and tier list, the right 5/12 for the calculator inputs, previews, discounts, and inquiry CTA/form. Invisible infrastructure components (loaders, URL state manager) sit logically on top of or around these sections but do not disturb the visual grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 90–100% of the initial viewport height. The navigation bar (outside the given components) forms a slim band at the top; the remaining vertical space is given to the hero’s two-column content with generous top and bottom margins. The pricing/configurator section below also uses a tall band, about 80–100% of the viewport height, with 96px-ish vertical separation from the hero. Horizontally, primary columns follow ~60–65% width for left columns and ~35–40% for right columns, maintaining strong alignment to the same max-width container. Each column’s content is padded inward by 48–64px from the container edges, and core blocks are separated by 24–48px vertical gaps to keep the premium spacious feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low-density and editorial. Each major section contains a small number of key elements with deliberate spacing: the hero right column shows only the ux-service-summary content and any trust messaging; the pricing/configurator section shows a clear vertical stack of controls and panels rather than dense form fields. Inputs and panels are separated by 16–32px gaps, with comfortable line heights and breathing room, avoiding visual clutter. Loaders appear as minimal, centered overlays rather than complex spinners with extra copy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The flow mirrors the modified Z-pattern from the mockup. The user first sees the logo and top-right global CTA (in the nav), then is drawn diagonally down to the large page headline on the left of the hero. From there, the eye moves right to the ux-service-summary in the hero’s right column, which explains UX Research & Testing services and sets trust. Scrolling slightly, the flow enters the pricing/configurator band: attention starts at the top left with the pricing-mode-toggle and section heading, then moves down the hourly-pricing-tier-list, then diagonally to the right column where the estimated-hours-input and desired-rate-cap-input sit above the hourly-rate-preview-panel and discount-options-panel.
