This naturally ends on the bold talk-to-us-button and the ux-hourly-inquiry-form, where inquiry-submission-status feedback appears after submission.

layout.overall_description: The page opens on a full-width, near full-height hero set against a deep, pure black background with neon magenta and cyan accents. A slim, fixed top navigation bar spans the width; below it, a centered max-width content band is split into two columns. On the left, an oversized, two-line page title like “UX Research & Testing” dominates the space in bright, high-contrast type, with ample negative space above, below, and to the sides. On the right, aligning to the same baseline, a narrower column holds the ux-service-summary—compact paragraphs describing your UX research and testing services—with room left beneath it for a trust badge or subtle credibility element, echoing the original mockup’s badge placement. Below this hero, after a generous vertical gap, a second wide section appears in the same dark aesthetic: on the left, the pricing-mode-toggle and hourly-pricing-tier-list stack in a tall, airy column; on the right, the configuration tools form a vertical calculator stack—estimated-hours-input and desired-rate-cap-input at the top, then the hourly-rate-preview-panel and discount-options-panel, and finally a large, bright talk-to-us-button leading into the ux-hourly-inquiry-form. The inquiry-submission-status sits tightly coupled to the form, lightly indicating progress or success. Throughout, content is aligned to a 12-column grid, with 60–65% width left columns and 35–40% right columns, generous 64–96px outer padding, and plenty of black negative space so that each neon-accented element feels deliberate, modern, and premium.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ux-service-content-loader is an invisible overlay scoped to the hero’s right column area where the ux-service-summary lives. Visually, when active, it appears as a semi-transparent, slightly blurred dark layer covering roughly the right 35–40% of the hero’s main content band, starting just below the top navigation and extending down to the bottom padding of the hero content. It is centered within that column both horizontally and vertically, presenting either a subtle spinner or loading indicator without introducing additional framing. It does not extend into the left headline column, preserving the dramatic visibility of the page title even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned absolutely within the hero container, anchored to the right column bounds. It overlays ux-service-summary when loading and disappears entirely when content is ready, reclaiming no permanent space in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ux-service-summary occupies the hero’s right column within the main max-width container. It spans approximately 5 of 12 grid columns, aligned flush with the container’s right edge, and begins roughly at the vertical midpoint of the hero, aligned horizontally with the baseline of the large left headline. The content is a compact stack of 2–3 paragraphs: the top paragraph sits about 24–32px below the top of this right column, with comfortable 24px vertical spacing between lines of text blocks. The overall column has about 48–64px padding from the top navigation and 64–96px from the bottom of the hero, leaving large black negative space above and below. Any trust badge or short supporting line can sit 24–32px beneath the main summary text, still within this right column, echoing the mockup’s small badge area. The left edge of this column aligns visually with about 60–65% of the container width, leaving a clear vertical gutter between the headline and this summary for breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, participating fully in the 12-column grid. It reflows to stack beneath the headline on narrower screens but remains right-aligned within the container on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing-mode-toggle is placed at the top-left of the pricing & configuration section, just below the hero. It sits within the main container, aligned to the same left edge as the hero headline, occupying roughly 2–3 grid columns in width. There is a generous vertical gap of about 80–96px between the bottom of the hero and the top of this toggle, maintaining a clear visual separation between storytelling and configuration. The toggle itself feels like a slim, neon-accented pill or segmented control with about 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding on each side, surrounded by at least 24px of empty black space above and below before other pricing content appears. This control anchors the left column of the second section and visually signals the start of the interactive segment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the pricing section. On long-scroll pages it will scroll away normally; it does not stick, but its alignment remains tied to the left 7/12 pricing column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hourly-pricing-loader overlays only the left pricing column that contains the pricing-mode-toggle and hourly-pricing-tier-list. When active, it manifests as a subtle dark veil stretching from just below the pricing-mode-toggle down to cover the vertical space allocated to the hourly-pricing-tier-list, roughly 7 of 12 grid columns wide and as tall as the tier content. A centered loading indicator appears about one-third down from the top of this column, ensuring it’s immediately visible without overwhelming the area. The right calculator column remains fully visible and interactive, reinforcing that loading pertains only to the tier data.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay inside the pricing section’s left column, covering the hourly-pricing-tier-list region while data is fetched. It is removed entirely once loading completes, freeing the underlying layout with no residual space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hourly-pricing-tier-list is the main content block of the pricing section’s left column. It sits directly beneath the pricing-mode-toggle with a vertical gap of about 32–40px. It spans approximately 7 of 12 grid columns, aligned flush to the left container edge, and runs vertically for 50–70% of the pricing section’s height depending on content. Each tier is presented as a minimalist horizontal card or row with clear separation—24–32px vertical spacing between tiers—and generous left padding from the column edge (16–24px) to avoid feeling crowded. The list’s rightmost content aligns with the imaginary vertical line that divides the 7/12 left column and the 5/12 right column, leaving a distinct central gutter of empty black space before the right calculator column begins. The overall list has ample bottom margin (48–64px) before the end of the section or any subsequent content, preserving the airy, premium layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling naturally as part of the main page flow. On very tall viewports, it may not fill the entire height, leaving intentional negative space below to match the low-density aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimated-hours-input is the first interactive element in the pricing section’s right column. This right column begins at the same vertical level as the pricing-mode-toggle but is positioned to the right, spanning about 5 of 12 grid columns and separated from the left column by a consistent gutter (roughly one grid column). The estimated-hours-input is placed at the top of this right column, aligned to its left edge, with about 8–16px of internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside the input field. Above it, there can be a concise label; below it, there is a 24–32px gap before the next control. The field stretches to occupy about 70–80% of the right column width, leaving some breathing room on the right margin. This placement makes it the primary entry point for the calculator, immediately noticeable once the user’s gaze shifts from left pricing tiers to the right configuration stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, part of a vertical stack of controls. On narrow screens it will stack beneath the tier list but maintain comfortable vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hourly-rate-preview-panel sits directly below the primary input controls in the right column, acting as a visual anchor for the user’s configuration. It appears after the estimated-hours-input and desired-rate-cap-input, separated from them by a combined 32–40px of vertical spacing. The panel occupies most of the right column width (about 85–90%), with 16–24px internal padding on all sides, and is framed subtly—either by a faint outline or a neon-accented top border—to stand out against the black background. It is roughly card-like in height, taking up 20–30% of the right column’s vertical space, leaving ample negative space above and below. The content inside is vertically stacked (e.g., a label, the previewed rate, and perhaps a short explanation), with 8–16px gaps between lines, maintaining the minimalist, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a fixed place in the right column’s vertical flow. As content within updates, the panel height may grow slightly but is designed to remain compact and not push other key elements too far off viewport on typical desktop heights.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The desired-rate-cap-input is positioned in the right column just below the estimated-hours-input, forming the second key field in the calculator stack. It is separated from the estimated-hours-input by about 24px of vertical space, mirroring the spacing pattern of the mockup’s clean stacks. The field’s width matches the estimated-hours-input, occupying about 70–80% of the right column width and aligned to the left edge. It may sit side-by-side with a compact label or hint text but overall remains visually simple, with generous internal padding (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). This placement keeps both quantity (hours) and constraint (rate cap) logically grouped at the top of the calculator, just above the hourly-rate-preview-panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the right column’s vertical stack. On smaller screens it may stack directly under the estimated-hours-input with increased vertical spacing (32px) to maintain clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discount-options-panel is placed directly below the hourly-rate-preview-panel within the right column, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It is slightly smaller and visually lighter than the preview panel, occupying around 75–85% of the right column’s width. Internally, any discount options or explanatory lines are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing, and the panel uses subtle borders or typographic hierarchy rather than heavy blocks to keep density low. Its bottom edge sits 32–40px above the talk-to-us-button, ensuring a clear visual break before the main CTA. Overall, it reads as supportive information—easily scannable but not dominating the calculator zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the column. It expands slightly if multiple discount options are present, but the surrounding negative space absorbs this without crowding adjacent elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The talk-to-us-button is the dominant CTA in the pricing/configurator area and mirrors the hero’s bold CTA style. It sits in the right column directly beneath the discount-options-panel, with a generous 32–40px gap above it. The button spans a substantial portion of the right column width—about 60–80%—centered or left-aligned depending on the visual system, but always with ample black space flanking it on both sides. Vertically, the button is tall, with 12–16px padding above and below the label and 24–32px horizontally, and it uses a vivid neon magenta or cyan fill to sharply contrast the black background. Below the button, a 24–32px gap is reserved before the ux-hourly-inquiry-form, signaling that the button is the primary next action, while the form is an extension for users ready to provide details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sitting in the flow of the right column. On smaller screens, it remains visually prominent by spanning nearly the full width of the content column, but still with slight lateral padding from the screen edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ux-hourly-inquiry-form is positioned directly beneath the talk-to-us-button in the right column, with a deliberate 24–32px vertical gap so it feels connected but secondary. The form is arranged as a vertical stack of a few key fields (name, email, brief project description, etc.), each using the same padded, minimalist style as other inputs: 8–12px vertical padding, 16–24px horizontal, and clear 16–24px spacing between fields. The form’s total width aligns with the button above, occupying about 70–80% of the right column width and aligned with the column’s left edge. The entire form block takes up the lower third of the right column’s vertical space, leaving at least 48–64px of black margin at the bottom of the section on typical desktop viewports. This gives the feeling of a compact, intentional panel rather than a long, cumbersome form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appearing in the normal scroll flow. If you choose to make it appear conditionally (e.g., after button click), it would slide into this reserved space without shifting the column’s overall structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inquiry-submission-status component is tightly coupled to the ux-hourly-inquiry-form and appears directly beneath it within the right column. It occupies a slim horizontal band spanning roughly the same width as the form (70–80% of the column) and sits just 16–24px below the last form field or submit interaction. The message is short and uses subtle but clear styling—e.g., a small neon accent icon plus text—without any heavy background that would disrupt the minimal feel. In success state, it may use a soft cyan accent; in error state, a more intense magenta-red, both maintaining high contrast against the black background. There is at least 32–40px of empty space between this status line and the bottom edge of the section, so it doesn’t feel cramped against the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in terms of position, but conditional in visibility. When hidden, the space collapses, and the bottom margin of the right column closes up slightly; when shown, it expands the column by one compact text line plus minimal padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-synchronizer has no direct visual representation. Conceptually, it sits at the page level, watching and updating the URL query parameters based on the user’s interactions with the pricing-mode-toggle, estimated-hours-input, desired-rate-cap-input, and possibly the hourly-pricing-tier-list filters. It does not occupy any space within the hero or pricing layout and does not affect the visual grid or spacing. Its presence is only inferred through behaviors like deep-linking and preserved state on refresh.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, with zero spatial footprint. It behaves like a background controller tied to the root of the page or the pricing section container, updating URL state without altering any physical layout.

name: Branding & Visual Identity
title: Branding & Visual Identity
description: Service detail page where users can understand branding services, review deliverables and process, compare branding packages, and navigate to specific package detail pages.

core_features: Load and display the branding service introduction, key deliverables, and process overview using getBrandingServiceContent.
Load active branding packages for comparison using getBrandingPackagesComparison and present them in a comparison table.
Provide a pricing summary section with a 'Compare packages' button that updates the URL and scrolls to or reveals the comparison table on the same page.
Display a comparison table of branding packages with key inclusions such as number_of_logo_concepts and includes_brand_guidelines, using fields from BrandingPackage.
Visually indicate and optionally filter packages that include at least 3 logo concepts and a brand guidelines document (number_of_logo_concepts >= 3 and includes_brand_guidelines == true).
Provide 'View details' buttons/links per package that navigate to branding_package.html with the package slug as a URL parameter.
Use URL parameters to control which section is focused (e.g., pricing vs. comparison), whether the comparison is expanded, which feature filter is applied, and which package (if any) is focused.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Understand Branding & Visual Identity service:
1. User navigates to branding.html (via footer link or Services Overview page).
2. Page initializes and calls getBrandingServiceContent.
3. The introduction, key_deliverables list, and process_overview steps are rendered.
4. User scrolls through the content to understand what is included in the service and how the process works.
5. If the user wants to see pricing options, they scroll to or jump to the pricing summary section (optionally via a navigation link or CTA on the page).

Workflow 2 – Compare packages and identify those with ≥3 logo concepts and brand guidelines:
1. User reaches the pricing summary section, which is driven by data from getBrandingPackagesComparison and summarizes available packages.
2. User clicks the 'Compare packages' button.
3. The button’s handler updates the URL parameter (e.g., section=comparison) and scrolls to or expands the comparison table component.
4. The comparison table is populated using data returned from getBrandingPackagesComparison.packages, showing at least: name, one_time_price (and/or monthly_price if present), number_of_logo_concepts, includes_brand_guidelines, and other boolean inclusions.
5. The table automatically highlights packages where number_of_logo_concepts >= 3 and includes_brand_guidelines is true (e.g., via an icon, label, or row state) and optionally applies an active-state marker in the legend.
6. User can optionally enable a filter control (e.g., 'Show only packages with ≥3 logo concepts & brand guidelines'), which updates the URL filter parameter and causes the table to only display qualifying packages.
7. User reviews the reduced set of packages to quickly identify those that meet the specified criteria.

Workflow 3 – Navigate from comparison table to a specific package detail page:
1. While viewing the comparison table (populated from getBrandingPackagesComparison), each package row includes a 'View details' button.
2. When the user clicks 'View details' for a specific package, the event handler reads that package’s slug from the BrandingPackage.slug field.
3. The browser navigates to branding_package.html, appending the slug as a URL parameter (e.g., branding_package.html?slug={package.slug}).
4. The package detail page then uses the slug parameter (according to its own configuration) to load and display full details for that specific package.

Workflow 4 – Deep-link into pricing or comparison using URL parameters:
1. User clicks a link from another page or an external source that points to branding.html with URL parameters (e.g., branding.html?section=comparison&filter=logo3_guidelines).
2. On load, the page-state logic parses URL parameters before or alongside the data calls.
3. The page calls getBrandingServiceContent and getBrandingPackagesComparison as usual.
4. Once data is available, the page automatically expands or scrolls to the comparison table because section=comparison.
5. The filter parameter (e.g., filter=logo3_guidelines) causes the comparison table to pre-apply the filter where number_of_logo_concepts >= 3 and includes_brand_guidelines is true.
6. User arrives directly in a focused, filtered state without manual interactions, but can still clear or change the filter and navigation state via UI controls, which in turn update the URL parameters.
interactions:
Click 'Compare packages' button:
- Trigger: User clicks the compare button in the pricing summary section.
- Behavior: Updates the URL parameter (e.g., section=comparison) using the History API, ensures the comparison table component is set to an expanded/visible state, and scrolls the viewport to the comparison table’s anchor element.

Toggle 'Show only packages with ≥3 logo concepts & brand guidelines' filter:
- Trigger: User toggles a filter control in or above the comparison table.
- Behavior: Sets or removes a URL parameter (e.g., filter=logo3_guidelines). When enabled, the comparison table component filters the list of packages from getBrandingPackagesComparison to those where number_of_logo_concepts >= 3 and includes_brand_guidelines == true. When disabled, it returns to showing all packages.
- The component re-renders the rows based on the filtered set.

Click 'View details' in a package row:
- Trigger: User clicks 'View details' for a specific package in the comparison table.
- Behavior: Reads the slug from the BrandingPackage.slug field of that row’s package and navigates the browser to branding_package.html with ?slug={slug} or an equivalent query parameter configuration. No additional APIs are called on this page as part of the click; the navigation hands off control to the detail page.

Scroll-based section activation:
- Trigger: User manually scrolls the page.
- Behavior: As different sections (intro, deliverables, process, pricing, comparison) cross a visibility threshold, the page may update the URL section parameter (e.g., section=intro, section=pricing) without a page reload, so that the URL always reflects the user’s active section. This enables deep-linking back into the same section later.

Initial load with URL parameters:
- Trigger: Page loads or URL changes via History API (back/forward navigation).
- Behavior: A state-management component reads parameters such as section, filter, and focusSlug. It then instructs sections and components to:
  - Scroll or jump to the specified section.
  - Apply or clear the package filter (e.g., filter=logo3_guidelines to enable the ≥3 logo concepts & guidelines filter).
  - If a focusSlug is present, wait for the packages to load, then highlight and optionally scroll to the matching package row in the comparison table.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL query parameters (no localStorage) to control visible sections and comparison state. Example parameters include:
- section: Controls which primary section is considered active or should be scrolled into view on load. Expected values may include intro, deliverables, process, pricing, comparison. On initial load, if section is present, the page-state router component scrolls to the corresponding section and may expand that section if it is collapsible (such as the comparison table when section=comparison). When the user scrolls, the router can update section to reflect the currently visible section.
- filter: Controls which package filter is active in the comparison table. A notable value is logo3_guidelines, which indicates that only packages meeting the condition number_of_logo_concepts >= 3 and includes_brand_guidelines == true should be shown. If filter is absent or set to a neutral value (e.g., all), the table shows all packages. Toggling the UI filter updates this parameter; changing it via the browser’s address bar or history navigation updates the table accordingly.
- focusSlug: Optional parameter specifying a BrandingPackage.slug that should be focused within the comparison table. After getBrandingPackagesComparison has resolved and the table has rendered, the comparison component searches the loaded packages for the one whose slug matches focusSlug. If found, it applies a focused/highlighted state to that row and can scroll it into view.
State transitions:
- Clicking 'Compare packages' sets or replaces section=comparison and may optionally clear filter unless a specific filter is requested from the context. The comparison table then becomes visible (if it was collapsed) and the view scrolls to it.
- Toggling the ≥3 logo concepts & guidelines filter sets filter=logo3_guidelines when enabled or removes/changes the parameter when disabled, causing the table data subset to re-render. The highlighting of qualifying packages remains visible even when all packages are shown, but is particularly important when no filter is active.
- Navigating via the browser's back/forward buttons restores previous section, filter, and focusSlug from the URL. The state-management component re-applies these settings without reloading the entire page, reusing the already-fetched data where possible.
All data displayed on this page is derived dynamically from getBrandingServiceContent and getBrandingPackagesComparison; no static content values are hardcoded into the functional logic, aside from the business rule that identifies qualifying packages by number_of_logo_concepts and includes_brand_guidelines.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getBrandingServiceContent with no parameters and distributes the returned intro_heading, intro_copy, key_deliverables, and process_overview to child content components via a shared state or props mechanism.
Displays the main introductory heading and copy of the Branding & Visual Identity service. It also registers an anchor to be targeted by the section URL parameter for deep-linking and scrolling.
Renders a structured list of key deliverables, with each list item sourced from the key_deliverables array returned by getBrandingServiceContent.

Displays the branding process as a sequence of steps, each mapped from the process_overview array returned by getBrandingServiceContent. It may label steps numerically based on their position in the array.

Fetches branding packages for the page by calling getBrandingPackagesComparison with no parameters. Provides the resulting packages array (of BrandingPackage entities) to downstream components such as the pricing summary and comparison table.

Summarizes branding package options based on the packages data loaded by branding-packages-comparison-loader, and contains the 'Compare packages' button. It may derive aggregated information (such as number of packages or min/max pricing) for display, but does not hardcode any package values. The summary section acts as the anchor for section=pricing.

Implements the 'Compare packages' button behavior. When clicked, it updates the URL query parameters to set section=comparison (or an equivalent value), signals the comparison table component to be expanded/visible if it is collapsible, and scrolls the page to the comparison table section.

Displays a table of branding packages using data from getBrandingPackagesComparison.packages. Each row corresponds to a BrandingPackage and includes fields such as name, one_time_price, monthly_price (if present), number_of_logo_concepts, includes_brand_guidelines, includes_stationery_design, includes_website_styleguide, and is_active. Rows for inactive packages can be excluded or specially marked, depending on business rules. Packages that satisfy number_of_logo_concepts >= 3 and includes_brand_guidelines == true are visually highlighted. Each row includes a 'View details' control that navigates to branding_package.html with the slug parameter.

Provides a toggle or selection control to apply the feature-based filter that only shows packages which include at least 3 logo concepts and a brand guidelines document. When the filter is activated or deactivated, it updates the filter URL parameter (e.g., filter=logo3_guidelines or filter=all) and notifies the comparison table to adjust the displayed dataset accordingly.

Encapsulates per-row actions for each package in the comparison table. Its primary action is 'View details', which reads the BrandingPackage.slug value for that row and constructs a navigation URL to branding_package.html with the slug query parameter. The component ensures that navigation uses the correct parameter name and encoding.

Manages synchronization between URL query parameters and the page’s internal state. On initialization and on URL changes (including back/forward navigation), it reads parameters such as section, filter, and focusSlug. It then instructs sections to scroll into view (e.g., scroll to pricing when section=pricing, scroll or expand comparison when section=comparison), informs the comparison table and filter control of the current filter state, and, after packages are loaded, highlights or scrolls to a specific package row whose slug matches focusSlug. When sections become visible or filters change via user interactions, this component updates the URL parameters via the History API without reloading the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow of full-width sections, each with a centered max-width content band on a pure black background. The first visible section acts as a hero-style intro for the Branding & Visual Identity service, mirroring the mockup: a two-column layout with a dominant left headline block and a supporting right column. Below that, sections for deliverables, process, and packages/pricing are stacked vertically, each treated as a clean, spacious band. Within each band, content is arranged into one or two columns based on importance, maintaining strong left alignment and clear typographic hierarchy. Interactive controls (filters, buttons, row actions) are integrated at the top-right or far-right positions of their sections to echo the CTA/menu placement from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow of full-width sections, each with a centered max-width content band on a pure black background. The first visible section acts as a hero-style intro for the Branding & Visual Identity service, mirroring the mockup: a two-column layout with a dominant left headline block and a supporting right column. Below that, sections for deliverables, process, and packages/pricing are stacked vertically, each treated as a clean, spacious band. Within each band, content is arranged into one or two columns based on importance, maintaining strong left alignment and clear typographic hierarchy. Interactive controls (filters, buttons, row actions) are integrated at the top-right or far-right positions of their sections to echo the CTA/menu placement from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The intro section occupies roughly 80–100% of the initial viewport height, with a narrow top margin for breathing room and a large internal content block centered vertically, similar to the hero in the mockup. Subsequent sections each get generous vertical padding (72–120px top and bottom) to preserve a premium, airy feel. Horizontally, all content sits within a max-width container that uses an implicit 12‑column grid: key two-column layouts allocate about 60–65% width to left content and 35–40% to right content. Tables and lists span nearly the full container width, while buttons and controls occupy minimal width but are given ample surrounding negative space. Data loaders, when visible, overlay either the full viewport or the full width of their section, dimming the background while keeping the spatial proportions intact.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is low and deliberate: few elements per section, large typography, and generous line height. Headings are oversized and occupy significant vertical space; supporting paragraphs in the right columns are moderately dense but broken into short blocks. Lists (deliverables, process steps, table content) are structured for readability with clear row spacing and column separation. The comparison table itself is information-dense relative to other sections, but it is visually balanced by wide margins, tall row heights, and strong column alignment so it still feels premium rather than cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a modified Z‑pattern influenced by the mockup. Users start at the top-left of the hero-style intro, reading the bold service title and key statement, then move to the top-right of that section where supporting text or a key reassurance lives. As they scroll, the eye continues to follow a left‑to‑right progression: section titles and primary messages appear on the left, with complementary details, controls, or badges toward the right. In the pricing/comparison area, the flow goes title (left) → compare trigger/filter controls (right) → down into the table where scanning becomes primarily vertical with light horizontal sweeps across columns. CTAs and interactive controls are consistently positioned in the top-right regions of their sections to echo the original CTA/menu placement from the mockup and to create predictable interaction anchors.

layout.overall_description: The Branding & Visual Identity page presents as a series of cinematic, full-width black bands stacked vertically, each wrapped in a centered max-width container that follows a 12‑column grid. The topmost visible band functions like a hero: on the left, a massive, two-line headline announces the service in bold, high-contrast type that occupies roughly two-thirds of the width; on the right, a narrower column contains a concise, multi-line description and key reassurance text, all spaced with generous breathing room. As you scroll, the next bands reveal the deliverables and process—still minimalist and typography-driven—with content primarily aligned to the left, and occasional right-side columns used to balance the layout and guide the eye in a gentle Z‑pattern. Further down, a spacious pricing section introduces the branding packages; its heading anchors the left side, while a bright, neon-accented compare control sits on the right. Immediately below, a wide comparison table stretches across the content width, with a subtle filter control pinned to the top-right of this area and row action icons aligned at the far right of each row. Throughout, large patches of empty black space around major headings, between sections, and around controls keep the interface feeling high-end and intentionally sparse, even where the information (like package features) is more detailed. Non-visual components like loaders and the state router sit behind this structure, occasionally overlaying it with centered loading states but otherwise preserving the same balanced spatial rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, this data loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay that covers the entire viewport, sitting above all other components. It darkens the already black background slightly and centers a small, neon-accented loader element (spinner or minimal text) horizontally and vertically within the screen. The overlay spans 100% of the width and height, aligning with the underlying grid but effectively masking it until loading completes. When inactive, it occupies no visible space and does not affect the layout of other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed, full-viewport overlay. It is positioned above the normal document flow (no allocated space) and is anchored to the viewport, not the scroll position, so it remains centered even as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is the first visible content block and mimics the hero layout from the mockup. It spans the full browser width with a pure black background, and its content is constrained within a centered max-width container (e.g., ~1200–1440px) aligned to an implicit 12‑column grid. Vertically, it takes up roughly 80–100% of the viewport height on desktop, with generous top and bottom padding (approximately 96px each) so the core content appears visually centered. Inside, the content splits into two columns: the left column occupies about 7 of 12 grid columns (roughly 60–65% width) and houses an oversized, two-line H1 for 'Branding & Visual Identity' and possibly a short subline beneath it. This text block starts near the top-left of the content container but has ample negative space on all sides, especially below the headline. The right column, occupying about 5 of 12 columns (35–40% width), aligns to the top of the left block and contains a multi-line supporting paragraph that explains the service, followed by a small detail such as a tagline, microcopy, or subtle badge aligned toward the bottom of this column. Vertical spacing within this column uses the 16–32px increments, ensuring each text block has breathing room. There are no visible card borders; everything is pure type and negative space, with neon accent colors limited to key phrases or small visual elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the normal document flow, anchored at the top of the main content area. It does not stick or float but is visually treated as the hero band for this page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly beneath the intro section, this band begins after a large vertical gap (approximately 80–96px) to maintain the airy, premium feel. It spans the full width with the same centered max-width container. Inside, the layout is predominantly left-weighted: a strong section heading ('Branding Deliverables', for example) sits at the top-left, spanning about 6–7 grid columns. Below this heading, the deliverables are arranged in a two-column list within the same horizontal zone: each column takes roughly 5–6 grid columns, with a gutter in between, so the entire list block uses most of the content width while still leaving comfortable side margins. Individual deliverable items are set as single lines or short multi-line rows with 16–24px vertical spacing between them. The rightmost portion of the container (about 1–2 columns) is left intentionally empty or used only for subtle alignment, reinforcing the negative space and ensuring the section does not feel crowded. The entire block has generous top and bottom padding (72–96px), with clear separation from both the intro above and the process section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section following the normal vertical flow, expanding vertically as needed based on the number of deliverables while preserving consistent side margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section appears immediately below the deliverables list, separated by a large vertical gap (around 80px). It mirrors the two-column emphasis of the hero to maintain visual continuity. The full-width black band houses a centered max-width container divided into a 7/5 column split. On the left (about 7 grid columns), a bold section heading like 'Our Branding Process' and a brief summary paragraph are stacked with 24–32px spacing. This left stack starts near the top of the section, aligned with the same left margin as previous headings, creating a strong vertical alignment line down the page. On the right (about 5 grid columns), the key process steps are presented as a vertical sequence—either numbered or labeled—each step occupying the full width of the right column with 24–32px vertical spacing between steps. The right column content is slightly more text-dense but uses generous line height and spacing so it still feels open. The bottom of this section leaves a comfortable margin (72–96px) before the pricing section begins, with no visible dividing lines, relying instead on negative space to separate sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal flow, but structured so that the left column anchors the main message while the right column can grow vertically if there are more process steps, without disturbing the left column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This loader is scoped to the pricing and comparison area rather than the entire page. When active, it overlays just the portion of the page containing the pricing summary and comparison table. Visually, it appears as a semi-opaque, darkened layer that stretches across the full width of the central content container and from the top of the pricing section to just below the comparison table’s expected height. A centered loading indicator (small neon-highlighted spinner or text) is vertically and horizontally centered within this bounded area. The surrounding page (intro, deliverables, process) remains visible and scrollable, but the pricing/comparison content beneath the overlay is visually subdued and non-interactive until loading completes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay relative to the pricing/comparison section. It does not take up layout space when hidden and scrolls with the page, always covering the associated section rather than the full viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing summary section sits below the process overview, separated by a generous vertical gap (approximately 96px). It forms the top part of a larger 'Packages & Comparison' band. Within the full-width black band, the max-width container starts with a horizontal row: on the left, a prominent section heading like 'Branding Packages' occupies about 6–7 grid columns, aligned with the same left margin as previous headings. On the right, roughly 5–6 grid columns are reserved for high-level pricing highlights—this might be a single line of text summarizing starting prices or two to three compact package summary blocks arranged horizontally. These summaries are not heavy cards; instead, they are minimal typographic groupings with plenty of surrounding negative space and consistent 16–24px vertical spacing between lines. The entire pricing summary row maintains at least 32–48px of space beneath it before any comparison controls or tables, ensuring a clear visual separation between the narrative summary and the detailed comparison below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal flow, acting as the upper anchor for the combined pricing/comparison section. It sets the context for the comparison table that follows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This button control is embedded within the top area of the pricing/comparison band, visually aligned with the pricing summary heading. It appears on the right side of the pricing summary row, occupying a compact width (roughly 1–2 grid columns) and aligned to the far right edge of the container. Horizontally, it sits in the same row as the 'Branding Packages' heading, creating a left–right tension similar to the logo vs. CTA in the hero mockup: heading on the left, action on the right. Vertically, there is enough padding (16–24px around the button) so it does not feel cramped against the heading. The button itself uses the bold neon accent color against the black background, making it a bright focal point. Below this row, a 32–48px gap separates the control from the top of the comparison table and filter, reinforcing it as the primary entry point into detailed comparison.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the pricing summary row, aligned to the right and remaining visible at the top of the pricing/comparison band. It does not stick on scroll, but its consistent placement in the top-right of this section mirrors the hero CTA behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison table sits immediately below the pricing summary and compare trigger, separated by a medium vertical gap (about 32–48px). It spans nearly the full width of the max-width container, using all 12 grid columns while maintaining comfortable left and right padding. The table header row is aligned just below the filter controls (which sit above and to the right), creating a clean stacked hierarchy: section heading and compare button → filter → table. Each row in the table is relatively tall (at least 48–64px height) to maintain generous vertical spacing, and columns are spaced so that text does not feel cramped. The leftmost column lists package names and is slightly wider to anchor the scan; central columns list feature availability; the far-right column is reserved for the row action group. Overall, the table reads as a single, wide, high-contrast grid of text and subtle dividers, sitting on the black background with minimal border styling. Ample margin (72–96px) at the bottom separates the table from any subsequent content or footer, letting it feel like a complete, standalone comparison surface.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow, expanding vertically based on the number of packages/features. It scrolls normally with the page; only the filter control above it may exhibit sticky behavior relative to this section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This filter control is placed just above the comparison table, aligned to the top-right of the table area within the same content container. Horizontally, it occupies about 2–3 grid columns on the right side, with the left side above the table largely empty or containing only a subtle label, preserving negative space. Vertically, it sits 16–24px above the table header row, clearly associated with the table but visually distinct. The control itself is compact (e.g., a dropdown, segmented control, or pill buttons) with comfortable horizontal padding (16–24px) and minimal but clear text. Surrounding whitespace ensures it feels like a refined control, not a cluttered form. Its right-aligned placement mirrors the hero’s top-right CTA and reinforces a visual pattern of important controls sitting on the upper right edge of key content blocks.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the comparison section: as the user scrolls down through the table rows, the filter remains pinned to the top of the viewport within the vertical bounds of the comparison band, so it is always visible while scanning the table, then scrolls away once the user leaves this section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This row action group lives within the far-right column of each row of the comparison table. In each row, the actions (such as a 'View details' icon or secondary CTA) are right-aligned and grouped horizontally, occupying a narrow vertical strip that matches the row’s height. The action icons or text buttons are spaced with 8–16px padding between them, ensuring they do not feel crowded. Because the table stretches across the full content width, this rightmost column sits near the right edge of the container, visually echoing the top-right CTAs seen elsewhere on the page. The actions are subtle in size compared to the main table content but benefit from the negative space surrounding them, making them easy to spot when scanning across from the package names on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each table row, aligned to the right edge of the table. They scroll normally as part of the table content and do not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state manager is an invisible structural component that controls which parts of the page are currently displayed—for example, whether the comparison table is visible, whether a specific package detail view is shown, or whether loading states are active. It does not render any visible UI of its own and therefore does not take up any spatial footprint or disturb the layout. Instead, it influences the presence or absence of other components (such as hiding the comparison table when a detail view is opened), while the underlying layout principles—full-width bands, centered container, and generous spacing—remain consistent in whatever state is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It lives at the page level in the component hierarchy and only affects which visual components are mounted, without altering the grid, spacing, or scroll behavior directly.

name: Branding Package Detail & Onboarding
title: Branding Package Detail & Onboarding
description: Page for viewing a specific branding package, selecting billing and add-ons, completing onboarding details, and submitting them for confirmation.

core_features: Load a specific branding package based on the 'slug' URL parameter using getBrandingPackageDetail and display package details, inclusions, and pricing.

Display and manage a billing option selector (e.g., one-time vs. installments), initializing it with the default_billing_option returned by getBrandingPackageDetail and allowing the user to explicitly select the one-time option when desired.
List all available branding add-ons returned by getBrandingPackageDetail as independent checkboxes, with the ability for the user to select any combination, including none of them.
Provide a 'Get started with this package' action that scrolls or jumps the viewport to the onboarding form section on the same page and updates the URL state to reflect the onboarding step.
Render an onboarding form that collects company_name, industry, and planned_brand_launch_date (via a date picker), and validates required fields according to the BrandingOnboarding model.
Offer a 'Continue to summary' action that validates the onboarding form and, if valid, reveals a summary/confirmation section on the same page and updates URL state to represent the summary step.
Display a summary/confirmation view combining the selected package, billing option, add-ons, and onboarding form values before submission.
Submit the combined onboarding information for the selected branding package via startBrandingOnboarding and display success or error feedback based on the response.
user_workflows:
View package details and configuration:
1. User navigates to branding_package.html with a slug query parameter (e.g., branding_package.html?slug=<package-slug>).
2. On page load, the page calls getBrandingPackageDetail with the slug parameter.
3. The page displays the package name, description, one_time_price, any available monthly_price, and key inclusions (such as number_of_logo_concepts and boolean inclusions) from the returned BrandingPackage.
4. The billing option selector initializes to default_billing_option from the API but can be changed by the user.
5. The add-on list shows all available_add_ons as checkboxes, initially all unselected.

Start onboarding from the package detail:
1. User reviews the package details and optionally adjusts billing_option and selected add-ons.
2. User clicks the 'Get started with this package' button.
3. The page scrolls to or reveals the onboarding form section.
4. The URL query string is updated (e.g., adding or updating a stage=onboarding parameter) without reloading the page, so that reloading preserves the user being in the onboarding context (though selections are not persisted via URL).

Complete the onboarding form:
1. In the onboarding form, the user enters company_name (text input).
2. The user selects industry from a fixed set of enum options defined by the BrandingOnboarding.industry field.
3. The user selects planned_brand_launch_date using a date picker that returns a date value; the client converts this to the datetime format expected by startBrandingOnboarding when eventually submitting.
4. The user clicks 'Continue to summary'.
5. The form validates required fields (company_name, industry, planned_brand_launch_date). If any are invalid or empty, errors are shown and the user remains on the onboarding form.
6. If validation passes, the page stores the onboarding field values in local page state and reveals the summary section.
7. The URL query string is updated (e.g., stage=summary) without reloading, reflecting that the user is in the summary step.

Review summary and submit onboarding:
1. In the summary section, the user sees a read-only summary including:
   - The selected BrandingPackage (name and key pricing info).
   - The chosen billing_option.
   - The currently selected add-ons (from selected_add_on_ids).
   - The onboarding details: company_name, industry, planned_brand_launch_date.
2. If needed, the user can use an 'Edit details' action to jump back to the onboarding form (updating the URL back to stage=onboarding) while preserving current form values in page state.
3. When satisfied, the user clicks a 'Confirm and submit' button.
4. The page compiles the current selections into a payload for startBrandingOnboarding, including branding_package_id from the loaded package data, billing_option, selected_add_on_ids, company_name, industry, and planned_brand_launch_date converted to a datetime.
5. startBrandingOnboarding is called.
6. On success (success=true), the page displays a confirmation state using the returned BrandingOnboarding object and message, and may optionally lock further edits.
7. On failure (success=false or network error), the page displays an error message and keeps the user in the summary step, allowing them to retry submission.

Navigate back to all branding packages:
1. At any point, the user can click a 'Back to all branding packages' link.
2. The browser navigates to branding.html without needing any API call from this page.
3. No onboarding state is persisted beyond the current page; navigation away discards the current configuration.
interactions:
Initial data loading:
- Event: Page load with a slug parameter present in the URL.
- Action: The data loader component extracts the slug from the query string and invokes getBrandingPackageDetail(slug).
- Result: On success, the page stores the received package, available_add_ons, and default_billing_option in its state and renders dependent components. On error (e.g., invalid slug, network error), an error state is displayed and functional components that depend on the package are disabled or hidden.

Billing option selection:
- Event: User clicks a billing option control (e.g., radio button or segmented control) to select either 'one_time' or 'installments'.
- Action: The billing selector component updates its internal selected value and writes this to shared page state.
- Result: Summary and onboarding logic use this selected billing_option. The 'one_time' option can always be explicitly selected by the user even if the default is something else.

Add-on selection:
- Event: User checks or unchecks an add-on checkbox.
- Action: The add-ons component updates its selected_add_on_ids array in page state by adding or removing the related BrandingAddOn.id.
- Result: The summary view and eventual startBrandingOnboarding payload use the current selected_add_on_ids. The user can uncheck all add-ons so that selected_add_on_ids is an empty array or undefined.

Get started with this package:
- Event: User clicks the 'Get started with this package' button.
- Action: The page scrolls or jumps to the onboarding form section and updates the URL query parameter (e.g., stage=onboarding) via history.pushState or similar without reloading.
- Result: The onboarding form becomes the primary focus; all previously selected billing options and add-ons remain in page state.

Onboarding form input and validation:
- Event: User enters or edits company_name, selects an industry value, and picks planned_brand_launch_date from a date picker.
- Action: The form component stores values in its local state and syncs them with shared page state on change or on blur.
- Event: User clicks 'Continue to summary'.
- Action: The form runs validation enforcing required fields. If validation fails, the page shows inline error messages and does not advance to summary.
- Result: On pass, the page reveals the summary section, updates URL to reflect stage=summary, and ensures the summary uses the latest values.

Summary review and submit:
- Event: User clicks 'Confirm and submit' in the summary section.
- Action: The summary component reads all relevant state (branding_package_id, billing_option, selected_add_on_ids, company_name, industry, planned_brand_launch_date), transforms planned_brand_launch_date to a datetime format as required, and calls startBrandingOnboarding with those parameters.
- Result: While awaiting the response, the submit button can be disabled and a loading state shown.
- On success: The confirmation state is displayed using data from the onboarding object and message; fields and selectors are disabled to prevent further edits.
- On failure: An error message is shown; the user remains in the summary section and can retry submission.

Edit from summary:
- Event: User clicks an 'Edit details' control in the summary.
- Action: The page scrolls back to the onboarding form and updates the URL query parameter (e.g., stage=onboarding) without reloading.
- Result: The form fields are prefilled with current state values, allowing modifications. On the next 'Continue to summary', the summary updates accordingly.

Back to all branding packages navigation:
- Event: User clicks the 'Back to all branding packages' link.
- Action: Browser navigates to branding.html.
- Result: Current page state and any partially completed onboarding data are discarded; no interfaces are called during this navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary routing state for this page is controlled by URL query parameters and in-memory page state, without using localStorage. The key URL parameter is 'slug', which is required to identify which BrandingPackage to load. On initial load, the page reads slug from the query string (e.g., branding_package.html?slug=<value>) and passes it to getBrandingPackageDetail. If slug is missing or empty, the page shows an appropriate error or redirect suggestion because it cannot call getBrandingPackageDetail.

In addition to slug, the page uses an optional 'stage' query parameter to represent the current user step: 'details', 'onboarding', or 'summary'. If 'stage' is absent, the page defaults to 'details', which shows the package details and configuration controls with the onboarding form collapsed or positioned below. When the user clicks 'Get started with this package', the page updates the URL to include stage=onboarding using history.pushState (or equivalent) without a full reload. When the user successfully validates the onboarding form and clicks 'Continue to summary', the page updates the URL to stage=summary. If the user chooses to edit details from the summary, the URL is updated back to stage=onboarding. Reloading the page preserves the visible section based on the stage parameter, provided the initial package data can still be loaded using the slug.

The package, add-ons, and default billing option are held in page state after being returned by getBrandingPackageDetail. The user's chosen billing_option and selected_add_on_ids are stored in this in-memory state and are not encoded into the URL, to keep the query string concise. The onboarding form values (company_name, industry, planned_brand_launch_date) are also kept in memory and synchronized between the onboarding form and summary view. The summary section reads all these state values to display the confirmation view and construct the payload for startBrandingOnboarding. When startBrandingOnboarding returns successfully, the page stores the returned BrandingOnboarding object and uses it to render a confirmation state; at that point, the page may optionally set a stage value such as 'completed' in internal state (and potentially in the URL if desired), but this is not required by the interfaces.

Error states are represented in internal state only (e.g., flags and messages for loading errors, validation errors, and submission errors) and are not persisted in the URL. The URL serves strictly to identify the selected package (slug) and the user's current step (stage).

components.functionality: Reads the 'slug' URL parameter, calls getBrandingPackageDetail with that slug, and stores the returned package, available_add_ons, and default_billing_option in page state for use by other components.

Displays the loaded branding package's primary details such as name, description, and key pricing fields, and indicates that this page is for configuring and starting onboarding for the selected package.

Shows available billing options (one_time and, if supported by the business rules, installments) initialized to default_billing_option returned by getBrandingPackageDetail, and ensures the user can explicitly choose and maintain the one_time option if desired.

Lists all available add-ons associated with the loaded branding package using available_add_ons, with each add-on represented as an independent checkbox. Manages an array of selected_add_on_ids in page state, allowing all checkboxes to be unselected.

Provides the 'Get started with this package' call-to-action. When clicked, it scrolls or jumps the viewport to the onboarding form component and updates the URL query string to reflect stage=onboarding without reloading the page.

Collects onboarding data for the selected branding package, including company_name (text input), industry (select input based on BrandingOnboarding.industry enum values), and planned_brand_launch_date (date picker). Performs client-side validation of required fields and, on a successful 'Continue to summary' action, stores validated values in shared state and triggers display of the summary section.

Displays a read-only summary of the selected branding package, the chosen billing_option, the selected_add_on_ids (resolved to the corresponding BrandingAddOn entries), and the onboarding form values. Provides controls to edit the onboarding details (scrolling back to the form and updating URL stage) or confirm and submit the onboarding data via startBrandingOnboarding.

Handles the actual submission of onboarding details by constructing and sending the startBrandingOnboarding request using current page state: branding_package_id, billing_option, selected_add_on_ids, company_name, industry, and planned_brand_launch_date. Processes the response, updating page state with the returned BrandingOnboarding object, success flag, and message, and triggers appropriate success or error UI in the summary/confirmation area.

Displays the post-submission confirmation or error state based on the result of startBrandingOnboarding, using the onboarding object and message for successful submissions, and providing clear feedback and retry options for failed submissions.
Provides a navigational link back to branding.html (the branding packages overview), allowing the user to leave this detailed package onboarding flow.

Synchronizes the visible step of the page (details, onboarding, summary) with the 'stage' URL query parameter. On URL changes, it shows or hides the relevant sections. On internal transitions (e.g., 'Get started with this package', 'Continue to summary', 'Edit details'), it updates the URL query parameter using the History API without reloading.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with a strong left-right split; the hero likely uses something like a 7/12 column span for the left headline block and a 5/12 span for the right content block within a centered max-width container.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear top-to-bottom page structure with a dominant full-width top band that behaves like the hero from the mockup. Inside a centered max-width container, maintain a two-column grid: a wider left column (≈7/12) for the primary page narrative and interactive flow (package header, selectors, onboarding form), and a narrower right column (≈5/12) for supporting context and reassurance (summary, pricing, status). The global site nav remains above this page content as a separate, persistent bar. Below the initial hero band, keep the same two-column structure so the left column continues the main flow while the right column keeps the summary visible.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear top-to-bottom page structure with a dominant full-width top band that behaves like the hero from the mockup. Inside a centered max-width container, maintain a two-column grid: a wider left column (≈7/12) for the primary page narrative and interactive flow (package header, selectors, onboarding form), and a narrower right column (≈5/12) for supporting context and reassurance (summary, pricing, status). The global site nav remains above this page content as a separate, persistent bar. Below the initial hero band, keep the same two-column structure so the left column continues the main flow while the right column keeps the summary visible.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses a full-width pure black background with a centered content band (≈1120–1280px max-width). The hero/detail header band occupies roughly the top 45–60% of the first viewport height, with generous top padding (≈64–96px from the nav) and bottom padding (≈64px) to create a cinematic intro. Horizontally, the left column gets about 60–65% of the container width, the right column about 35–40%. Below the hero, sections in the left column stack vertically with large separations (≈48–80px) to preserve the premium, spacious feel. The right column’s summary area runs almost the full height of the content, with its own internal spacing, and may scroll within the main page. Major groups (header, selectors, form, confirmation) are separated by 72–120px of vertical space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, editorial feel despite the functional content. Large typography for the package title and section headings, with ample breathing room around each block. Text lines are kept to comfortable lengths in both columns. Form elements and selectors are grouped into clear, simple blocks with generous padding (16–24px inside components, 24–32px between them) and no clutter. The right column summary is concise and structured, with enough negative space above and below each item to avoid an information-dense look. The overall impression is controlled and minimal rather than crowded, with the dark background and neon accents amplifying focus.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors a modified Z-pattern. The user begins at the top-left with the subtle 'Back to packages' link, then moves slightly down to the large, bold package name in the detail header on the left. From there, the eye is drawn to the right column summary for supporting context (price, key inclusions), echoing the mockup’s descriptive right column. Once orientated, the main interaction path flows downward along the left column: first billing option selection, then add-ons, then the onboarding form, and finally the submission control. Throughout, the right column acts as a persistent, reassuring frame of reference, updating or reflecting choices. On submission, focus shifts to a centered confirmation state in the left column, with the right column still present but visually secondary, reinforcing completion.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure black canvas with a centered, max-width content band divided into two columns. After the global navigation, the content begins with a spacious hero-like band: on the left, a large, two-line package name and short descriptor set in oversized, bold type, glowing subtly with magenta–cyan accents; on the right, a tightly composed package summary with pricing, key highlights, and a reassuring summary panel. Generous vertical padding around this band creates a cinematic first impression with plenty of black negative space. As the user scrolls, the two-column structure persists. The left column becomes a vertically stacked flow of interactions—billing selection, add-ons, onboarding details, and submission—each separated by broad, intentional gaps that keep the layout airy. The right column remains a narrower, high-contrast summary panel that visually anchors the experience, surfacing live updates based on the user’s selections. Neon-highlighted buttons punctuate the dark background at key decision points. Overall, the layout feels like a premium, modern onboarding console: minimal chrome, dramatic typography, and a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom narrative that softly guides the user from understanding the package to completing their onboarding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a full-page overlay above the entire content area (covering both left and right columns and the hero band), slightly dimming the black background with a subtle translucent layer. At the center of the viewport, a small, minimal loader indicator and short status text are vertically and horizontally centered, surrounded by generous negative space so nothing competes for attention. When inactive, this component is invisible and occupies no layout space; when active, it sits fixed across the viewport, independent of scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay centered in the viewport, covering the full width and height of the page content while loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The detail header forms the top hero band of the page’s content area. It sits directly below the global navigation and spans the full width of the main content container. Within the 12-column grid, the header primarily occupies the left 7/12 of the container, starting about 64–96px below the nav, with a sharp left alignment to reinforce the strong, editorial feel. The package title is set as a large, two-line headline, taking up most of the vertical space in this left portion, with a short subtitle or key descriptor directly beneath it. The total height of this header block fills roughly the upper half of the first viewport, leaving substantial black space above and below the text to match the dramatic hero style from the mockup. The right side of the grid is visually reserved to align with the summary section (in effect, the header’s right side feels open and balanced by the summary rather than densely packed with its own content).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the main content, defining the hero band and anchoring the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing option selector is the first interactive section directly below the detail header in the left column. It is aligned flush with the left edge of the content container, occupying the same approximate 7/12 width as the header’s text block. A section heading sits at the top of this block, followed by the billing options laid out as large, tappable cards or pill-like segments within the block’s width. Internally, the selector has generous padding (≈16–24px) and 16–24px spacing between individual options. Vertically, there is a pronounced gap (≈48–64px) between the bottom of the header and the top of the billing selector, maintaining the low-density feel. Horizontally, there is clear separation from the right summary column, with at least 48px of gutter between the two columns. The selector sits above the add-ons and the onboarding form, establishing the top of the interactive flow in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the left column, stacked immediately after the hero header content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-ons selector is positioned directly below the billing option selector in the left column, maintaining the same left alignment and width (≈7/12 of the container). It is separated from the billing section by a comfortable vertical gap (≈32–48px) so each section reads as a distinct block. The add-ons themselves appear as a vertical list or grid of cards within this block, each with a clear label, brief description, and selection control. Internal spacing between add-on items is generous (≈16–24px), with ample padding around the entire selector to keep a light visual footprint. The block extends downward but remains clearly contained within the left column, leaving the right column summary visible and aligned to its right across the vertical span.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the left column, stacked after the billing selector, scrolling naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The get started button is placed at the end of the initial selection phase in the left column, visually bridging the selection sections (billing and add-ons) and the more detailed onboarding form. It sits directly below the add-ons selector, offset by a modest vertical gap (≈24–32px). The button is left-aligned with the column, sized as a prominent primary CTA (≈200–280px wide or sized to its label) with magenta or cyan glow against the black background, and enough vertical padding (≈12–16px) to feel substantial. There is ample empty space to its right, aligning with the right column gutter, so it doesn’t feel cramped. Below this button, another vertical gap (≈32–48px) separates it from the onboarding form, reinforcing its role as a step-change action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static CTA within the left column; sits inline in the vertical flow between selection components and the onboarding form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The onboarding form occupies a sizeable block beneath the get started button in the left column, continuing the same 7/12 width alignment. It begins after a generous vertical gap (≈32–48px) to signal a new stage in the flow. The form fields are organized into a small number of logical groups (e.g., business details, brand preferences), each group separated by 24–32px of vertical spacing to maintain a low-density, calm feel. Labels and inputs are primarily arranged in a single-column stack to keep the visual line length tight and readable, with no more than two fields placed side-by-side on desktop if necessary. The top of the form is still within the height of the right column summary, so when the user scrolls, the form content passes alongside the still-visible summary. The overall form block may extend well past the first viewport, but it retains consistent left alignment and generous spacing all the way down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable block in the left column, consuming the main vertical space as the user advances through onboarding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary section anchors the right column, starting in horizontal alignment with the top of the detail header’s headline. It occupies approximately the right 5/12 of the content container, with a clear gutter separating it from the left column. Vertically, the summary is structured as a tall, contained panel with its own internal hierarchy: a concise title at the top (e.g., ‘Your branding package’), followed by key details like selected billing plan, total price, chosen add-ons, and perhaps trust or guarantee notes. Each group of information is separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing. The panel has comfortable internal padding (≈24–32px) and plenty of black negative space around it, allowing it to feel like a premium, focused reference area. As the user scrolls the main content in the left column, the summary remains in the same column on the right; on larger screens it may stay near the top of the viewport (short sticky behavior) so the user can always see their configuration while completing the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-column block aligned with the hero band; can be configured as sticky within its column on desktop so it remains visible while the left column scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The onboarding submitter sits at the very bottom of the onboarding form area in the left column, acting as the final action in the flow. It is left-aligned with the form fields and sized as a bold primary CTA, visually similar in weight to (but distinct from) the earlier get started button. There is a clear vertical buffer (≈24–32px) between the last form field and this submitter to prevent crowding. Additional supporting text (such as a short reassurance line or indication of next steps) may appear directly beneath the button with smaller, subdued typography. When the user reaches this section, the right column summary is still visible to the right, reinforcing what will be submitted. Ample black negative space surrounds this control at the bottom of the left column, making it feel deliberate and important.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control at the end of the left-column form flow; appears once all relevant form fields are shown and scrolls into view naturally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful submission, the onboarding confirmation appears in place of the active onboarding form area within the left column. It is centered vertically within the portion of the viewport currently showing the left column, and horizontally aligned with the left column edges, often rendered as a contained confirmation panel or message block. The confirmation uses a concise headline (e.g., ‘You’re all set’) with a short supporting paragraph beneath, and potentially a subtle icon or checkmark, all surrounded by generous internal padding (≈32px) and sizeable margins (≈48–64px) above and below, reintroducing a hero-like spaciousness. The right column summary remains visible but feels secondary, simply echoing the final configuration; its visual intensity may be slightly reduced (e.g., lighter text or subtle dimming) to keep the user’s focus on the success message in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the onboarding form block in the left column after submission; static within the scroll context, appearing as the main focal state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-packages link is a subtle, text-only control placed at the very top of the page’s content band, just above or aligned with the top edge of the detail header in the left column. It is left-aligned with the main content, sitting a short distance (≈16–24px) below the global navigation bar. The link is small in size compared to the main headline and uses a subdued color compared to primary CTAs, but it is still comfortably spaced with clear breathing room above and below so it is easy to spot. It does not extend beyond the left column width and is visually treated as a quiet escape hatch rather than a competing call-to-action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text link at the top-left of the content area, scrolling away with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stage router conceptually wraps the main interactive portion of the page—billing selection, add-ons, onboarding form, submitter, and confirmation—within the left column, determining which of these components is visible at any given time. Visually, it does not introduce any additional chrome or layout elements of its own. Its space coincides with the left column below the detail header, extending vertically as needed. The router ensures that the flow feels like a single continuous column, rather than disjointed screens, by transitioning states within the same spatial footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible logical container occupying the left column under the header, controlling which left-column components are rendered while preserving the surrounding layout.
